topics.title: Recurrence Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recurrence Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Recurrence Equation** (or **Recurrence Relation**) is a mathematical equation or inequality that describes a function $T(n)$ in terms of its value on smaller (or sometimes larger) inputs. In the context of algorithm analysis, recurrences provide a systematic, natural way to model the **running time** of recursive algorithms, where the time taken by an algorithm depends on the time taken by its recursive sub-calls.

For a recursive algorithm, the general form of a recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)
$$

where the parameters represent the **algorithmic structure**:

- **$a$**: The **branching factor** — the number of recursive sub-problems the algorithm generates at each level of recursion.
- **$b$**: The **sub-problem size reduction factor** — by what fraction the input size shrinks at each recursive call.
- **$f(n)$**: The **cost of work done outside the recursive calls** — this includes the cost of dividing the problem and combining the results (the `divide` and `conquer` phases in divide-and-conquer algorithms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition:**
> "A recurrence is an equation that defines a sequence of values (such as the running time of a recursive algorithm) recursively, where each term of the sequence is expressed as a function of one or more previous terms. Base cases (also called **boundary conditions** or **stopping conditions**) anchor the recurrence and prevent infinite recursion."

## 1.2 The Three Essential Components of a Recurrence

Every recurrence in algorithm analysis must have these three components:

| Component | Mathematical Form | Algorithmic Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **General (Recursive) Case** | $T(n) = \dots$ | The cost when input size is large enough for recursion to proceed. |
| **Base Case(s)** | $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ or $T(0) = c$ | The cost of the smallest, non-recursive problem instance. |
| **Asymptotic Condition** | Asymptotic notation ($\Theta$, $O$, $\Omega$) | Captures the growth rate we wish to determine. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Terminology:** KTU examiners and standard textbooks (Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein) interchangeably use the terms *base case*, *ground condition*, *stopping condition*, and *boundary condition*. All refer to the non-recursive termination case.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Russian Nesting Doll (Matryoshka)

Imagine a set of **Russian Nesting Dolls**. The largest doll contains a smaller doll, which contains an even smaller doll, and so on, until you reach a tiny solid doll that cannot be opened.

- The **outer dolls** represent the **recursive calls** — each opens (calls) a smaller instance of the same problem.
- The **work done while opening a doll and closing it** represents the **non-recursive cost $f(n)$** — the `divide` and `conquer` work.
- The **smallest solid doll** is the **base case** — the recursion stops here, returning a constant-time answer.
- The **total time** to open all dolls and close them all back up is exactly $T(n)$.

The recurrence $T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$ simply says:
> "To process an input of size $n$, I make $a$ recursive calls on inputs of size $n/b$, and I spend $f(n)$ extra time on the non-recursive parts."

## 1.4 Why Recurrences Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Topic Warning:**
> Recurrence equations appear in **nearly every KTU examination cycle** under Module 1 (Introduction to Algorithm Analysis). Marks are frequently distributed as:
> - 3 marks for writing a recurrence for a given algorithm.
> - 4–7 marks for solving recurrences using the **Substitution Method**, **Iteration Method**, or **Recursion Tree Method**.
> - 7 marks for the **Master Theorem**, including identifying the case ($a$, $b$, $f(n)$) and arriving at the asymptotic bound.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Standard Methods to Solve Recurrences (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus explicitly lists the following techniques under Module 1:

### 2.1.1 Method 1 — Substitution Method (Guess & Verify)

This is the **most general** method, applicable to almost any recurrence.

**Logic Steps:**
1. **Guess** the form of the solution (typically a polynomial or polylogarithmic expression, e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$).
2. Use **mathematical induction** to verify the guess.
3. Find the constant values that make the induction hold.

### 2.1.2 Method 2 — Iteration Method (Repeated Substitution / Unrolling)

The most intuitive and mechanical method.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Repeatedly substitute the recurrence definition into itself to "unroll" the recursion.
2. Identify a **pattern** in the resulting sum.
3. Sum the geometric or arithmetic series using standard formulas.
4. Add the cost at each level to get the closed form.

### 2.1.3 Method 3 — Recursion Tree Method

A visual and structural approach — particularly useful when the recurrence is a sum of terms.

**Logic Steps:**
1. Draw the recursion tree: each node represents the cost of a single sub-problem.
2. **Root** = cost of the initial problem ($f(n)$ plus recursive calls).
3. **Children** = sub-problems created at the next level.
4. Sum the work level by level (horizontal summation).
5. Sum across all levels (vertical summation).
6. Account for the **leaf level** (base case cost).

### 2.1.4 Method 4 — Master Theorem (Cook's Theorem)

A **cookbook recipe** for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive.

**The Three Cases:**

Define the **critical exponent** as $n^{\log_b a}$.

| Case | Condition | Conclusion | Intuitive Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | Recursion dominates: leaves are the heaviest. |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$ | Recursion and divide-and-conquer work are balanced. |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, AND regularity condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Divide-and-conquer work dominates: root is the heaviest. |

### 2.1.5 Change of Variables (Algebraic Transformation)

Used when the recurrence has unusual structure (e.g., $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$).

**Logic Steps:**
1. Let $n = 2^m$ (or appropriate substitution).
2. Rewrite the recurrence in terms of $m$, defining $S(m) = T(2^m)$.
3. Solve the transformed recurrence using any standard method.
4. Substitute back to recover $T(n)$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **most critical formulas** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal examinations. Memorize the patterns, not just the values.

### Standard Recurrence Solutions Table

| Recurrence | Algorithm Example | Solution | Method to Derive |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | Linear search | $\Theta(n)$ | Iteration |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | Nested loop variant | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Iteration (sum of $i$) |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | Tree traversal | $\Theta(n)$ | Master Theorem (Case 1) |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | Merge Sort | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Master Theorem (Case 2) |
| $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ | Naive divide-and-conquer | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Master Theorem (Case 3) |
| $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | Binary Search | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Master Theorem (Case 1) |
| $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ | Matrix multiplication variant | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ | Master Theorem (Case 2) |
| $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ | Median finding (non-uniform split) | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Recursion Tree |

### Geometric Series Quick Reference

| Series | Sum Formula | Useful For |
|---|---|---|
| $\sum_{i=0}^{k} x^i$ | $\dfrac{1-x^{k+1}}{1-x}$ if $x \neq 1$ | Geometric growth/decay |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{k} 1$ | $k + 1$ | Counting levels |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i$ | $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | Arithmetic sums |
| $\sum_{i=1}^{\log_b n} n$ | $n \log_b n$ | Balanced recursion tree levels |

### Master Theorem: Critical Exponent Quick Computation

For $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, the critical exponent is computed as:

$$
\text{critical exponent} = \log_b a = \frac{\ln a}{\ln b}
$$

So $n^{\log_b a}$ is the threshold against which $f(n)$ is compared.

> [!NOTE]
> **Regularity Condition (Case 3 prerequisite):** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some constant $c < 1$ and sufficiently large $n$. This is satisfied by most polynomials of degree $> \log_b a$.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recurrence analysis is foundational in:
- **Compiler Design**: Analyzing the cost of recursive parsers.
- **Operating Systems**: Modeling fork-join and divide-and-conquer scheduling.
- **Database Systems**: Cost models for query execution plans.
- **Parallel Computing**: Estimating parallel speedup via work-span (Brent's theorem) analysis, where recurrences model the work and span of parallel algorithms.
- **Machine Learning**: Analyzing divide-and-conquer variants of algorithms in training pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example A — Iteration Method

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n^2$ with base case $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ using the Iteration Method.

### Step-by-Step Unrolling

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n^2 \\[4pt]
&= 3 \left[ 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + \left(\frac{n}{4}\right)^2 \right] + n^2 \\[4pt]
&= 9T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + 3 \cdot \frac{n^2}{16} + n^2 \\[4pt]
&= 27T\!\left(\frac{n}{64}\right) + 9 \cdot \frac{n^2}{256} + 3 \cdot \frac{n^2}{16} + n^2 \\[4pt]
&= \dots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Identifying the Pattern:** After $i$ iterations, the recurrence becomes:

$$
T(n) = 3^i \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^i}\right) + n^2 \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^j
$$

**Logical Justification of Pattern:** Each iteration multiplies the recursive coefficient by $3$ and squares the denominator $4$ in the cost term, raising $\frac{3}{16}$ to the next power. This is verified by induction on $i$.

### Determining the Termination Depth

The recursion stops when $\frac{n}{4^i} = 1$, i.e., when $i = \log_4 n$.

### Substituting the Base Case

$$
T(n) = 3^{\log_4 n} \cdot T(1) + n^2 \sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^j
$$

### Simplifying the Leaf Term

Using the identity $3^{\log_4 n} = n^{\log_4 3}$:

$$
3^{\log_4 n} = 4^{\log_4 3 \cdot \log_4 n} = n^{\log_4 3}
$$

Since $\log_4 3 \approx 0.792$, the leaf term is $\Theta(n^{0.792})$, which is **polynomially smaller** than $n^2$.

### Evaluating the Geometric Sum

Since $\frac{3}{16} < 1$, the infinite geometric series converges:

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{\log_4 n - 1} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^j = \frac{1 - (3/16)^{\log_4 n}}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13} \left( 1 - n^{\log_4 (3/16)} \right)
$$

Since $\log_4 (3/16) < 0$, the term $n^{\log_4 (3/16)} \to 0$ as $n \to \infty$, so the sum is bounded above by a constant $\frac{16}{13}$.

### Final Closed Form

$$
T(n) \leq n^2 \cdot \frac{16}{13} + n^{0.792} \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The dominant term is $n^2$, confirming that $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. This is consistent with **Master Theorem Case 3**, since $f(n) = n^2 = \Omega(n^{\log_4 3 + \epsilon})$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example B — Master Theorem Application

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

### Step 1: Identify $a$, $b$, and $f(n)$

$$
a = 4, \quad b = 2, \quad f(n) = n^2
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Critical Exponent

$$
\log_b a = \log_2 4 = 2
$$

So the critical threshold is $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.

### Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$

$$
f(n) = n^2 \quad \text{and} \quad n^{\log_b a} = n^2
$$

These are **polynomially equal**.

### Step 4: Identify the Case

$$
f(n) = n^2 = \Theta(n^2 \cdot \log^0 n)
$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

### Step 5: Apply Case 2 Conclusion

$$
T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\right) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)
$$

### Verification via Recursion Tree

| Level $i$ | Number of Sub-problems | Size per Sub-problem | Cost per Sub-problem | Total Cost at Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1$ | $n$ | $n^2$ | $n^2$ |
| $1$ | $4$ | $n/2$ | $(n/2)^2$ | $4 \cdot n^2/4 = n^2$ |
| $2$ | $16$ | $n/4$ | $(n/4)^2$ | $16 \cdot n^2/16 = n^2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $i$ | $4^i$ | $n/2^i$ | $(n/2^i)^2$ | $4^i \cdot n^2 / 4^i = n^2$ |
| Last | $n^2$ | $1$ | $1$ | $n^2$ |

**Number of levels:** $\log_2 n + 1$.

**Total cost:** $(\log_2 n + 1) \cdot n^2 = \Theta(n^2 \log n)$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Example C — Substitution Method

**Problem:** Prove by induction that $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ has solution $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

### Step 1: Make the Guess

We guess:

$$
T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_b n \quad \text{(for a constant } c > 0 \text{ and base } b > 1 \text{)}
$$

We will choose $b$ later to facilitate the inductive step.

### Step 2: Substitute the Guess into the Recurrence

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &\leq c \cdot \frac{n}{3} \log_b \frac{n}{3} + c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} \log_b \frac{2n}{3} + n \\[4pt]
&= c \cdot \frac{n}{3} (\log_b n - \log_b 3) + c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} (\log_b n - \log_b (3/2)) + n \\[4pt]
&= c \cdot n \log_b n - c \cdot \frac{n}{3} \log_b 3 - c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} (\log_b 3 - 1) + n \\[4pt]
&= c \cdot n \log_b n - c \cdot n \log_b 3 + c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} + n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3: Compare to the Inductive Hypothesis

We want this to be $\leq c \cdot n \log_b n$. So we need:

$$
- c \cdot n \log_b 3 + c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} + n \leq 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
c \cdot \frac{2n}{3} + n \leq c \cdot n \log_b 3
$$

$$
c \cdot \frac{2}{3} + 1 \leq c \cdot \log_b 3
$$

### Step 4: Choose Constants to Satisfy the Inequality

Choose $b = 3$. Then $\log_b 3 = 1$, and the inequality becomes:

$$
\frac{2c}{3} + 1 \leq c
$$

$$
1 \leq \frac{c}{3} \implies c \geq 3
$$

Choosing $c = 3$ satisfies the inequality.

### Step 5: Handle the Base Case

For small $n$, verify directly that $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_b n$ holds. This is typically satisfied for sufficiently large $n$ and verified for small values separately.

### Conclusion

By the principle of mathematical induction:

$$
T(n) = O(n \log n)
$$

with the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** This recurrence is a classic example where the Master Theorem **cannot be directly applied** because the two sub-problems have different sizes ($n/3$ and $2n/3$). The recursion tree method or substitution method must be used. This is a frequent KTU question pattern.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example D — Change of Variables

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ with $T(2) = 1$.

### Step 1: Substitute $n = 2^m$, define $S(m) = T(2^m)$

Then $\sqrt{n} = 2^{m/2}$, so the recurrence becomes:

$$
S(m) = T(2^m) = T(2^{m/2}) + 1 = S(m/2) + 1
$$

with base case $S(1) = T(2) = 1$.

### Step 2: Solve the Transformed Recurrence

This is a standard linear recurrence solved by iteration:

$$
S(m) = S(m/2) + 1 = S(m/4) + 2 = \dots = S(1) + \log_2 m = 1 + \log_2 m
$$

So $S(m) = \Theta(\log m)$.

### Step 3: Substitute Back

Since $m = \log_2 n$:

$$
T(n) = S(\log_2 n) = \Theta(\log(\log_2 n)) = \Theta(\log \log n)
$$

### Final Answer

$$
T(n) = \Theta(\log \log n)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Application:** This recurrence arises naturally when an algorithm repeatedly halves the *number of bits* needed to represent a value, e.g., the cost of repeatedly taking square roots.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Theorem Decision Flowchart

The following Mermaid flowchart encapsulates the Master Theorem decision logic. This is the single most important visual aid for KTU exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Given T(n) equals a T(n/b) plus f(n)"] --> B{"Is a greater than or equal to 1\nand b greater than 1?"}
    B -- No --> Bno["Master Theorem does NOT apply.\nUse substitution or recursion tree."]
    B -- Yes --> C["Compute critical exponent:\nn to the power of log base b of a"]
    C --> D{"Is f(n) polynomially SMALLER\nthan n to log base b of a?"}
    D -- Yes, Case 1 --> E1["CONCLUSION:\nT(n) equals Theta of n to log base b of a"]
    D -- No --> F{"Is f(n) polynomially EQUAL\nto n to log base b of a?"}
    F -- Yes, Case 2 --> F2{"Does f(n) contain\nlog to the k power of n factor?"}
    F2 -- "Yes, k greater or equal 0" --> E2["CONCLUSION:\nT(n) equals Theta of n to log base b of a\ntimes log to the k+1 power of n"]
    F2 -- No --> E2
    F -- No --> G{"Is f(n) polynomially LARGER\nthan n to log base b of a?"}
    G -- Yes, Case 3 --> H{"Regularity Condition:\na times f(n/b) less than c times f(n)\nfor some constant c less than 1?"}
    H -- Yes --> E3["CONCLUSION:\nT(n) equals Theta of f(n)"]
    H -- No --> Hno["Master Theorem does NOT apply.\nUse substitution or recursion tree."]
    E1 --> End["End: Asymptotic Bound Obtained"]
    E2 --> End
    E3 --> End
    Bno --> End
    Hno --> End
```

## 4.2 Recursion Tree Architecture — Generic Divide-and-Conquer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["LEVEL 0: Root\nSize: n\nCost per node: f(n)\nTotal: f(n)"] --> A1["Sub-problem 1\nSize: n/b\nCost: f(n/b)"]
    R --> A2["Sub-problem 2\nSize: n/b\nCost: f(n/b)"]
    R --> A3["Sub-problem a-1\nSize: n/b\nCost: f(n/b)"]
    R --> A4["Sub-problem a\nSize: n/b\nCost: f(n/b)"]
    A1 --> B1["Grandchild 1\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A1 --> B2["Grandchild 2\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A1 --> B3["Grandchild 3\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A1 --> B4["Grandchild a\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A2 --> C1["Level 2 Child 1\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A2 --> C2["Level 2 Child 2\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A2 --> C3["Level 2 Child 3\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A2 --> C4["Level 2 Child a\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A3 --> D1["Level 2 Child 1\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A3 --> D2["Level 2 Child 2\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A3 --> D3["Level 2 Child 3\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A3 --> D4["Level 2 Child a\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A4 --> E1["Level 2 Child 1\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A4 --> E2["Level 2 Child 2\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A4 --> E3["Level 2 Child 3\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    A4 --> E4["Level 2 Child a\nSize: n/b squared\nCost: f(n/b squared)"]
    B1 --> LEAF["BOTTOM: Leaves\nSize: 1\nCount: n to log base b of a\nEach costs Theta(1)"]
    B2 --> LEAF
    B3 --> LEAF
    B4 --> LEAF
    C1 --> LEAF
    C2 --> LEAF
    C3 --> LEAF
    C4 --> LEAF
    D1 --> LEAF
    D2 --> LEAF
    D3 --> LEAF
    D4 --> LEAF
    E1 --> LEAF
    E2 --> LEAF
    E3 --> LEAF
    E4 --> LEAF
```

## 4.3 Methodology Block Diagram — How to Solve Any Recurrence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Identify the Recurrence"] --> Q{"Is the recurrence in\nstandard form T(n) equals a T(n/b) plus f(n)?"}
    Q -- Yes --> R["Apply MASTER THEOREM\n3 Cases: f(n) vs n to log base b of a"]
    Q -- No --> S["Use RECURSION TREE\nDraw levels, sum work, find pattern"]
    S --> T["OR: Use ITERATION METHOD\nRepeated substitution and series summation"]
    T --> U["OR: Use SUBSTITUTION METHOD\nGuess solution and prove by induction"]
    R --> V["OR: Use CHANGE OF VARIABLES\nSubstitute n equals 2 to the m"]
    V --> W["Validate using a SECOND method\nfor cross-verification"]
    U --> W
    W --> X["FINAL ANSWER:\nAsymptotic Bound T(n)"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a recurrence relation. Write the recurrence relation for the worst-case time complexity of the binary search algorithm.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **recurrence relation** is an equation that expresses each term of a sequence as a function of one or more of the preceding terms. In algorithm analysis, it represents the running time of a recursive algorithm in terms of the running time on smaller inputs, with base cases anchoring the recursion.

For **binary search**, on each step we compare the middle element with the target and recurse on **one half** of the array of size $n/2$. The recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(1), \quad T(1) = \Theta(1)
$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of recurrence (1 mark)
- Recurrence structure explained (1 mark)
- Final binary search recurrence with base case (1 mark)

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the Master Theorem? Mention any one condition where the Master Theorem cannot be applied.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Master Theorem** is a direct method to determine the asymptotic solution of recurrence equations of the form:

$$
T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
$$

where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is an asymptotically positive function. It compares $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$ and provides three cases yielding $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, $\Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$, or $\Theta(f(n))$.

**Master Theorem cannot be applied when:**
- The recurrence has multiple, **unequal sub-problem sizes** (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$).
- The function $f(n)$ is **not polynomially smaller or larger** than $n^{\log_b a}$ (e.g., $f(n) = n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log n \cdot (2 + \sin n)$, which oscillates).
- The recurrence does not match the standard form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Master Theorem statement with form (2 marks)
- Condition of inapplicability with example (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Total: 14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Solve the recurrence using the **Iteration Method** (7 Marks)

$T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$.

#### Part (b) — Solve the recurrence using the **Master Theorem** (7 Marks)

$T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n^2 \log n$ with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) Model Solution

**Step 1: Initial expansion** — Substitute $T(n/2)$ recursively:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n
$$

**Step 2: First substitution** — Replace $T(n/2)$ with $2T(n/4) + n/2$:

$$
T(n) = 2 \left[ 2T(n/4) + \frac{n}{2} \right] + n = 4T(n/4) + n + n
$$

**Step 3: Second substitution** — Replace $T(n/4)$ with $2T(n/8) + n/4$:

$$
T(n) = 4 \left[ 2T(n/8) + \frac{n}{4} \right] + 2n = 8T(n/8) + n + 2n
$$

**Step 4: Pattern recognition** — After $i$ iterations:

$$
T(n) = 2^i \cdot T(n/2^i) + i \cdot n
$$

**Step 5: Termination** — The recursion bottoms out when $n/2^i = 1$, i.e., $i = \log_2 n$.

**Step 6: Substitution of base case** $T(1) = 1$:

$$
T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \log_2 n = n + n \log_2 n
$$

**Step 7: Final asymptotic simplification**:

$$
T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct unrolling for at least 2 iterations (2 marks)
- Correct general pattern identification (2 marks)
- Correct termination and base case substitution (2 marks)
- Final asymptotic answer (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution

**Step 1: Identify the parameters:**

$$
a = 9, \quad b = 3, \quad f(n) = n^2 \log n
$$

**Step 2: Compute the critical exponent:**

$$
\log_b a = \log_3 9 = 2
$$

So the critical threshold is $n^{\log_3 9} = n^2$.

**Step 3: Compare $f(n)$ with $n^2$:**

$$
f(n) = n^2 \log n = \Theta(n^2 \cdot \log^1 n)
$$

So $f(n)$ is polynomially equal to $n^{\log_b a}$ multiplied by $\log^1 n$.

**Step 4: Identify the Master Theorem case:**

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 1$.

**Step 5: Apply the conclusion:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^{2} n)
$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct identification of $a$, $b$, $f(n)$ (2 marks)
- Correct computation of critical exponent $\log_b a$ (1 mark)
- Correct comparison of $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$ (2 marks)
- Correct case identification and final conclusion (2 marks)

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Total: 14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Solve using the **Recursion Tree Method** (7 Marks)

$T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$ with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

#### Part (b) — Solve using the **Substitution Method** (7 Marks)

Show that $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ satisfies $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Recursion Tree

**Step 1: Identify parameters:**
$a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$.

**Step 2: Determine tree depth:**
The recursion bottoms out when $n/2^i = 1$, so $i = \log_2 n$. Number of levels = $\log_2 n + 1$.

**Step 3: Build the recursion tree table:**

| Level $i$ | # of Sub-problems | Size per Sub-problem | Cost per Node | Total Cost at Level |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $0$ | $1$ | $n$ | $n^2$ | $n^2$ |
| $1$ | $4$ | $n/2$ | $(n/2)^2 = n^2/4$ | $4 \cdot n^2/4 = n^2$ |
| $2$ | $16$ | $n/4$ | $n^2/16$ | $16 \cdot n^2/16 = n^2$ |
| $3$ | $64$ | $n/8$ | $n^2/64$ | $64 \cdot n^2/64 = n^2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $\log_2 n$ | $n^2$ | $1$ | $1$ | $n^2 \cdot 1 = n^2$ |

**Step 4: Sum across all internal levels (levels $0$ to $\log_2 n - 1$):**

There are $\log_2 n$ internal levels, each contributing $n^2$:

$$
\text{Internal Total} = n^2 \cdot \log_2 n
$$

**Step 5: Sum across all leaf levels (level $\log_2 n$):**

There are $n^2$ leaves, each costing $\Theta(1)$:

$$
\text{Leaf Total} = n^2 \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Step 6: Combine totals:**

$$
T(n) = n^2 \log_2 n + \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^2 \log n)
$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct tree depth calculation (1 mark)
- Correct per-level cost computation (3 marks)
- Correct sum of internal levels (1 mark)
- Correct leaf contribution (1 mark)
- Final asymptotic conclusion (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Substitution Method

**Goal:** Prove by induction that there exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n$ for all $n \geq 2$.

**Step 1: Base Case.** For $n = 2$: $T(2) = 2T(1) + 2 = 4$. We need $4 \leq c \cdot 2 \cdot 1 = 2c$, so $c \geq 2$. Choose $c = 2$. ✓

**Step 2: Inductive Hypothesis.** Assume $T(k) \leq c \cdot k \log_2 k$ for all $k < n$.

**Step 3: Inductive Step.** Using the recurrence and the hypothesis:

$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \leq 2 \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2} \log_2 \frac{n}{2} + n = c \cdot n (\log_2 n - 1) + n
$$

**Step 4: Simplification:**

$$
T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n - c \cdot n + n = c \cdot n \log_2 n - n(c - 1)
$$

**Step 5: Apply the inductive constraint.** We need:

$$
T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n
$$

This is satisfied if and only if:

$$
- n(c - 1) \leq 0 \iff c \geq 1
$$

Since we chose $c = 2 \geq 1$, the inequality holds.

**Step 6: Conclusion.** By induction:

$$
T(n) \leq 2 \cdot n \log_2 n \quad \text{for all } n \geq 2
$$

Therefore:

$$
T(n) = O(n \log n)
$$

**Mark Distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct base case (1 mark)
- Correct inductive hypothesis (1 mark)
- Correct inductive step using the recurrence (2 marks)
- Correct algebraic simplification (1 mark)
- Correct final constant selection and conclusion (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base case** in the iteration method: Students often terminate the sum but forget to multiply by $T(1)$ at the leaf level. This loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing the cases of Master Theorem**: Case 1 requires $f(n)$ to be polynomially **smaller**, Case 3 requires it to be polynomially **larger**. Students often flip these. Draw a memory aid: **MT1 → smaller f → leaves dominate**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to verify the regularity condition** in Case 3. Even if $f(n)$ appears polynomially larger, the regularity condition $a f(n/b) \leq c f(n)$ must hold. The KTU examiner explicitly checks this.
>
> 4. **Substitution method**: Students write the induction but forget the constants. Always check that the constant chosen (e.g., $c$) satisfies the base case AND the inductive step.
>
> 5. **Iteration method summation errors**: Geometric series sums with ratio $> 1$ diverge (sum is dominated by last term), while ratio $< 1$ converge (sum is bounded by constant). Mis-identifying the ratio is a frequent error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Recurrence Equations**

- [ ] **Definition**: A recurrence defines $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ on smaller inputs, with **base case(s)** that terminate the recursion.
- [ ] **General Form**: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — know the role of each parameter.
- [ ] **Master Theorem Critical Exponent**: $n^{\log_b a}$ is the benchmark against which $f(n)$ is compared.
- [ ] **Case 1 (Leaf-Dominant)**: $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}) \implies T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- [ ] **Case 2 (Balanced)**: $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n) \implies T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- [ ] **Case 3 (Root-Dominant)**: $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ with regularity $\implies T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- [ ] **Iteration Method**: Unroll the recurrence, find the pattern at iteration $i$, terminate at depth $\log_b n$, sum the geometric series.
- [ ] **Recursion Tree Method**: Total cost = (sum of internal level costs) + (sum of leaf level costs).
- [ ] **Substitution Method**: Guess the form, prove by strong induction, choose constants carefully to satisfy base case AND inductive step.
- [ ] **Change of Variables**: Useful for $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + \text{stuff}$. Substitute $n = 2^m$, solve transformed recurrence, convert back.
- [ ] **Common Recurrences to Memorize**:
  - Merge Sort: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \implies \Theta(n \log n)$.
  - Binary Search: $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 \implies \Theta(\log n)$.
  - Karatsuba: $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n) \implies \Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$.
  - Strassen: $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) \implies \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$.
- [ ] **Master Theorem Inapplicable**: When sub-problem sizes differ (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$) or when $f(n)$ is not polynomially comparable.
- [ ] **Geometric Series**: $\sum_{i=0}^{k} x^i = \frac{1 - x^{k+1}}{1-x}$ for $x \neq 1$.
- [ ] **Identity**: $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ — this identity is **essential** when simplifying leaf-level terms.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solution of Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solution of Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Recurrence Relation?

A **recurrence relation** is an equation (or inequality) that defines a sequence of values $T(n)$ in terms of one or more preceding values of the sequence, typically for smaller arguments. In algorithm analysis, a recurrence expresses the **running time** $T(n)$ of a recursive algorithm as a function of the running time on smaller inputs, plus the cost of the work done at the current level of recursion.

Formally, a recurrence has two essential components:

1. **The Recursive Body (Recursive Case):** An expression like $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ that reduces the problem into $a$ subproblems of size $n/b$, and combines their solutions using $f(n)$ work.
2. **The Base Case (Boundary Condition):** A stopping condition such as $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ or $T(2) = \Theta(1)$ that halts the recursion when the input size becomes a small constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim from Syllabus):**
> *"A recurrence is a function that is defined in terms of (a) one or more base cases and (b) itself, with smaller arguments."*
> Solving a recurrence means finding a **closed-form (explicit) expression** for $T(n)$ in terms of $n$ alone, removing all recursive references on the right-hand side.

## 1.2 What is the Iteration Method?

The **Iteration Method** (also called the **Substitution Method of Expansion** or **Recurrence Expansion Method**) is the most direct and intuitive technique for solving recurrence relations. The strategy is to:

1. Repeatedly **substitute** (expand) the recurrence body into itself.
2. **Unroll** the recursion $k$ times until a **pattern** emerges in the resulting summation.
3. **Terminate** the expansion at the base case to identify the depth $k$ of the recursion.
4. **Sum** the per-level work (the non-recursive part $f(n)$) across all levels to obtain a closed-form expression.

Unlike the Master Theorem or Akra-Bazzi method, the iteration method is **mechanical, doesn't require memorizing case classifications, and works for any recurrence** — including non-standard ones like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ or $T(n) = T(n-2) + n^2$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Unrolling a Russian Nesting Doll"

Imagine a **Russian Matryoshka doll**: the outermost doll contains a smaller doll, which contains an even smaller doll, and so on. The iteration method is the process of **opening each doll one-by-one**, laying them out on a table in a row, and then **counting the total size** of all the opened pieces.

In this analogy:
- The **outermost doll** = the original call $T(n)$.
- Each **smaller doll inside** = the recursive sub-call $T(n')$ where $n' < n$.
- The **material/paint on the doll's surface** = the non-recursive work $f(n)$ done at that level.
- The **smallest solid wooden piece** (which cannot be opened further) = the base case $T(1)$.
- **Counting the total paint + the wooden base** = computing the closed-form sum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Iteration"?** We are *iterating* (looping) the algebraic substitution process until the structure repeats. The name distinguishes this approach from the *Recursion Tree* method, which draws a tree first, and from the *Master Theorem*, which compares terms in $f(n)$ against $n^{\log_b a}$.

## 1.4 Standard Notation Used Throughout KTU 2024

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $T(n)$ | Total time/space on input of size $n$ | unknown (to be solved) |
| $a$ | Number of recursive subproblems | $a \geq 1$ (integer) |
| $b$ | Factor by which input size shrinks | $b > 1$ (integer) |
| $f(n)$ | Cost of work *outside* the recursive calls | $\Theta(\cdot)$ of some function |
| $n^{\log_b a}$ | The "critical exponent" from the recursion alone | comparison benchmark |
| $k$ | Number of recursion levels (depth) | $k = \lfloor \log_b n \rfloor$ typically |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When the question says "solve by the iteration method," it almost always expects: (i) full unrolled expansion, (ii) identification of the pattern, (iii) substitution of the base case to find the depth, and (iv) the final closed form **and** the asymptotic Big-Oh class.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Pipeline of the Iteration Method

The method follows a strict, examiner-friendly pipeline. Memorize these five stages in order.

**Stage 1 — Write the Recurrence in Canonical Form**
Express $T(n)$ clearly: separate the recursive terms (the $aT(\cdot)$ part) from the non-recursive work $f(n)$. For example, normalize $T(n) = 2T(n/3) + 5n$ by writing it as $T(n) = 2\,T(n/3) + 5n$. Always restate the base case $T(c) = d$ for some small constant $c$.

**Stage 2 — Perform $k$ Substitutions to Unroll**
Substitute the right-hand side into itself $k$ times, treating $k$ as a symbolic integer (not yet substituted). At each substitution, the argument of $T(\cdot)$ shrinks: $n \to n/b \to n/b^2 \to \dots \to n/b^k$. The non-recursive part also shrinks accordingly because $f$ is applied to the new argument: $f(n) \to f(n/b) \to f(n/b^2) \to \dots$.

**Stage 3 — Identify the Recursion Depth $k^*$**
The recursion stops when the subproblem size reaches the base-case size $c$. Solve the equation:

$$n / b^k = c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k^* = \log_b (n/c)$$

For integer floors: $k^* = \lfloor \log_b n \rfloor$ (when $c$ is a small constant that becomes negligible at the asymptotic level).

**Stage 4 — Substitute $k = k^*$ and Split the Sum**
The unrolled expression typically looks like:

$$T(n) = a^k \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^k}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^i \, f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^i}\right)$$

Plug in $k = k^*$. The first term $a^{k^*} T(c)$ becomes a **constant** $\Theta(a^{\log_b n}) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$. The second term is a **finite geometric-like sum** that you must evaluate using standard summation identities (see Formula Sheet below).

**Stage 5 — Asymptotic Simplification**
Discard lower-order terms, constant factors, and floors/ceilings. Express the result in **Big-Oh**, **Big-Theta**, or **Big-Omega** notation as the question demands.

## 2.2 The Master Identity (Unrolled Form)

This is the **single most important equation** in the iteration method. Every solution passes through it.

$$T(n) = a^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^{i} f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Derivation of the identity (quick intuition):
> - $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — level $0$
> - $T(n) = a\bigl[aT(n/b^2) + f(n/b)\bigr] + f(n) = a^2 T(n/b^2) + a f(n/b) + f(n)$ — level $2$
> - $T(n) = a^3 T(n/b^3) + a^2 f(n/b^2) + a f(n/b) + f(n)$ — level $3$
> - The general pattern is the master identity above.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Iteration Method

| # | Recurrence Pattern | Depth $k^*$ | Closed Form (Iterated) | Big-Oh Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$,  $T(1)=1$ | $n-1$ | $T(n) = n$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| 2 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$,  $T(1)=1$ | $n-1$ | $T(n) = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| 3 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n^2$,  $T(1)=1$ | $n-1$ | $T(n) = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$ | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| 4 | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$,  $T(0)=1$ | $n$ | $T(n) = 2^{n+1} - 1$ | $\Theta(2^n)$ |
| 5 | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = \log_2 n + 1$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| 6 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = 2n - 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| 7 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = n\log_2 n + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| 8 | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = 3^{\log_2 n}(1) + \sum 3^i \cdot (n/2^i)$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$ |
| 9 | $T(n) = T(n/2) + n$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = 2n - 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| 10 | $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$,  $T(1)=1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $T(n) = n^2 (\log_2 n + 1)$ | $\Theta(n^2 \log n)$ |

### 2.3.1 Essential Summation Identities Used During Stage 4

| Identity | Formula | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Constant Sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} c = ck$ | $f(n) = c$ (a constant) |
| Geometric Sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} r^{i} = \dfrac{r^{k}-1}{r-1}$, for $r>1$ | $f(n) = a^i \cdot (\text{const})$ |
| Decreasing Geometric | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \dfrac{1}{b^{i}} = \dfrac{1 - (1/b)^k}{1 - 1/b} < \dfrac{b}{b-1}$ | $f(n) = n / b^i$ type |
| Logarithmic Sum | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (n/b^i) = n \cdot \dfrac{1 - (1/b)^k}{1 - 1/b} \approx \dfrac{nb}{b-1}$ | $f(n) = n / b^i$ (linear shrink) |
| $i$-Times Geometric | $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} i \cdot r^{i} = \dfrac{r - (k-1)r^{k+1} + kr^{k+1} - r^{k+1}}{(1-r)^2}$ | $f(n) = i \cdot a^i$ patterns |
| Power Sum | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ type |
| Sum of Squares | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i^{2} = \dfrac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{6}$ | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n^2$ type |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The iteration method is the *only* method that works uniformly for divide-and-conquer recurrences, decrease-by-one recurrences, and pathological recurrences like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$. In real-world software engineering, it is used to **verify** the Big-Oh output of automated profilers and to **derive** closed forms for cost models in compiler optimization, hardware cache analysis, and parallel algorithm design.

## 2.4 Decision Heuristic — When Does the Sum Dominate?

After applying the master identity, you will face a sum of the form $\sum_{i=0}^{k^*-1} a^i f(n/b^i)$. Whether the sum is dominated by:

- The **last term** (largest $i = k^*-1$) — happens when the subproblem work $f$ grows at least as fast as the number of subproblems $a^i$ shrinks.
- The **first term** (smallest $i = 0$) — happens when $f$ shrinks faster than $a^i$ grows.
- A **balance** of all terms — happens when the geometric growth of $a^i$ exactly matches the decay of $f(n/b^i)$.

This trichotomy is precisely the structural foundation of the **Master Theorem** (Cases 1, 2, 3), which can be derived rigorously from the iteration method.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains **four fully-worked KTU-standard examples**. Each example follows the five-stage pipeline from Section 2. **No step is skipped, no algebraic transition is omitted.**

---

## 3.1 Example 1 — Linear Decrease with Constant Work

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Stage 1 — Canonical Form

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Here, $a = 1$ recursive subproblem, the argument decreases by $1$ (not divide), and $f(n) = 1$ (constant work).

### Stage 2 — Unroll $k$ Times

**Substitution 1:** Replace $T(n)$ on the left by its definition, and replace $T(n-1)$ on the right by $T(n-1) = T(n-2) + 1$:

$$T(n) = \bigl[T(n-2) + 1\bigr] + 1 = T(n-2) + 2$$

**Substitution 2:** Replace $T(n-2)$ by $T(n-3) + 1$:

$$T(n) = \bigl[T(n-3) + 1\bigr] + 2 = T(n-3) + 3$$

**Substitution 3:** Pattern is now visible. After $i$ substitutions:

$$T(n) = T(n-i) + i$$

### Stage 3 — Determine Depth $k^*$

Stop when the subproblem argument reaches the base case $T(1)$. Set $n - i = 1$:

$$i = n - 1 = k^*$$

### Stage 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

Substitute $i = n - 1$ into the pattern $T(n) = T(n-i) + i$:

$$T(n) = T\bigl(n - (n-1)\bigr) + (n-1) = T(1) + (n-1)$$

Apply the base case $T(1) = 1$:

$$T(n) = 1 + n - 1 = n$$

### Stage 5 — Asymptotic Class

$$T(n) = n = \Theta(n)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** This is the recurrence of a simple linear loop (e.g., linear search). It is correct that the running time is linear in $n$.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Linear Decrease with Linear Work

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Stage 1 — Canonical Form

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Here, $a = 1$, the argument decreases by $1$, and $f(n) = n$.

### Stage 2 — Unroll $k$ Times

**Substitution 1:**
$$T(n) = T(n-1) + n = \bigl[T(n-2) + (n-1)\bigr] + n = T(n-2) + (n-1) + n$$

**Substitution 2:**
$$T(n) = \bigl[T(n-3) + (n-2)\bigr] + (n-1) + n = T(n-3) + (n-2) + (n-1) + n$$

**Substitution 3:** After $i$ substitutions, the pattern is:

$$T(n) = T(n-i) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} (n - j)$$

### Stage 3 — Determine Depth $k^*$

Setting $n - i = 1 \Rightarrow i = n - 1$.

### Stage 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

Plug $i = n-1$:

$$T(n) = T(1) + \sum_{j=0}^{n-2} (n - j)$$

Apply $T(1) = 1$. Now evaluate the sum. Re-index with $m = n - j$; when $j = 0$, $m = n$; when $j = n-2$, $m = 2$:

$$\sum_{j=0}^{n-2} (n - j) = \sum_{m=2}^{n} m = \bigl(1 + 2 + 3 + \dots + n\bigr) - 1 = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1$$

So:

$$T(n) = 1 + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1 = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$$

### Stage 5 — Asymptotic Class

$$T(n) = \frac{n^2 + n}{2} = \Theta(n^2)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** This recurrence models Selection Sort's nested loop. Quadratic time $\Theta(n^2)$ matches the known cost.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Divide and Conquer with Linear Combine Cost (Merge Sort)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Stage 1 — Canonical Form

$$T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Here, $a = 2$ (two subproblems), $b = 2$ (size halves), and $f(n) = n$ (the combine step — e.g., merge cost).

### Stage 2 — Unroll Using the Master Identity

Apply the master identity:

$$T(n) = a^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^{i} f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$$

Substituting $a=2$, $b=2$, $f(n)=n$:

$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}}$$

**Crucial observation:** The factor $2^i$ from the subproblem count **exactly cancels** the $1/2^i$ from the argument shrinkage in $f(n/2^i) = n/2^i$:

$$2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = n$$

The sum simplifies dramatically:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} n = n \cdot k$$

### Stage 3 — Determine Depth $k^*$

The recursion bottoms out at $T(1)$. Set $n/2^k = 1$:

$$2^k = n \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k^* = \log_2 n$$

### Stage 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \cdot \log_2 n = n \cdot 1 + n \log_2 n = n \log_2 n + n$$

### Stage 5 — Asymptotic Class

$$T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This is the canonical Merge Sort recurrence. The result $\Theta(n \log n)$ is the lower bound for comparison-based sorting, and the iteration method lets you **derive** it from scratch without invoking the Master Theorem.

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Divide and Conquer with Sub-Linear Combine Cost

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Stage 1 — Canonical Form

$$T(n) = 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Here, $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1$.

### Stage 2 — Unroll Using the Master Identity

$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} \cdot 1$$

The sum is a standard geometric series with ratio $r = 2$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} = \frac{2^{k} - 1}{2 - 1} = 2^{k} - 1$$

Therefore:

$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \bigl(2^{k} - 1\bigr)$$

### Stage 3 — Determine Depth $k^*$

Setting $n/2^k = 1 \Rightarrow k^* = \log_2 n$.

### Stage 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + 2^{\log_2 n} - 1 = n \cdot 1 + n - 1 = 2n - 1$$

### Stage 5 — Asymptotic Class

$$T(n) = 2n - 1 = \Theta(n)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** This is the recurrence of Breadth-First Search on a balanced binary tree, or of binary tree traversals where the per-level combine cost is $O(1)$. The total is dominated by the *number of leaves* $n$, not the height.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 — Master Theorem Case 1 Type (Master-Recursion Dominates)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.

### Stage 1 — Canonical Form

$$T(n) = 3\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$

Here, $a = 3$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

### Stage 2 — Unroll Using the Master Identity

$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}}$$

Combine the exponentials: $3^i / 2^i = (3/2)^i$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = n \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{i}$$

Apply the geometric sum formula with $r = 3/2 > 1$:

$$n \cdot \frac{(3/2)^{k} - 1}{3/2 - 1} = n \cdot \frac{(3/2)^{k} - 1}{1/2} = 2n \bigl[(3/2)^{k} - 1\bigr]$$

So:

$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + 2n \left[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{k} - 1\right]$$

### Stage 3 — Determine Depth $k^*$

Setting $n/2^k = 1 \Rightarrow k^* = \log_2 n$.

### Stage 4 — Substitute and Evaluate

**Term A** (leaf term):
$$3^{k} T(1) = 3^{\log_2 n} = 2^{\log_2 3 \cdot \log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 3}$$

**Term B** (sum):
$$2n \left[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\log_2 n} - 1\right] = 2n \cdot \frac{3^{\log_2 n}}{2^{\log_2 n}} - 2n = 2n \cdot \frac{n^{\log_2 3}}{n} - 2n = 2n^{\log_2 3} - 2n$$

**Combine:**
$$T(n) = n^{\log_2 3} + 2n^{\log_2 3} - 2n = 3n^{\log_2 3} - 2n$$

### Stage 5 — Asymptotic Class

Since $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585 > 1$:

$$T(n) = 3n^{\log_2 3} - 2n = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_2 3}\right) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Theorem Cross-Check:** Here $a = 3$, $b = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 3} \approx n^{1.585}$. Since $f(n) = n = O(n^{\log_2 3 - \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon \approx 0.585$, this is **Case 1** of the Master Theorem. The iteration method must yield the same answer, and it does: $\Theta(n^{\log_2 3})$.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Summary Matrix of the Five Examples

| # | Recurrence | Depth $k^*$ | Closed Form | Big-Oh | Master Theorem Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + 1$ | $n-1$ | $n$ | $\Theta(n)$ | N/A (linear) |
| 2 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | $n-1$ | $n(n+1)/2$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | N/A (linear) |
| 3 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $\log_2 n$ | $n\log_2 n + n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Case 2 |
| 4 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ | $\log_2 n$ | $2n - 1$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Case 1 |
| 5 | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$ | $\log_2 n$ | $3n^{\log_2 3} - 2n$ | $\Theta(n^{1.585})$ | Case 1 |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Iteration Pipeline (Five-Stage Workflow)

This flowchart captures the algorithmic workflow of the iteration method, exactly mirroring the five stages from Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Read Recurrence T of n equals a times T of n over b plus f of n, with base case T of c equals d"] --> B["STAGE 1: Canonical Form — Identify a, b, f of n, base case"]
    B --> C["STAGE 2: Unroll k times — Substitute recurrence into itself k symbolic times"]
    C --> D["STAGE 2B: Detect Pattern — Look for i in T of n equals a to the i T of n over b to the i plus sum of a to j f of n over b to the j"]
    D --> E["STAGE 3: Solve n over b to the k equals c — Compute depth k star equals log base b of n over c"]
    E --> F{"STAGE 4A: Does a to the i cancel n over b to the i exactly?"}
    F -- "Yes" --> G["STAGE 4B: Each level contributes constant c — Sum equals c times k star"]
    F -- "No" --> H["STAGE 4C: Apply geometric sum formula — Sum equals r to the k minus 1 over r minus 1"]
    F -- "Decay" --> I["STAGE 4D: Apply decreasing geometric — Bounded by constant b over b minus 1"]
    G --> J["STAGE 5: Substitute k star, add leaf term a to the k star T of c, simplify"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["STAGE 5B: Drop floors, ceilings, constants — Express in Big-Oh notation"]
    K --> L["END: Final Answer T of n equals closed form equals Theta of g of n"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

This tree diagram visualizes how the iteration method "sees" the per-level work that we summed analytically in Example 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0["Level 0: T of n — Work n — Subproblems 1"]
    R1L["Level 1 Left: T of n over 2 — Work n over 2 — Subproblems 2"]
    R1R["Level 1 Right: T of n over 2 — Work n over 2 — Subproblems 2"]
    R2LL["Level 2 LL: T of n over 4 — Work n over 4 — Subproblems 3"]
    R2LR["Level 2 LR: T of n over 4 — Work n over 4 — Subproblems 3"]
    R2RL["Level 2 RL: T of n over 4 — Work n over 4 — Subproblems 3"]
    R2RR["Level 2 RR: T of n over 4 — Work n over 4 — Subproblems 3"]
    RDOT["Level Dots — Continues down to leaves"]
    RLEAF["Leaf Level: n leaves each T of 1 — Total leaf work equals n"]
    R0 --> R1L
    R0 --> R1R
    R1L --> R2LL
    R1L --> R2LR
    R1R --> R2RL
    R1R --> R2RR
    R2LL --> RDOT
    R2LR --> RDOT
    R2RL --> RDOT
    R2RR --> RDOT
    RDOT --> RLEAF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the recursion tree:**
> - **Each level contributes exactly $n$ units of work** because the number of nodes doubles while the work per node halves.
> - **Total work = (work per level) × (number of levels) = $n \times \log_2 n$**.
> - **Leaves contribute $n \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n)$** which is dominated by $n \log n$ for large $n$.
> - This visual corresponds exactly to the algebraic sum $\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} n = n \cdot k$ in Example 3.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of the Method

This block diagram maps the *engineering* workflow: from problem statement, through the iteration engine, to validated output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN["INPUT: Recurrence T of n equals a T of n over b plus f of n with base case T of c equals d"] --> PRE["PREPROCESSOR: Extract parameters a, b, f of n, c, d"]
    PRE --> EXP["EXPANSION ENGINE: Iteratively substitute k times, generate symbolic pattern"]
    EXP --> PAT["PATTERN MATCHER: Detect arithmetic, geometric, polynomial structure in sum"]
    PAT --> DEP["DEPTH COMPUTER: Solve n over b to the k equals c for k star"]
    DEP --> SUM["SUMMATION SOLVER: Apply sum identities, evaluate closed form"]
    SUM --> SIMP["ASYMPTOTIC SIMPLIFIER: Drop floors, ceilings, constants, low-order terms"]
    SIMP --> OUT["OUTPUT: T of n equals g of n equals Theta of h of n"]
    OUT --> VAL["VALIDATOR: Cross check with Master Theorem or Recursion Tree"]
    VAL --> DONE["DONE: Verified closed form and Big-Oh class"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation for KTU Exams:** Always draw the recursion tree *alongside* the algebraic unrolling. The two methods must agree. If they don't, you have an algebraic error in Stage 2 of the pipeline.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> **Q1 (3 marks):** Define a recurrence relation. With a suitable example, distinguish between the recursive case and the base case of a recurrence.

**Model Answer:**
A recurrence relation is a mathematical equation that defines a sequence of values $T(n)$ in terms of one or more of its preceding values for smaller arguments. It consists of two parts: **(i) the recursive case**, which expresses $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ evaluated at a smaller argument (e.g., $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$), and **(ii) the base case**, which specifies the value of $T$ at a small constant input where the recursion terminates (e.g., $T(1) = 1$). For example, in the recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$, the equation $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ is the recursive case, while $T(1) = 1$ is the base case. **(3 marks — Full marks for clear distinction.)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> **Q2 (3 marks):** What is the iteration method for solving recurrence relations? List its key steps.

**Model Answer:**
The iteration method is a technique for solving recurrence relations by **repeatedly substituting** the recurrence into itself until a pattern emerges, then summing the per-level work to obtain a closed-form expression. The key steps are: **(1)** Write the recurrence in canonical form, **(2)** Unroll the recurrence $k$ times and identify the pattern, **(3)** Determine the recursion depth $k^*$ by setting the subproblem size equal to the base case, **(4)** Substitute $k^*$ and evaluate the resulting sum using standard summation identities, and **(5)** Simplify asymptotically to express the answer in Big-Oh notation. **(3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Iteration Method on $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$**
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style, Module 1]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method. Show every step of the expansion.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Verify your closed-form answer from part (a) using a recursion tree, and state the asymptotic Big-Theta class. Also, identify the Master Theorem case that applies.

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Canonical form.** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, with base case $T(1) = 1$. Parameters: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Unroll.** Applying the master identity:

$$T(n) = 2^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the sum.** Note that $2^i \cdot n/2^i = n$ for every $i$:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} n = n \cdot k$$

**Step 4: Determine $k^*$.** Setting $n/2^k = 1 \Rightarrow k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 5: Substitute and close the form.**

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + n \log_2 n = n + n \log_2 n$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Recursion tree verification:**
- **Level $i$** has $2^i$ subproblems, each with argument $n/2^i$ and work $n/2^i$.
- **Per-level work** = $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n$. Constant across all $\log_2 n$ levels.
- **Leaf work** = $2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) = n \cdot 1 = n$.
- **Total work** = $n \log_2 n + n$.

**Asymptotic class:** $T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

**Master Theorem case:** $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 2} = n^{1} = n$. Since $f(n) = n = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$, this is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem. The Master Theorem gives $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n) = \Theta(n \log n)$, which agrees with our iteration result. **✅ Verified.**

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Iteration Method on $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$**
> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model Paper Style, Module 1]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$ using the iteration method.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** If the recurrence were changed to $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n^2$ with $T(1) = 1$, determine its Big-Oh class using the iteration method. Compare both results and comment.

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Canonical form.** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$, with $T(1) = 1$. Parameters: $a = 3$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Apply the master identity.**

$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify the sum.** Factor $n$ and combine exponents:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = n \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{i}$$

Apply the geometric sum formula with ratio $r = 3/2$:

$$n \cdot \frac{(3/2)^{k} - 1}{3/2 - 1} = 2n \bigl[(3/2)^{k} - 1\bigr]$$

**Step 4: Determine $k^*$.** $k = \log_2 n$.

**Step 5: Substitute and close the form.**

$$T(n) = 3^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + 2n \left[\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{\log_2 n} - 1\right]$$

Convert $3^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 3}$ and $(3/2)^{\log_2 n} = 3^{\log_2 n} / 2^{\log_2 n} = n^{\log_2 3} / n$:

$$T(n) = n^{\log_2 3} + 2n \left[\frac{n^{\log_2 3}}{n} - 1\right] = n^{\log_2 3} + 2n^{\log_2 3} - 2n = 3n^{\log_2 3} - 2n$$

**Asymptotic class:** $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Modified recurrence:** $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n^2$, with $T(1) = 1$. Here $f(n) = n^2$.

**Apply the master identity:**

$$T(n) = 3^{k} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{k}}\right) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} 3^{i} \cdot \left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right)^{2}$$

Simplify the sum:

$$3^{i} \cdot \frac{n^{2}}{2^{2i}} = n^{2} \cdot \frac{3^{i}}{4^{i}} = n^{2} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i}$$

Apply the geometric sum formula with ratio $r = 3/4 < 1$:

$$n^{2} \cdot \frac{1 - (3/4)^{k}}{1 - 3/4} = 4n^{2} \left[1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{k}\right]$$

Substitute $k = \log_2 n$:

$$\text{Sum} = 4n^{2} \left[1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{\log_2 n}\right]$$

As $n \to \infty$, $(3/4)^{\log_2 n} \to 0$, so the sum is bounded above by $4n^2$.

**Closed form:**

$$T(n) = 3^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + 4n^{2}\left[1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{\log_2 n}\right] = n^{\log_2 3} + 4n^{2} - 4n^{2}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{\log_2 n}$$

Since $\log_2 3 \approx 1.585 < 2$, the term $4n^2$ dominates $n^{1.585}$:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$$

**Comparison and comment:**
- For $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n$: $\Theta(n^{1.585})$. The recursion dominates; cost is super-linear.
- For $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n^2$: $\Theta(n^{2})$. The combine cost $n^2$ dominates the recursion's $n^{1.585}$.
- **Insight:** When the per-level combine work $f(n)$ grows polynomially faster than the critical exponent $n^{\log_b a}$, the total cost is dictated by $f(n)$ at the **root** (largest subproblem). This is the iteration-method perspective on **Case 3** of the Master Theorem.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in the Iteration Method (KTU Valuation Key Insights):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the base-case substitution into the master identity.** Many students write $T(n) = a^k T(n/b^k) + \sum \cdots$ and then forget to evaluate $T(n/b^k) = T(c) = d$ at $k = k^*$. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Algebraic error in the exponent conversion $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$.** Always show the step $a^{\log_b n} = 2^{\log_b a \cdot \log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$ explicitly. Sloppy work loses full credit on the final closed form.
> 3. **Using the wrong geometric-sum formula direction.** When $r > 1$, the sum grows as $(r^k - 1)/(r-1)$. When $r < 1$, the sum is bounded by a constant $1/(1-r)$. Mixing these up yields an incorrect Big-Oh class — a **fatal error** worth 7 marks.
> 4. **Ignoring floors and ceilings in $n/b$ for non-power-of-2 inputs.** Mention "we assume $n$ is a power of $b$ for clarity; the asymptotic class is unaffected" — this single sentence can earn 1 bonus mark.
> 5. **Not stating the Big-Oh class explicitly.** The closed form is worth ~10 marks; the Big-Oh class is worth ~4 marks. Always end with $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$.
> 6. **Confusing $T(n) = T(n-1) + f(n)$ (linear recurrence) with $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + f(n)$ (divide-and-conquer).** The iteration method handles both, but the depth $k^*$ is $n$ for the former and $\log_b n$ for the latter.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Iteration Method (Module 1, KTU 2024)**
>
> - [ ] **Definition:** Iteration method = repeated substitution + pattern detection + summation.
> - [ ] **Master identity** to memorize: $T(n) = a^{k} T(n/b^{k}) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} a^{i} f(n/b^{i})$.
> - [ ] **Depth formula:** $k^* = \log_b (n/c)$ where $c$ is the base-case input size.
> - [ ] **Key exponent conversion:** $a^{\log_b n} = n^{\log_b a}$.
> - [ ] **Linear recurrences** ($T(n) = T(n-1) + f(n)$) have depth $n$, not $\log n$.
> - [ ] **Divide-and-conquer recurrences** ($T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$) have depth $\log_b n$.
> - [ ] **Per-level work** for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + n$ type is **constant** $= n$ (because $a^i$ cancels $(1/b)^i$).
> - [ ] **Geometric sum identities:** sum with $r > 1$ grows as $r^k$; sum with $r < 1$ is bounded by a constant.
> - [ ] **Master Theorem cross-check:** Case 1 → recursion leaves dominate, Case 2 → balanced, Case 3 → root cost dominates.
> - [ ] **Always state both the closed form AND the Big-Oh class** in the final answer.
> - [ ] **Always mention the assumption** "$n$ is a power of $b$" to handle floors/ceilings cleanly.
> - [ ] **The iteration method works for ALL recurrences** — including non-standard ones like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$ and $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$ — whereas the Master Theorem does not.
> - [ ] **Mental models:** Russian dolls (unrolling), onion layers (peeling), snowball (growing), tree branches (splitting).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Solution of Recurrence Equations: Iteration Method**

topics.title: Recursion Tree Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recursion Tree Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Recursion Tree Method** is a non-closed-form, diagrammatic technique used in the analysis of algorithms to asymptotically solve **recurrence relations** that describe the running time (or space) of **divide-and-conquer algorithms**. The method works by expanding the recurrence into a tree where each node represents the **cost contributed by a single subproblem**, the children of a node represent the recursive subcalls it generates, and the subproblem size shrinks at each level according to the recurrence's branching rule.

Formally, for a recurrence of the form:
$$T(n) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i T(b_i n) + f(n)$$

the recursion tree enumerates the subproblems, calculates the per-level work $W(L)$, sums the level-wise cost $\sum_{L=0}^{h} W(L)$, and adds the leaf cost to obtain a tight asymptotic bound on $T(n)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The Recursion Tree Method is one of the **three prescribed techniques** for solving recurrences, alongside the **Substitution Method** and the **Master Theorem**. It is treated as the *intuitive* and *visual* precursor to the formal Master Theorem and is heavily weighted in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Family Tree of a Company

Imagine a **CEO** at the top of a corporation who is given the task: *"Grow the company by solving a problem of size n."* The CEO is too busy to do everything herself, so she splits the work between **2 Vice Presidents (VPs)**, each tackling a problem of size $\frac{n}{2}$. Each VP in turn delegates to 2 Directors, each handling $\frac{n}{4}$, and so on, until the problems become small enough that a single junior employee can handle them trivially.

- **The CEO's overhead** (cost of splitting, merging, and bookkeeping) = the $f(n)$ term.
- **The number of recursive calls at each level** = the branching factor $a$ raised to the level.
- **The size of the problem at depth $L$** = $n / b^L$.
- **The leaves** = the base case, where the work is a constant (e.g., $\Theta(1)$).
- **The total cost of the company** = the sum of all work done at every level of the org chart.

The recursion tree is the **org chart**, and summing the costs of every row gives the **total company cost**, which is the running time of the algorithm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Just as you cannot tell how expensive a corporation is by looking at only the CEO, you cannot solve a recurrence by looking at only the root node. The total cost is the sum of work done at **every level**, from the root all the way down to the leaves.

## 1.3 Where It Fits in Algorithm Design

The recursion tree method is the workhorse used to solve recurrences arising from canonical algorithms such as:

| Algorithm | Recurrence |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ |
| **Binary Search** | $T(n) = T(n/2) + \Theta(1)$ |
| **Karatsuba Multiplication** | $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ |
| **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ |
| **Median of Medians (Selection)** | $T(n) = T(n/5) + T(7n/10) + \Theta(n)$ |
| **Towers of Hanoi** | $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + \Theta(1)$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Phase Operational Protocol

The recursion tree method, when applied rigorously for a KTU exam, follows exactly four mechanical phases.

**Phase 1 — Draw the Tree (Geometric Expansion)**
Expand the recurrence $k$ levels deep. At each level $L$, every node of size $m$ spawns $a$ children, each of size $\frac{m}{b}$. The expansion is purely visual and does not yet involve arithmetic summation.

**Phase 2 — Compute Per-Level Work $W(L)$**
For every node at level $L$, evaluate the non-recursive cost $f(\text{size})$. Multiply by the number of nodes at that level. The standard result is:
$$W(L) = a^L \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^L}\right)$$

**Phase 3 — Sum Across All Levels**
Add the per-level work for every level $L = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, h$, where $h$ is the height of the tree (the level at which subproblem size drops to a constant, typically $h = \lfloor \log_b n \rfloor$). Also add the cost of the leaves.

**Phase 4 — Apply Asymptotic Bounds**
Use the **Master Theorem's Case logic** (or geometric series rules) to convert the level sum into a $\Theta(\cdot)$ bound. This is the critical "judgment" step examiners reward.

## 2.2 The Three Asymptotic Cases — Cheat Sheet

For a recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$, the recursion tree sums to one of three canonical forms.

| Case | Condition | Recursion Tree Interpretation | Closed-Form Bound |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ | Root cost **decays** geometrically; leaf cost **dominates**. | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ | Every level contributes **equal** asymptotic work. | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$ |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ | Root cost **grows**; root level **dominates**. | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A recursion tree can sometimes be **asymmetric** (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$). In that case, the geometric series trick fails and you must argue using the **leftmost or rightmost branch path** to bound the height. Always mention the path-based bound in the answer.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ symbol is forbidden inside markdown tables. We use the LaTeX command $\vert$ for absolute value bars.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Number of subproblems at level $L$ | $a^L$ | Dimensionless integer |
| 2 | Size of each subproblem at level $L$ | $\dfrac{n}{b^L}$ | Input size |
| 3 | **Per-Level Work** $W(L)$ | $a^L \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^L}\right)$ | Cost units |
| 4 | **Height of the tree** (symmetric) | $h = \log_b n$ | Number of levels to leaves |
| 5 | **Height of the tree** (asymmetric, longest path) | $h_{\max} = \log_{b_{\min}} n$ | where $b_{\min} = \min_i(b_i)$ |
| 6 | **Number of leaves** | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Equals $a^h$ |
| 7 | Geometric series sum (ratio $r < 1$) | $\sum_{L=0}^{\infty} r^L = \dfrac{1}{1-r}$ | Converges to constant |
| 8 | Geometric series sum (ratio $r = 1$) | $\sum_{L=0}^{h} 1 = h + 1 = \log_b n + 1$ | Harmonic with log |
| 9 | Geometric series sum (ratio $r > 1$) | $\sum_{L=0}^{h} r^L = \dfrac{r^{h+1} - 1}{r - 1}$ | Dominated by last term $r^h$ |
| 10 | Cost at the leaves | $a^h \cdot \Theta(1) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | $T(1)$ per leaf |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The recursion tree method is not just an academic exercise — it underpins the **time-complexity analysis** of:

- **Cloud Computing Schedulers** (e.g., divide-and-conquer task allocation in Hadoop/Spark).
- **VLSI Design Automation** (recursive floorplanning algorithms).
- **Computational Geometry** (recursive convex hull, closest pair of points).
- **Cryptography** (recursive modular exponentiation used in RSA).
- **Bioinformatics** (Smith–Waterman sequence alignment with gap penalties).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:** At Google, the cost model of distributed sorting in **MapReduce** is derived using exactly this technique, where $f(n)$ is the network/merge cost and $a T(n/b)$ is the recursive partition across workers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Key Insight:** Examiners award 7 marks for the **diagram + level sum** and 7 marks for the **asymptotic conclusion and the substitution verification**. You must show *both* phases in every answer.

---

## 3.1 Example 1 (Symmetric, Case 2) — Merge Sort Recurrence

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{2}\right) + cn$, with $T(1) = \Theta(1)$, where $c$ is a positive constant.

### Step 1 — Build the Tree (First 4 Levels)

```
Level 0:                          cn
                                /     \
Level 1:                    c(n/2)    c(n/2)
                           /    \     /    \
Level 2:               c(n/4) c(n/4) c(n/4) c(n/4)
                      ...        (continues)
Level h:                  Θ(1) per leaf × n leaves
```

### Step 2 — Compute Per-Level Cost

At level $L$, there are $2^L$ subproblems, each of size $\frac{n}{2^L}$. The per-node cost is $c \cdot \frac{n}{2^L}$.

$$W(L) = 2^L \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2^L} = c n$$

Thus **every level contributes exactly $cn$ units of work**.

### Step 3 — Compute the Tree Height

The recursion bottoms out when $\frac{n}{2^L} = 1$, i.e., $L = \log_2 n$.

### Step 4 — Sum All Levels

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{L=0}^{\log_2 n} W(L) + \text{leaf cost} \\
     &= \sum_{L=0}^{\log_2 n} c n \;+\; n \cdot \Theta(1) \\
     &= c n \cdot (\log_2 n + 1) + \Theta(n)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Asymptotic Conclusion

The dominant term is $cn \log_2 n$, so:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

This matches the running time of Merge Sort — a result we can verify by substitution: $T(n) = 2 \cdot \Theta((n/2)\log(n/2)) + cn = n \log n - cn + cn = \Theta(n \log n)$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Example 2 (Case 3) — $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^2$

**Problem:** Show that $T(n) = 3T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{4}\right) + cn^2$ is $O(n^2)$.

### Step 1 — Per-Level Work

At level $L$, there are $3^L$ nodes, each of size $\frac{n}{4^L}$, with cost $c \cdot \left(\frac{n}{4^L}\right)^2$.

$$W(L) = 3^L \cdot c \cdot \frac{n^2}{4^{2L}} = c n^2 \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^L$$

### Step 2 — Sum the Geometric Series

Since the common ratio $\frac{3}{16} < 1$, the series converges to a constant:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum_{L=0}^{\infty} W(L) &= c n^2 \sum_{L=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^L \\
&= c n^2 \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{3}{16}} \\
&= c n^2 \cdot \frac{16}{13} \\
&< 2 c n^2
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Asymptotic Conclusion

$$\boxed{T(n) = O(n^2)}$$

This is **Case 3** of the Master Theorem because the root cost $n^2$ polynomially dominates the leaf cost $n^{\log_4 3} \approx n^{0.792}$.

---

## 3.3 Example 3 (Asymmetric) — $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$

This is the famous recurrence for algorithms like **median-of-medians selection**.

### Step 1 — Visualize the Tree

```
                          cn                         (size n)
                       /       \
                  c(n/3)        c(2n/3)              (Level 1)
                 /     \        /       \
             c(n/9) c(2n/9)  c(2n/9)  c(4n/9)        (Level 2)
              ...        (the rightmost branch dominates)
```

### Step 2 — Per-Level Cost

Every level $L$ has total work summing to $cn$ (each level partitions $n$ into pieces totaling $n$).

$$W(L) = c n \quad \text{for all } L$$

### Step 3 — Height is Asymmetric

The **rightmost (longest)** path decreases as $\frac{2n}{3}$ each level. So height is:

$$h = \log_{3/2} n$$

### Step 4 — Total Cost

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{L=0}^{\log_{3/2} n} c n \\
     &= c n \cdot \log_{3/2} n + \text{leaf cost} \\
     &= c n \log_{3/2} n + O(n)
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5 — Asymptotic Conclusion

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write $h = \log_3 n$ using the *short* branch. This is **wrong** for an asymmetric tree — you must use the **longest** path ($\log_{3/2} n$ here). The $\Theta(n \log n)$ bound is unchanged because logarithms to different bases differ only by a constant factor.

---

## 3.4 Example 4 (Case 1) — $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + cn$

### Step 1 — Per-Level Work

$$W(L) = 4^L \cdot c \cdot \frac{n}{2^L} = c n \cdot 2^L$$

### Step 2 — Height

$h = \log_2 n$, so the last level has $4^{\log_2 n} = n^2$ leaves, each $\Theta(1)$.

### Step 3 — Sum

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= c n \cdot \sum_{L=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} 2^L + \Theta(n^2) \\
     &= c n \cdot (2^{\log_2 n} - 1) + \Theta(n^2) \\
     &= c n (n - 1) + \Theta(n^2) \\
     &= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}$$

This is **Case 1** of the Master Theorem, where leaf cost $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$ dominates the root cost $n$.

---

## 3.5 Example 5 (Unbalanced) — $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ (Towers of Hanoi variant)

### Step 1 — Per-Level Work

The single branch gives $W(L) = c(n - L)$.

### Step 2 — Height = $n - 1$ Levels

### Step 3 — Sum

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{L=0}^{n-1} c(n - L) \\
     &= c \cdot \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \\
     &= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **Diagram Interpretation:** Each level contributes the **same** cost $cn$ (sum of node labels at any horizontal slice equals $cn$). The tree has height $h = \log_2 n$ and $n$ leaves. The total cost is therefore $cn \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Asymmetric Tree ($T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$)

```mermaid
graph TD
    N0["cn  (size n)"]:::root
    N1L["c(n/3)  (size n/3)"]:::left

    N2LL["c(n/9)"]:::leaf

    N2RR["c(4n/9)"]:::leaf

    NB2["..."]:::dot

    N0 --> N1L
```
