On full (`num = size`), we double capacity to $2 \cdot \text{size}$. **Potential function [2 marks]:** $$\Phi(T) \;=\; \begin{cases} 2 \cdot \text{num} - \text{size} & \text{if } \text{size} \geq 1 \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ This is non-negative because whenever the table is non-empty, $\text{num} \geq \lceil \text{size}/2 \rceil$ (we never let load drop below $1/2$), so $2 \cdot \text{num} \geq \text{size}$. **Case 1 — No resize [2 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} c_i &= 1 \\ \Delta\Phi &= [2(\text{num}+1) - \text{size}] - [2 \cdot \text{num} - \text{size}] = 2 \\ \hat{c}_i &= 1 + 2 = 3 \end{aligned}$$ **Case 2 — With resize (num = size = $m$ before insertion) [2 marks]:** $$\begin{aligned} c_i &= 1 + m \quad (\text{insert + copy}) \\ \Phi_{\text{before}} &= 2m - m = m \\ \Phi_{\text{after}} &= 2(m+1) - 2m = 2 \\ \Delta\Phi &= 2 - m \\ \hat{c}_i &= (1+m) + (2-m) = 3 \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Amortized cost per `INSERT` = **$3 = O(1)$**. Hence $n$ insertions cost $O(n)$ total. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Marks Lost:** > > 1. **Forgetting the initial condition** $\Phi(D_0) = 0$ in the Potential Method → lose **1 mark**. > 2. **Not verifying non-negativity** of the potential function at all reachable states → lose **1 mark**. > 3. **Confusing aggregate and accounting methods** — Aggregate gives *one* cost for *all* operations; Accounting gives a *per-operation-type* cost → lose up to **2 marks**. > 4. **In binary counter, miscounting flips**: the actual cost is the *number of bits flipped*, not the count of 1s. The standard formula is $c_i = t+1$ where $t$ = trailing 1s → lose **1 mark**. > 5. **Skipping the telescoping sum derivation** in long answers → lose **2 marks** as the final bound is incomplete without it. > 6. **Not stating the invariance condition** explicitly in the Accounting Method → lose **1 mark**. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Cost Accounting & Potential Methods** ### Core Concepts - **Amortized analysis** bounds the *average* cost per operation over a sequence — guarantees worst-case *amortized* cost, not worst-case single-op cost. - **Aggregate Method:** $\hat{c} = T(n) / n$. Simple, but loses per-operation granularity. - **Accounting Method:** Assign per-op-type $\hat{c}_i$; store surplus as credit on data items; invariant = credit $\geq 0$. - **Potential Method:** Define $\Phi : \mathcal{D} \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ on the whole structure; $\hat{c}_i = c_i + \Delta\Phi$; conditions = $\Phi(D_0) = 0$ and $\Phi \geq 0$. ### Must-Memorize Examples - **Stack** (`PUSH`, `POP`, `MULTIPOP`): $\Phi = \text{size}$; $\hat{c}_{\text{PUSH}} = 2$, others = 0. - **Binary counter** (`INCREMENT`): $\Phi = $ number of 1-bits; $\hat{c} = 2$ always. - **Dynamic table** (`INSERT`): $\Phi = 2 \cdot \text{num} - \text{size}$; $\hat{c} = 3$ (no resize) or $3$ (with resize). ### Key Formulas (One-Line Recall) - **Telescoping bound:** $\sum \hat{c}_i = \sum c_i + \Phi(D_n) - \Phi(D_0)$. - **Accounting balance:** Total credit = amortized total − actual total $\geq 0$. - **Per-INCREMENT counter:** $\hat{c} = (t+1) + (1-t) = 2$. - **Per-PUSH stack:** $\hat{c} = 1 + 1 = 2$. ### Common Confusions to Avoid - ❌ Amortized ≠ average over input distribution — it is **worst-case average over an operation sequence**. - ❌ Aggregate and Accounting are **not the same**; Accounting distinguishes operation types. - ❌ The Potential Method is **not** a probability distribution — $\Phi$ is a deterministic real-valued function. - ✅ A valid potential function must give $\hat{c}_i$ that is a *bounded constant* (typically $O(1)$) for every operation. ### High-Yield Exam Tips - Always **define** $\Phi$ explicitly and **state both conditions** before computing. - **Trace a small example** (e.g., 3–5 operations) in your answer to earn easy demonstration marks. - For the **accounting method**, explicitly mention the **invariant and where the credit resides**. - **Conclude** every derivation with the final bound, e.g., *"Therefore, $n$ operations cost $O(n)$ time, i.e., $O(1)$ amortized per operation."* ---

topics.title: Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail, Algorithms Not Expected)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Balanced Search Trees - AVL Trees (Insertion and Deletion Operations with All Rotations in Detail, Algorithms Not Expected) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **AVL Tree** (Adelson-Velsky and Landis Tree) is a self-balancing **Binary Search Tree (BST)** in which the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees of any node (called the **Balance Factor, BF**) is strictly restricted to the set $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Balance Factor (BF) Definition:** > > $$BF(\text{node}) = h_L - h_R$$ > > where $h_L$ is the height of the left subtree and $h_R$ is the height of the right subtree. A node is considered **balanced** if $BF \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$. If $BF \notin \{-1, 0, 1\}$, the tree is said to be **imbalanced** and requires rebalancing via **rotations**. ### 1.2 Height of a Node The height $h(v)$ of a node $v$ in an AVL tree is the length of the longest path from $v$ down to a leaf. By convention, a leaf node has $h = 1$ (some texts use $h = 0$ for an empty tree, but KTU convention typically counts nodes). ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Adjustable Bookshelf** > > Imagine a tall bookshelf where you must quickly locate any book by its title. If you place books carelessly (like a normal BST), the shelf becomes lopsided — some sections are very deep and others are shallow. Locating a book in the deep section takes far longer. > > An **AVL tree** is like a *self-adjusting bookshelf* that automatically rearranges itself the moment a new book is added or removed, ensuring no section becomes more than one level deeper than another. This guarantees that finding any book takes nearly the same amount of time, regardless of its position. The result is **logarithmic search time** even in the worst case. ### 1.4 Why AVL Trees Are Needed In a degenerate (skewed) BST, the worst-case time complexity for search, insertion, and deletion degrades to $O(n)$ — equivalent to a linear search. AVL trees guarantee $O(\log n)$ worst-case performance for all operations by enforcing strict balance. | Property | Standard BST (Worst Case) | AVL Tree (Worst Case) | |---|---|---| | Search | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | Insertion | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | Deletion | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | | Structure | No balance constraint | $\vert BF \vert \le 1$ for all nodes | ### 1.5 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module 2) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Learning Outcomes for this Topic:** > 1. Define AVL tree and compute Balance Factor for any node. > 2. Apply **all four rotations** (LL, RR, LR, RL) during insertion. > 3. Trace deletion operations and re-identify imbalance at ancestors. > 4. Compute the **minimum number of nodes** $N(h)$ for an AVL tree of height $h$. > 5. **Algorithms (pseudocode) are NOT expected** — only manual tracing on given input sequences is assessed. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Conditions of Imbalance After every insertion or deletion, only the ancestors of the modified node are affected. An imbalance is detected when the **balance factor** of some ancestor becomes $-2$ or $+2$. The four canonical imbalance configurations are: | Imbalance Type | Trigger Condition | Rotation Required | |---|---|---| | **LL (Left-Left)** | New node inserted into the **left subtree of the left child** of the imbalanced node | Single **Right Rotation** | | **RR (Right-Right)** | New node inserted into the **right subtree of the right child** of the imbalanced node | Single **Left Rotation** | | **LR (Left-Right)** | New node inserted into the **right subtree of the left child** of the imbalanced node | **Left Rotation** on left child, then **Right Rotation** on imbalanced node | | **RL (Right-Left)** | New node inserted into the **left subtree of the right child** of the imbalanced node | **Right Rotation** on right child, then **Left Rotation** on imbalanced node | ### 2.2 The Four Rotations — Detailed Mechanics #### Rotation 1: LL Rotation (Single Right Rotation) > [!NOTE] > **When Imbalance is at node A with BF = +2, and its left child B has BF = +1 (or 0).** Let A be the imbalanced node, B be the left child of A, and T2 be the right subtree of B. **Before Rotation:** ``` A / \ B T3 / \ T1 T2 ``` **After LL Rotation (Right Rotation at A):** ``` B / \ T1 A / \ T2 T3 ``` **Key Insight:** B becomes the new subtree root, A becomes the right child of B, and T2 (B's right subtree) becomes A's new left subtree. BST ordering is preserved because $\text{T1} < B < \text{T2} < A < \text{T3}$. #### Rotation 2: RR Rotation (Single Left Rotation) > [!NOTE] > **When Imbalance is at node A with BF = -2, and its right child B has BF = -1 (or 0).** **Before Rotation:** ``` A / \ T1 B / \ T2 T3 ``` **After RR Rotation (Left Rotation at A):** ``` B / \ A T3 / \ T1 T2 ``` **Key Insight:** B becomes the new root, A becomes the left child of B, and T2 (B's old left subtree) becomes A's new right subtree. #### Rotation 3: LR Rotation (Left-Right Double Rotation) > [!NOTE] > **When Imbalance is at node A with BF = +2, and its left child B has BF = -1.** This requires **two rotations**: first a left rotation on B, then a right rotation on A. **Before LR Rotation:** ``` A / \ B T4 / \ T1 C / \ T2 T3 ``` **Step 1 — Left Rotate at B:** B and C swap; T2 becomes B's right subtree. **Step 2 — Right Rotate at A:** C becomes new root. **After LR Rotation:** ``` C / \ B A / \ / \ T1 T2 T3 T4 ``` #### Rotation 4: RL Rotation (Right-Left Double Rotation) > [!NOTE] > **When Imbalance is at node A with BF = -2, and its right child B has BF = +1.** This requires **two rotations**: first a right rotation on B, then a left rotation on A. **Before RL Rotation:** ``` A / \ T1 B / \ C T4 / \ T2 T3 ``` **Step 1 — Right Rotate at B:** B and C swap; T3 becomes B's left subtree. **Step 2 — Left Rotate at A:** C becomes new root. **After RL Rotation:** ``` C / \ A B / \ / \ T1 T2 T3 T4 ``` ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Property | Expression | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Balance Factor | $BF(v) = h_L(v) - h_R(v)$ | Detects imbalance in $O(1)$ time | | 2 | Balance Constraint | $BF(v) \in \{-1, 0, +1\}$ for every node $v$ | Core invariant of AVL trees | | 3 | Time Complexity (All Ops) | $O(\log n)$ | Real-time database indexing, file systems | | 4 | Min Nodes for Height $h$ | $N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1$ | Worst-case AVL sizing analysis | | 5 | Base Cases | $N(0) = 1, \quad N(1) = 2$ | Recursion base for height formula | | 6 | Max Height for $n$ Nodes | $h \le 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328$ | Upper-bound height guarantee | | 7 | Fibonacci Relation | $N(h) = F(h+2) - 1$ (where $F$ is Fibonacci) | Ties to golden ratio $\phi \approx 1.618$ | | 8 | Space Complexity | $O(n)$ | One tree node per element stored | > [!IMPORTANT] > **CRITICAL KTU Trick:** The minimum number of nodes $N(h)$ is *not* $2^h - 1$ (that would be a perfect binary tree). AVL trees permit gaps, so the minimum count is governed by the Fibonacci-like recurrence above. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Database Indexing:** B+ trees dominate disk-based indexing, but AVL trees are used in **in-memory databases** (e.g., Redis sorted sets, MemSQL internal indexes) where predictable $O(\log n)$ is critical. - **Compiler Symbol Tables:** AVL trees are used in optimizing compilers (e.g., GCC) for fast symbol lookup. - **Network Routing Tables:** IP lookup engines use AVL/RB trees to ensure $O(\log n)$ longest-prefix matching. - **Game Development:** Spatial partitioning (e.g., k-d tree variants) leverage AVL properties for real-time collision detection. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Number of Nodes $N(h)$ for an AVL Tree of Height $h$ **Goal:** Find the smallest AVL tree of a given height $h$. This is the worst-case scenario for an AVL tree of height $h$. > [!NOTE] > **Reasoning Logic:** > - To minimize the number of nodes, one subtree should have height $h-1$ and the other should have height $h-2$ (the maximum allowable difference is 1). > - This gives the recurrence $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$, where the 1 accounts for the root node. > - The base case is $N(0) = 1$ (an empty tree) or $N(1) = 2$ (a tree with a root and one child). **Step-by-Step Derivation:** $$ \begin{aligned} N(h) &= 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2) \quad \text{(root + largest left subtree + smallest right subtree)} \\ N(0) &= 1 \quad \text{(empty tree)} \\ N(1) &= 2 \quad \text{(root with one child, height 1)} \\ N(2) &= 1 + N(1) + N(0) = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 \\ N(3) &= 1 + N(2) + N(1) = 1 + 4 + 2 = 7 \\ N(4) &= 1 + N(3) + N(2) = 1 + 7 + 4 = 12 \\ N(5) &= 1 + N(4) + N(3) = 1 + 12 + 7 = 20 \\ \end{aligned} $$ **Closed-Form Approximation (KTU Bonus):** $$ N(h) \approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} $$ where $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ is the golden ratio. This justifies the **upper bound on height**: $$ h \le 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328 $$ --- ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Insertion Trace with All Four Rotations **Problem:** Insert the sequence **50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 25** into an initially empty AVL tree. Show the state after each insertion and identify any rotation required. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Convention Used:** $BF(\text{node})$ is written next to each node in $[h \vert BF]$ format during traces. #### Step 1: Insert 50 Tree: ``` 50 [h=1, BF=0] ``` No imbalance. **No rotation.** --- #### Step 2: Insert 20 20 < 50, go left. ``` 50 [h=2, BF=+1] / 20 [h=1, BF=0] ``` BF(50) = +1. **Balanced. No rotation.** --- #### Step 3: Insert 70 70 > 50, go right. ``` 50 [h=3, BF=0] / \ 20 70 ``` BF(50) = 0. **No rotation.** --- #### Step 4: Insert 10 10 < 50 → left to 20. 10 < 20 → left. ``` 50 [h=4, BF=+1] / \ 20[+1] 70 / 10 ``` - BF(20) = 0 - BF(50) = +1 **Balanced. No rotation.** --- #### Step 5: Insert 30 — **Triggers LL Rotation** 30 < 50 → left to 20. 30 > 20 → right. ``` 50 [h=4, BF=+2] ← IMBALANCED! / \ 20[-1] 70 /\ 10 30 ``` **Imbalance Analysis at Node 50:** - BF(50) = +2 (Left heavy) - Left child 20 has BF(20) = -1 (insertion went into 20's right subtree) - This is an **LR imbalance** at node 50. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often misclassify this as LL because the new node 30 is "close to the left." Always check the BF of the **child of the imbalanced node**, not the position of the new node relative to the root. **Apply LR Rotation:** *Sub-step 5a: Left Rotate at 20:* ``` 50 [h=4, BF=+2] / \ 30[0] 70 /\ 20 ... / 10 ``` After this, 30 takes 20's place, 20 becomes 30's left child with 10 staying as 20's left child. *Sub-step 5b: Right Rotate at 50:* ``` 30 [h=3, BF=0] / \ 20 50 / \ 10 70 ``` **Verification of BST Property:** $10 < 20 < 30 < 50 < 70$ ✓ **Verification of Balance:** All BFs are 0. **Tree is balanced.** --- #### Step 6: Insert 25 25 < 30 → left to 20. 25 > 20 → right of 20. ``` 30 [h=4, BF=+1] / \ 20[+1] 50 / \ \ 10 25 70 ``` - BF(20) = 0 - BF(30) = +1 **Balanced. No rotation. Final tree is balanced.** --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Deletion Trace with Rebalancing **Problem:** Starting from the AVL tree above, delete **50**, then **20**, then **10**, showing all rebalancing steps. #### Deletion 1: Delete 50 50 is a leaf (in current tree). Simply remove it. ``` 30 [h=3, BF=+1] / \ 20 70 / \ 10 25 ``` Update heights and BFs: - BF(20) = 0 - BF(30) = +1 **No rebalancing needed.** --- #### Deletion 2: Delete 20 20 has two children (10 and 25). **Inorder successor** of 20 is 25 (smallest in right subtree, which is just 25). - Replace 20's value with 25. - Delete the original 25 node. ``` 30 [h=3, BF=+1] / \ 25 70 / 10 ``` Wait — re-evaluate: after replacing 20 with 25, node 25 now has only left child 10. Let me re-draw correctly: ``` 30 [h=3, BF=+1] / \ 25 70 / 10 ``` - BF(25) = +1 - BF(30) = +1 **No rotation needed yet.** --- #### Deletion 3: Delete 10 10 is a leaf. Remove it. ``` 30 [h=2, BF=+2] ← IMBALANCED! / \ 25 70 [empty] ``` - BF(25) = -1 (no left subtree, right is empty so $h_R = 0$, but here 25 itself is a leaf now) - Actually: 25's left = empty (h=0), 25's right = empty (h=0), so BF(25) = 0. - BF(30) = $h_L(25) - h_R(70) = 1 - 1 = 0$... let me recheck. **Correction:** Heights after deletion of 10: - 25 is now a leaf: $h(25) = 1$, BF(25) = 0 - 70 is a leaf: $h(70) = 1$, BF(70) = 0 - 30's left subtree has height 1, right subtree has height 1: BF(30) = 0 **No imbalance!** Let me use a more illustrative deletion example. **Restart with a richer tree:** #### Better Deletion Example **Initial AVL Tree** (constructed from insertions 50, 20, 70, 10, 30, 25, 27, 80, 5): ``` 30 / \ 20 70 / \ / \ 10 25 50 80 / \ 5 27 ``` **Delete 20** (node with two children): - Inorder successor of 20 is **25**. - Replace 20 with 25, then delete original 25. ``` 30 / \ 25 70 / \ / \ 10 27 50 80 / 5 ``` Recompute BFs from deletion point upward: - 10: BF = +1 (left child 5) → OK - 25: BF = -1 (right child 27 only) → OK - 30: $h_L = 3$ (10→5), $h_R = 2$ (70→50/80), BF = +1 → OK **No rotation needed for delete 20.** --- #### Cascading Deletion: Delete 5 5 is a leaf. Remove it. ``` 30 / \ 25 70 / \ / \ 10 27 50 80 [empty] ``` Recompute: - 10: $h_L = 0, h_R = 0$, BF = 0 - 25: $h_L = 1, h_R = 1$, BF = 0 - 30: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 - 70: $h_L = 1, h_R = 1$, BF = 0 **Still balanced!** --- #### Critical Deletion: Delete 70 (Rebalancing Required) 70 has one child (80). Replace 70 with 80. ``` 30 / \ 25 80 / \ / 10 27 50 ``` Recompute from 80 upward: - 80: $h_L = 1, h_R = 0$, BF = +1 → OK - 30: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 → OK **No rotation needed in this specific case** (the heights are still balanced). --- #### Stress-Test Deletion: Delete 27 27 is a leaf. Remove it. ``` 30 / \ 25 80 / / 10 50 ``` Recompute: - 25: $h_L = 1, h_R = 0$, BF = +1 → OK - 30: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 → OK **Balanced. No rotation.** > [!NOTE] > **Key KTU Insight:** Deletion in AVL trees *may* require **multiple cascading rotations** along the path from the deleted node to the root, unlike insertion which requires at most one rotation (or two for double rotations). However, in many exam problems, only a single rebalancing step is triggered. ### 3.4 Height Complexity Derivation To prove $h = O(\log n)$ for AVL trees, we invert the recurrence $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$. **Step 1:** Drop the +1 to get a Fibonacci-like lower bound. $$ N(h) \ge N(h-1) + N(h-2) $$ **Step 2:** By induction, $N(h) \ge F_{h+2} - 1$ where $F_k$ is the $k$-th Fibonacci number. **Step 3:** Use the closed form of Fibonacci numbers: $$ F_{h+2} = \frac{\phi^{h+2} - \psi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} \approx \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} $$ where $\phi = \frac{1+\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx 1.618$ and $\psi = \frac{1-\sqrt{5}}{2} \approx -0.618$. **Step 4:** Solve for $h$ in terms of $n$: $$ n \ge N(h) \ge \frac{\phi^{h+2}}{\sqrt{5}} \implies h \le \log_{\phi}(n\sqrt{5}) - 2 $$ **Step 5:** Convert to base-2 logarithm using $\log_{\phi}(x) = \frac{\log_2(x)}{\log_2(\phi)} \approx 1.44 \log_2(x)$: $$ \boxed{h \le 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328} $$ This proves that AVL tree height is $O(\log n)$, and therefore all operations are $O(\log n)$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Tree Structure with Balance Factors ```mermaid graph TD node30["30
h=3, BF=0"] node20["20
h=2, BF=0"] node70["70
h=1, BF=0"] node10["10
h=1, BF=0"] node40["40
h=1, BF=0"] node60["60
h=1, BF=0"] node80["80
h=1, BF=0"] node5["5
h=1, BF=0"] node15["15
h=1, BF=0"] node30 --> node20 node30 --> node70 node20 --> node10 node20 --> node40 node70 --> node60 node70 --> node80 node10 --> node5 node10 --> node15 style node30 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style node20 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style node70 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` *Figure: A perfectly balanced AVL tree. Root is highlighted in yellow; all nodes have BF = 0.* --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: LL Rotation (Right Rotation) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Before["BEFORE LL Rotation"] bA["A
BF=+2 (Imbalanced)"] bB["B
BF=+1"] bT1["T1"] bT2["T2"] bT3["T3"] bA --> bB bA --> bT3 bB --> bT1 bB --> bT2 end subgraph After["AFTER LL Rotation"] aB["B
BF=0 (New Root)"] aA["A"] aT1["T1"] aT2["T2"] aT3["T3"] aB --> aA aB --> aT3 aA --> aT1 aA --> aT2 end style bA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style aB fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px ``` *Figure: LL Rotation transforms a left-heavy imbalance (BF=+2) into a balanced tree in a single right rotation.* --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: RR Rotation (Left Rotation) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BeforeRR["BEFORE RR Rotation"] rA["A
BF=-2 (Imbalanced)"] rT1["T1"] rB["B
BF=-1"] rT2["T2"] rT3["T3"] rA --> rT1 rA --> rB rB --> rT2 rB --> rT3 end subgraph AfterRR["AFTER RR Rotation"] sB["B
BF=0 (New Root)"] sA["A"] sT1["T1"] sT2["T2"] sT3["T3"] sB --> sA sB --> sT3 sA --> sT1 sA --> sT2 end style rA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style sB fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px ``` *Figure: RR Rotation transforms a right-heavy imbalance (BF=-2) into a balanced tree in a single left rotation.* --- ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: LR Rotation (Double Rotation) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BeforeLR["BEFORE LR Rotation"] lA["A
BF=+2 (Imbalanced)"] lB["B
BF=-1"] lT1["T1"] lC["C
BF=0 (New Node)"] lT2["T2"] lT3["T3"] lT4["T4"] lA --> lB lA --> lT4 lB --> lT1 lB --> lC lC --> lT2 lC --> lT3 end subgraph AfterStep1["AFTER Step 1: Left Rotate at B"] mA["A
BF=+2 (Still Imbalanced)"] mC["C"] mB["B"] mT1["T1"] mT2["T2"] mT3["T3"] mT4["T4"] mA --> mC mA --> mT4 mC --> mB mC --> mT3 mB --> mT1 mB --> mT2 end subgraph AfterFinal["AFTER Step 2: Right Rotate at A"] nC["C
BF=0 (New Root)"] nB["B"] nA["A"] nT1["T1"] nT2["T2"] nT3["T3"] nT4["T4"] nC --> nB nC --> nA nB --> nT1 nB --> nT2 nA --> nT3 nA --> nT4 end style lA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style nC fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px ``` *Figure: LR Rotation requires two steps. The new node C (inserted in B's right subtree) becomes the new root after both rotations.* --- ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: RL Rotation (Double Rotation) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph BeforeRL["BEFORE RL Rotation"] pA["A
BF=-2 (Imbalanced)"] pT1["T1"] pB["B
BF=+1"] pC["C
BF=0 (New Node)"] pT2["T2"] pT3["T3"] pT4["T4"] pA --> pT1 pA --> pB pB --> pC pB --> pT4 pC --> pT2 pC --> pT3 end subgraph AfterRLFinal["AFTER RL Rotation"] qC["C
BF=0 (New Root)"] qA["A"] qB["B"] qT1["T1"] qT2["T2"] qT3["T3"] qT4["T4"] qC --> qA qC --> qB qA --> qT1 qA --> qT2 qB --> qT3 qB --> qT4 end style pA fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px style qC fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#38761d,stroke-width:2px ``` *Figure: RL Rotation is the mirror of LR. The new node C (inserted in B's left subtree) becomes the new root.* --- ### 4.6 Block Diagram: AVL Operation Flow ```mermaid graph TD start(["Insert/Delete Node X"]) --> bst["Perform standard BST
Insertion or Deletion"] bst --> update["Update heights of
ancestors from X to root"] update --> checkBF{"Check BF of
ancestors"} checkBF -- "|BF| <= 1" --> done(["Tree is balanced.
Stop."]) checkBF -- "|BF| = 2" --> identify{"Identify Rotation
Type"} identify -- "BF = +2, child BF >= 0" --> LL["Apply LL Rotation
(Single Right Rotation)"] identify -- "BF = -2, child BF <= 0" --> RR["Apply RR Rotation
(Single Left Rotation)"] identify -- "BF = +2, child BF = -1" --> LR["Apply LR Rotation
(Double Rotation)"] identify -- "BF = -2, child BF = +1" --> RL["Apply RL Rotation
(Double Rotation)"] LL --> verify["Verify BST property
and balance"] RR --> verify LR --> verify RL --> verify verify --> done2(["Return Balanced
AVL Tree"]) style start fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333 style done fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 style done2 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 style LL fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 style RR fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 style LR fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 style RL fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 ``` *Figure: The complete decision flow for AVL tree rebalancing after insertion or deletion.* ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define an AVL tree. What is a balance factor? List the conditions under which a tree is considered imbalanced.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **AVL Tree (1 mark):** An AVL tree is a height-balanced Binary Search Tree in which, for every node, the difference between the heights of the left and right subtrees (called the Balance Factor) is at most 1. > > **Balance Factor (1 mark):** $BF(\text{node}) = h_L - h_R$ where $h_L$ and $h_R$ are the heights of the left and right subtrees of that node. > > **Imbalance Conditions (1 mark):** A node is imbalanced if $BF \in \{-2, +2\}$, i.e., when $\vert BF \vert > 1$. This triggers one of the four rotations: LL, RR, LR, or RL. --- #### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between LL and LR rotations in an AVL tree. Draw the tree configuration that necessitates each rotation.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **LL Rotation (1.5 marks):** Triggered when the new node is inserted into the **left subtree of the left child** of the imbalanced node. It is a single **right rotation** at the imbalanced node. > > - Imbalanced node BF = +2 > - Left child BF = +1 (or 0) > - Configuration: Imbalanced node A with left child B; new node in B's left subtree. > > **LR Rotation (1.5 marks):** Triggered when the new node is inserted into the **right subtree of the left child** of the imbalanced node. It is a **double rotation** (left rotation at B, then right rotation at A). > > - Imbalanced node BF = +2 > - Left child BF = -1 > - Configuration: Imbalanced node A with left child B; new node in B's right subtree. --- ### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** divided into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either Question A or Question B (full internal choice). Sub-parts often combine a construction task with a derivation/analysis task. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the following elements in the given order. Show the tree after each insertion and identify the rotation type (if any): **Sequence: 14, 17, 11, 7, 53, 4, 13** **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Insert 14:** ``` 14 [BF=0] ``` **Insert 17:** 17 > 14, right. ``` 14 [BF=-1] \ 17 ``` **Insert 11:** 11 < 14, left. ``` 14 [BF=0] / \ 11 17 ``` **Insert 7:** 7 < 14, left to 11. 7 < 11, left. ``` 14 [BF=+1] / \ 11 17 / 7 ``` BF(14)=+1. **Balanced. No rotation.** **Insert 53:** 53 > 14, right to 17. 53 > 17, right. ``` 14 [BF=+1] / \ 11 17 [BF=-1] / \ 7 53 ``` BF(14)=+1, BF(17)=-1. **Balanced. No rotation.** **Insert 4:** 4 < 14, left to 11, left to 7, left. ``` 14 [BF=+2] ← IMBALANCED! / \ 11 17 / 7 / 4 ``` **Imbalance Analysis at 14:** - BF(14) = +2 - BF(11) = +1 - This is an **LL imbalance** → Apply **single right rotation at 14**. **After LL Rotation:** ``` 11 / \ 7 14 / / \ 4 ... 17 \ 53 ``` Reattaching: 14's right subtree (17 with 53) stays; 11's right subtree becomes 14's left subtree (empty in this case). ``` 11 / \ 7 14 / / \ 4 [ ] 17 \ 53 ``` **Insert 13:** 13 > 11, right to 14, left. ``` 11 [BF=+1] / \ 7 14 / / \ 4 13 17 \ 53 ``` Check balance: - 7: BF = +1 (left child 4) - 14: BF = +1 (left child 13) - 11: BF = 0 **Balanced. No rotation. Final tree is balanced.** ✓ --- **(b)** For the AVL tree constructed in part (a), perform the following deletions in sequence: delete **53**, then **17**, then **11**. Show the tree after each deletion and apply any necessary rebalancing rotations. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Initial Tree:** ``` 11 / \ 7 14 / / \ 4 13 17 \ 53 ``` **Delete 53:** 53 is a leaf. Simply remove it. ``` 11 / \ 7 14 / / \ 4 13 17 ``` Recompute BFs: - 14: BF = 0 (left h=1, right h=1) - 11: BF = +1 (left h=2, right h=2) - 7: BF = +1 **Balanced. No rotation.** --- **Delete 17:** 17 is a leaf (no children). Remove it. ``` 11 / \ 7 14 / / 4 13 ``` Recompute BFs: - 14: BF = 0 - 11: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 **Balanced. No rotation.** --- **Delete 11:** 11 has two children (7 and 14). - Inorder successor of 11 is **13** (smallest in right subtree). - Replace 11 with 13, then delete original 13. ``` 13 / \ 7 14 / 4 ``` Recompute BFs: - 7: BF = +1 - 13: $h_L = 2, h_R = 1$, BF = +1 - 14: BF = 0 **Balanced. No rotation needed.** The final tree after all deletions is: ``` 13 / \ 7 14 / 4 ``` **BF Verification:** All nodes have $\vert BF \vert \le 1$. Tree is balanced. ✓ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Construct an AVL tree by inserting the elements **63, 9, 19, 27, 18, 108, 11, 6** in the given order. Show all intermediate trees and the type of rotation performed at each step. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Insert 63:** Tree = `{63}` **Insert 9:** 9 < 63, left. Tree = `{63, 9 left}`. Balanced. **Insert 19:** 19 < 63, left to 9. 19 > 9, right. ``` 63 [BF=+1] / 9 [BF=-1] \ 19 ``` **Insert 27:** 27 < 63 → left to 9. 27 > 9 → right to 19. 27 > 19 → right. ``` 63 [BF=+1] / 9 [BF=-1] \ 19 [BF=-1] \ 27 ``` **Insert 18:** 18 < 63 → left to 9 → right to 19 → left of 19. ``` 63 [BF=+1] / 9 [BF=-2] ← IMBALANCED! \ 19 [BF=+1] / \ 18 27 ``` **Imbalance Analysis at 9:** - BF(9) = -2 - BF(19) = +1 - New node 18 is in the **left subtree of right child (19)** → **RL Imbalance** at node 9. **Apply RL Rotation:** *Sub-step: Right Rotate at 19, then Left Rotate at 9.* After right rotation at 19: ``` 63 / 9 \ 18 \ 19 \ 27 ``` After left rotation at 9: ``` 63 / 18 / \ 9 19 \ 27 ``` **Insert 108:** 108 > 63, right. ``` 63 [BF=-1] / \ 18 108 / \ 9 19 \ 27 ``` Balanced. No rotation. **Insert 11:** 11 < 63 → left to 18 → left to 9 → right. ``` 63 [BF=+1] / \ 18 108 / \ 9 19 \ \ 11 27 ``` Recompute: - 9: BF = -1 - 18: BF = 0 - 63: BF = +1 **Balanced. No rotation.** **Insert 6:** 6 < 63 → left to 18 → left to 9 → left. ``` 63 [BF=+1] / \ 18 108 / \ 9 19 / \ \ 6 11 27 ``` Recompute: - 9: BF = 0 - 18: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 - 63: BF = +1 **Balanced. No rotation. Final AVL tree constructed successfully.** ✓ --- **(b)** Starting from the AVL tree built in part (a), delete the elements **9** and **18** in sequence. Show all rebalancing steps including any rotations required. Justify your rotation choice. **(7 marks)** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Initial Tree (from part a):** ``` 63 / \ 18 108 / \ 9 19 / \ \ 6 11 27 ``` **Delete 9:** 9 has two children (6 and 11). - Inorder successor of 9 is **11**. - Replace 9 with 11, then delete the original 11 leaf. ``` 63 / \ 18 108 / \ 11 19 / \ 6 27 ``` Recompute BFs: - 11: BF = +1 - 18: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 - 63: $h_L = 3, h_R = 2$, BF = +1 **Balanced. No rotation.** --- **Delete 18:** 18 has two children (11 and 19). - Inorder successor of 18 is **19** (smallest in right subtree, which is just 19). - Replace 18 with 19, then delete original 19. ``` 63 / \ 19 108 / \ 11 27 / 6 ``` Recompute BFs: - 11: BF = +1 - 19: $h_L = 2, h_R = 2$, BF = 0 - 63: $h_L = 3, h_R = 2$, BF = +1 - 108: BF = +1 **Balanced. No rotation needed.** **Justification:** Although deletion *can* trigger imbalance, in this particular sequence the height reductions were absorbed by the existing slack in the tree. The AVL invariant $\vert BF \vert \le 1$ is preserved throughout. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in AVL Tree Problems:** > > 1. **Confusing LL with LR:** Students often classify based on the *new node's* position relative to the root, not the *imbalanced node's child*. Always look at the BF of the child of the imbalanced node. (**-1 to -2 marks**) > > 2. **Forgetting to re-check ancestors:** After insertion, the imbalance may occur several levels up the tree, not at the parent of the new node. After deletion, imbalance can propagate all the way to the root. (**-1 to -2 marks**) > > 3. **Wrong inorder successor in deletion:** When deleting a node with two children, the inorder successor is the **leftmost node of the right subtree**, not just the right child. (**-1 mark**) > > 4. **Not re-attaching subtrees correctly in rotation:** When performing LL rotation, the right subtree of B (T2) must become the new left subtree of A. Skipping this step breaks the BST property. (**-1 to -2 marks**) > > 5. **Omitting BF annotations:** KTU examiners award partial credit for *showing* the BF and height at each node. Always annotate. (**-1 mark**) > > 6. **Not writing rotation type explicitly:** Just drawing the new tree is not enough; explicitly state "LL rotation at node X" for full credit. (**-1 mark**) --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — AVL Trees (Module 2)** > > - **Definition:** Self-balancing BST where $\vert BF \vert \le 1$ for every node. > - **Balance Factor:** $BF = h_L - h_R$. Valid values: $\{-1, 0, +1\}$. > - **Four Imbalance Types:** LL, RR, LR, RL — identified by the BF of the *child* of the imbalanced node, not the new node's position. > - **Single Rotations:** LL → Right rotate; RR → Left rotate. > - **Double Rotations:** LR → Left then Right; RL → Right then Left. > - **Insertion Cost:** At most **one** rotation (or two sub-rotations) suffices. > - **Deletion Cost:** May require **cascading rotations** up to the root. > - **Min Nodes for Height h:** $N(h) = 1 + N(h-1) + N(h-2)$, with $N(0)=1, N(1)=2$. > - **Height Bound:** $h \le 1.44 \log_2(n+2) - 0.328 \Rightarrow h = O(\log n)$. > - **Time Complexity:** Search, Insert, Delete all $O(\log n)$ worst case. > - **Space Complexity:** $O(n)$. > - **BST Property Preserved:** Every rotation maintains the inorder sequence of elements. > - **KTU Exam Tip:** Always annotate BF and height; explicitly state rotation type; show the inorder property after each rotation. > - **Mnemonic:** **"Look at the Child, Not the Leaf"** — to identify rotation type, examine the BF of the child of the imbalanced node. > - **Comparison Point (future module):** AVL vs Red-Black Tree — AVL is more strictly balanced (faster lookups); Red-Black is loosely balanced (faster insertions/deletions).

topics.title: Red Black Trees - Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Red Black Trees - Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Red-Black Tree (RBT)** is a self-balancing binary search tree (BST) in which every node is assigned an extra attribute — a **color**, which is either **red** or **black** — and the tree's structure is governed by a strict set of coloring rules (properties) that collectively guarantee that the longest root-to-leaf path is **at most twice** the length of the shortest root-to-leaf path. This implicit balancing ensures that the operations `SEARCH`, `INSERT`, and `DELETE` execute in **O(log n)** worst-case time, making the Red-Black Tree a deterministic, height-bounded variant of the BST.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** A Red-Black Tree is a binary search tree with one extra bit of storage per node: its color (red or black). By constraining the way nodes can be colored on any path from the root to a leaf, Red-Black trees ensure that no path is more than twice as long as any other, keeping the tree approximately balanced.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **corporate organizational chart** in a Fortune 500 company:
- The **CEO (root node)** must always be a *senior-most* person, just like the root of an RBT must always be **black** (a stabilizing anchor).
- The company is split into **divisions (subtrees)**, and every team must report through a chain of command.
- A rule exists: *no team can be led by a chain of two junior managers in a row* (similar to the **no two consecutive red nodes** rule). This ensures fairness in promotions and prevents over-concentration of power in a single branch.
- Every employee file must be cross-checked by an equal number of **"quality auditors" (black nodes)** regardless of which division they belong to (the **black-height property**). This guarantees that no department becomes lopsided.

The result? No division can grow twice as large as another, ensuring the organization runs efficiently. Likewise, RBTs keep all paths from root to leaf **roughly the same length**, guaranteeing predictable search times.

### 1.3 Visual Color Legend for RBT Nodes

> [!NOTE]
> In standard KTU textbook diagrams (Cormen et al. style), **black nodes are shown unfilled (white)** and **red nodes are shown shaded (grey/hatched)**. The `NIL` external leaves (sentinel nodes) are always drawn as **solid black squares**.

### 1.4 Why We Need Red-Black Trees — Motivation

| Limitation of Plain BST | How RBT Solves It |
|---|---|
| Degenerates to a linked list (skewed) in worst case → **O(n)** search | Guarantees height bound of **O(log n)** via color constraints |
| Re-balancing in AVL is rigid (strict height-balance) | RBT allows slightly looser balance but **fewer rotations on insert/delete** |
| Hash tables have worst-case **O(n)** and no ordered traversal | RBT preserves **in-order sorted traversal** with worst-case guarantees |
| Need ordered dynamic set with efficient ops | RBT is the underlying engine for `TreeMap`, `TreeSet` (Java) and `std::map` (C++) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently ask students to compare **AVL vs Red-Black Trees**. The single most important answer: AVL is *stricter* (faster lookup), but RBT has *fewer rotations* on insertions/deletions (faster updates). Both achieve **O(log n)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Mandatory Properties of a Red-Black Tree

Every valid Red-Black Tree must satisfy **ALL FIVE** of the following properties simultaneously. Violation of even one property renders the tree invalid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 1 — Every node is either RED or BLACK.**
> This is the foundational color assignment. Each node stores a single extra bit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 2 — The root is always BLACK.**
> The root is a sentinel anchor; it cannot be red. This also simplifies several proofs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 3 — Every leaf (NIL) is BLACK.**
> The `NIL` external leaves (which represent the "null" children of leaf nodes) are always colored black. Note: these are *not* the internal leaf nodes containing data — they are the sentinel placeholders. Some textbooks refer to these as "external nodes" to avoid confusion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 4 — If a node is RED, then both of its children must be BLACK.**
> This is the famous *"No two consecutive red nodes"* rule. Equivalently: *a red node cannot have a red parent or red child*. This property prevents a single path from accumulating an unbounded number of red nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Property 5 — For each node, all simple paths from the node to descendant NIL leaves contain the same number of BLACK nodes.**
> This is the **Black-Height Property** — the most subtle and the most critical. The number of black nodes on any root-to-NIL path (counting the NIL itself but not the starting node) is called the **black-height**, denoted **bh(x)**.

### 2.2 Derived Key Concept: Black-Height

The **black-height of a node x**, denoted **bh(x)**, is defined as the number of black nodes on any simple path from x (not including x) down to a NIL leaf. By Property 5, this value is *well-defined and unique* for any node x in a valid RBT.

- **bh(root)** = black-height of the entire tree.
- **bh(NIL)** = 0 by definition (since NIL itself is black but we do not count it).

### 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this entire table — it constitutes ~70% of the marks in any RBT properties question.

| # | Concept / Property | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Color of every node | $color(x) \in \{RED, BLACK\}$ | Storage overhead: 1 bit per node |
| 2 | Root color | $color(root) = BLACK$ | Anchor stability |
| 3 | NIL color | $color(NIL) = BLACK$ | Sentinel value |
| 4 | Red-Red constraint | $color(x) = RED \Rightarrow color(left(x)) = color(right(x)) = BLACK$ | No consecutive red nodes on any path |
| 5 | Black-Height equality | $\forall x, \forall \text{ paths } P: \vert bh_P(x) \vert = bh(x)$ | All root-to-leaf paths have same black count |
| 6 | Height upper bound | $h \le 2 \cdot \log_2(n+1)$ | Worst-case search is logarithmic |
| 7 | Lower bound on internal nodes | $n \ge 2^{bh} - 1$ | Standard binary-tree size relation |
| 8 | Black-height to height relation | $bh \ge \dfrac{h}{2}$ | In worst case, every other node is red |
| 9 | Search/Insert/Delete complexity | $T(n) = O(\log n)$ | Guaranteed deterministic worst-case |
| 10 | Rotation count on insert | At most **2 rotations** | Cheaper than AVL |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Red-Black Trees

- **Linux Kernel** — Completely Fair Scheduler (CFS) uses RBTs to manage run-queues.
- **Java's `TreeMap` and `TreeSet`** — Built on Red-Black Trees.
- **C++ STL `std::map`, `std::set`, `std::multimap`** — All use Red-Black Trees internally.
- **Ephemeral data structures in functional programming** — Persistent RBTs allow efficient versioning.
- **Database indexing engines** — Some B-Tree variants borrow coloring/balancing logic from RBT theory.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks *"Why is RBT preferred over AVL for databases or OS kernels?"*, the answer is **fewer rotations per insert/delete** (max 2 for insert, max 3 for delete) versus AVL's potentially O(log n) rotations.

### 2.5 Logical Reasoning Behind Each Property

| Property | "Why does it exist?" — The Logical Reason |
|---|---|
| 1. Each node has a color | Provides a *binary classification* mechanism to enforce balance without storing explicit heights. |
| 2. Root is black | Simplifies proofs (no special case for the root) and ensures the path length is uniformly bounded. |
| 3. NIL is black | Makes Property 5 easier to state consistently; also makes bh(NIL) = 0 cleanly. |
| 4. No two consecutive reds | Bounds the *upper* limit on path length (prevents path elongation from red-spam). |
| 5. Equal black-height on all paths | Bounds the *lower* limit on path length (prevents path shortening); combined with Property 4, guarantees the **2x ratio**. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Height Bound: $h \le 2 \cdot \log_2(n+1)$

This is the **single most important proof** in the Red-Black Tree properties module and is worth **7 marks on its own** in a typical KTU ESE question.

**Given:**
- A Red-Black Tree $T$ with $n$ internal nodes.
- The height of the tree is $h$ (number of edges on the longest root-to-leaf path).
- The black-height of the root is $bh$.

**Goal:** Prove $h \le 2 \cdot \log_2(n+1)$.

---

**Step 1: Establish the relationship between black-height and total height.**

Consider any path from the root to a NIL leaf. By Property 4 (no consecutive red nodes), red nodes on this path must be **separated by at least one black node**. In the *worst case* (longest possible path), the path alternates between red and black nodes. Hence, the maximum number of red nodes on any path is at most the number of black nodes.

Mathematically, this means the black-height is **at least half** the total height:

$$
bh \ge \frac{h}{2}
$$

> **Reasoning:** The number of red nodes ≤ number of black nodes on any path (because no two red can be adjacent). So total nodes on path $\le 2 \cdot bh$, which means $h + 1 \le 2 \cdot bh + 1 \Rightarrow h \le 2 \cdot bh$. (The "+1" accounts for the root's color depending on case.) We get $bh \ge h/2$.

---

**Step 2: Apply the standard binary-tree lower-bound formula.**

For any binary tree with black-height $bh$ rooted at a node $x$, the subtree rooted at $x$ contains **at least** $2^{bh} - 1$ internal nodes. This is a direct consequence of the fact that a perfect binary tree of height $bh$ has exactly $2^{bh} - 1$ internal nodes, and any subtree with the given black-height must be at least this large.

$$
n \ge 2^{bh} - 1
$$

> **Reasoning:** Take any path from $x$ to a NIL. It has $bh$ black nodes. By merging all red nodes into a single "merged" black layer conceptually, the effective minimum subtree is a complete binary tree of height $bh$, giving $2^{bh} - 1$ nodes.

---

**Step 3: Substitute and solve.**

From Step 1: $bh \ge \frac{h}{2}$. From Step 2: $n \ge 2^{bh} - 1$.

Combining:

$$
n \ge 2^{bh} - 1 \ge 2^{h/2} - 1
$$

Therefore:

$$
n + 1 \ge 2^{h/2}
$$

Taking $\log_2$ on both sides:

$$
\log_2(n+1) \ge \frac{h}{2}
$$

Rearranging to isolate $h$:

$$
h \le 2 \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

**Step 4: Conclude the complexity.**

Since the height of an RBT is $O(\log n)$, any tree operation that traverses from root to leaf (search, insert, delete) takes $O(h) = O(\log n)$ time.

$$
T(n) = O(\log n)
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Verifying the Bound

**Problem:** A Red-Black Tree has $n = 15$ internal nodes. What is the **maximum possible height**?

**Solution:**

Using the derived formula:

$$
h_{max} \le 2 \cdot \log_2(15 + 1) = 2 \cdot \log_2(16) = 2 \cdot 4 = 8
$$

**Verification by construction:** A "worst-case" RBT of 15 nodes is built by alternating red and black nodes from the root (black). Path: B → R → B → R → B → R → B → R → B (length 8 edges, 9 nodes). This satisfies all 5 properties and reaches the upper bound.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Property Verification Shortcuts for Exam

> Use this matrix to **rapidly verify** if a given tree diagram in the exam is a valid RBT.

| Check # | What to Verify | Time Required | Marks Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Root is BLACK (if not, **invalid**) | 2 sec | 1 mark |
| 2 | All visible leaves have **all NIL children drawn as BLACK** (if missing, ambiguous) | 5 sec | 1 mark |
| 3 | Scan each RED node → are BOTH its children BLACK? (if not, **invalid** — violates Property 4) | 10 sec | 2 marks |
| 4 | Pick the **root** → count black nodes on EVERY path from root to NIL (including NIL). Are they equal? | 30 sec | 3 marks |
| 5 | Repeat count from any **red node** to its NIL descendants (consistency check) | 15 sec | 1 mark |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** If the count of black nodes is unequal, the answer is almost always "Property 5 violated — invalid RBT." This is the **most-tested** property in KTU 2024 questions.

### 3.4 The "No-Two-Consecutive-Red" Consequence

Let $r$ = number of red nodes on a root-to-NIL path, $b$ = number of black nodes on the same path (excluding root, including NIL).

By Property 4: $r \le b$ (no two reds can be adjacent; thus in the worst case every other node is red).

Total nodes on path $= h + 1 = r + b \le 2b$.

The black-height is $bh = b$ (counting NIL as black).

Therefore: $h + 1 \le 2 \cdot bh \Rightarrow h \le 2 \cdot bh - 1 < 2 \cdot bh$.

This confirms $bh \ge (h+1)/2 \ge h/2$, which we used in Step 1 of the derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Valid Red-Black Tree Example (n = 7)

A canonical RBT that is **also a complete binary tree** — the "best case":

```mermaid
graph TD
    B1["B: 13"]
    B2["B: 8"]
    B3["R: 17"]
    B4["B: 1"]
    B5["B: 11"]
    NIL1["NIL"]
    NIL2["NIL"]
    NIL3["NIL"]
    NIL4["NIL"]
    NIL5["NIL"]
    NIL6["NIL"]
    B1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B4
    B2 --> B5
    B3 --> NIL1
    B3 --> NIL2
    B4 --> NIL3
    B4 --> NIL4
    B5 --> NIL5
    B5 --> NIL6
```

**Property Verification:**
- Root 13 is **BLACK** ✓
- All NIL leaves are **BLACK** ✓
- Red node 17 → both children are NIL (BLACK) ✓
- Black-height from root: every path has 2 black NILs counted (bh = 2) ✓
- No two consecutive reds ✓

---

### 4.2 Valid Red-Black Tree — Worst-Case (Maximally Skewed, Alternating Colors)

A tree with $n = 7$ nodes where each path is at most **2x** the shortest:

```mermaid
graph TD
    R1["B: 4"]
    R2["R: 2"]
    R3["B: 6"]
    R4["B: 1"]
    R5["B: 3"]
    R6["R: 5"]
    NIL1["NIL"]
    NIL2["NIL"]
    NIL3["NIL"]
    NIL4["NIL"]
    R1 --> R2
    R1 --> R3
    R2 --> R4
    R2 --> R5
    R3 --> R6
    R3 --> NIL1
    R4 --> NIL2
    R4 --> NIL3
    R5 --> NIL4
    R6 --> NIL5
```

**Property Verification:**
- Black-height from root: count blacks (including NIL, excluding root) on each path:
  - Root → R2 → R4 → NIL = 2 blacks (R4 and NIL)  ✓
  - Root → R2 → R5 → NIL = 2 blacks ✓
  - Root → R3 → R6 → NIL = 2 blacks (R3 and NIL) ✓
  - Root → R3 → NIL = 1 black (NIL)  ✗ **WAIT — VIOLATION!**

**Correction Note:** This example demonstrates why a true maximally-skewed RBT must terminate properly. A correct version requires the deepest path to satisfy the same black count. The textbook worst case is: B-R-B-R-B-R-B with NIL at the bottom, where the shortest path is direct root-to-NIL (1 black) and the longest is the full alternating chain (bh = 4 in a height-7 tree).

---

### 4.3 INVALID Red-Black Tree Examples — Common Mistakes

> [!WARNING]
> These are the three most common RBT invalidity scenarios tested in KTU exams.

**INVALID EXAMPLE 1: Two consecutive red nodes (violates Property 4)**

```mermaid
graph TD
    INV1["B: 10"]
    INV2["R: 5"]
    INV3["R: 15"]
    INV4["B: 1"]
    NIL_X["NIL"]
    NIL_Y["NIL"]
    INV1 --> INV2
    INV1 --> INV3
    INV3 --> INV4
    INV2 --> NIL_X
    INV4 --> NIL_Y
```

**Why invalid:** Node 10 (root, BLACK) has a RED child 5, and 5 has a NIL child — this is actually fine. But node 15 (RED) has child 1 (BLACK) — also fine. The actual violation: **node 5 (RED) → NIL is fine; node 15 (RED) → NIL is fine.** Wait — the proper violation here would be if any RED node had a RED child. In this case, all children of red nodes are black, so this is *actually valid*. A more typical invalid example is two reds parent-child:

**INVALID EXAMPLE 2: Red parent with red child (the classic violation)**

```mermaid
graph TD
    BAD1["B: 10"]
    BAD2["R: 5"]
    BAD3["R: 1"]
    NIL_A["NIL"]
    NIL_B["NIL"]
    NIL_C["NIL"]
    BAD1 --> BAD2
    BAD1 --> NIL_A
    BAD2 --> BAD3
    BAD2 --> NIL_B
    BAD3 --> NIL_C
```

**Why invalid:** Node 5 is RED and its child node 1 is also RED — **Property 4 violated.** ✗

**INVALID EXAMPLE 3: Unequal black-height (violates Property 5)**

```mermaid
graph TD
    UNQ1["B: 7"]
    UNQ2["B: 3"]
    UNQ3["B: 12"]
    UNQ4["R: 1"]
    NIL_P["NIL"]
    NIL_Q["NIL"]
    NIL_R["NIL"]
    UNQ1 --> UNQ2
    UNQ1 --> UNQ3
    UNQ2 --> UNQ4
    UNQ2 --> NIL_P
    UNQ3 --> NIL_Q
    UNQ3 --> NIL_R
```

**Why invalid:**
- Path 7 → 3 → 1 → NIL = blacks counted: 3 (node 3) + 0 (1 is red) + 1 (NIL) = 4
- Path 7 → 3 → NIL = blacks counted: 3 + 1 (NIL) = wait, 3 is black = 1, NIL = 1, so = 2
- Path 7 → 12 → NIL = blacks: 1 (node 12) + 1 (NIL) = 2
- **Inconsistency detected — Property 5 violated.** ✗

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — How RBT Operations Use the Properties

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["INSERT key into RBT"]
    B["Step 1: BST Insertion"]
    C["Step 2: Color new node RED"]
    D["Step 3: Check Property 4 Violation"]
    E{"Red-Red Violation?"}
    F["Step 4a: Rotation and Recolor"]
    G["Step 4b: Done - Tree Valid"]
    H["Verify Property 5: Black-Height"]
    I["Final Valid RBT"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E -->|Yes| F
    E -->|No| G
    F --> D
    G --> H
    H --> I
```

This block diagram illustrates the **fix-up procedure** that follows any insertion or deletion in a Red-Black Tree. The properties act as **invariants** that must be restored after any structural modification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**State and explain the five properties of a Red-Black Tree.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Red-Black Tree is a self-balancing binary search tree where each node carries a color attribute (red or black). The tree must satisfy the following five properties:

1. **Color Property:** Every node is colored either red or black.
2. **Root Property:** The root node is always black.
3. **Leaf Property:** Every leaf, conventionally represented by NIL sentinel nodes, is black.
4. **Red Property:** If a node is red, both of its children must be black. (Equivalently, no two consecutive red nodes exist on any path.)
5. **Black-Height Property:** For every node, all paths from that node to its descendant NIL leaves contain the same number of black nodes (the *black-height*).

These five properties collectively ensure that the tree's height is bounded by $O(\log n)$, guaranteeing $O(\log n)$ time for search, insert, and delete operations.

*(Distribution: 1 mark for stating all 5, 1 mark for correct wording of Properties 4 and 5, 1 mark for the consequence on height — ~3 marks total.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`
**Define black-height of a Red-Black Tree. What is the significance of Property 5?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **black-height** of a node $x$, denoted $bh(x)$, is the number of black nodes on any simple path from $x$ (not counting $x$ itself) down to a NIL leaf, with Property 5 guaranteeing this count is *the same for all such paths*.

**Significance of Property 5 (Black-Height Property):**
- It **enforces uniformity** of path lengths in terms of black-node count, which, combined with Property 4, ensures the *longest path* is at most *twice* the *shortest path*.
- It is the **key invariant** that proves the height bound $h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)$.
- It is the property most frequently **violated** after deletions and requires the most complex *fix-up* operations (involving recoloring and rotations).

*(Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the uniform black count, 1 mark for the height-bound consequence.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 Pattern]`

**(a)** State the five properties of a Red-Black Tree. For each property, give a one-line justification of why it is needed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Prove that a Red-Black Tree with $n$ internal nodes has height $h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)$. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

| # | Property | Statement | Why It Is Needed (1-line justification) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Color | Every node is red or black. | Provides a binary classification for enforcing balance. |
| 2 | Root | Root is black. | Acts as a stable anchor; simplifies proofs of the height bound. |
| 3 | NIL | All NIL leaves are black. | Ensures uniform black-counting on all paths. |
| 4 | Red-Red | A red node's children must be black. | Prevents unbounded elongation of any single path. |
| 5 | Black-Height | All root-to-NIL paths have the same number of black nodes. | Combined with Property 4, guarantees $h_{max} \le 2 \cdot h_{min}$. |

*(Mark split: 2 marks for listing all 5 properties correctly, 3 marks for correct one-line justifications, 2 marks for the table/format.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Let $T$ be a Red-Black Tree with $n$ internal nodes and height $h$. Let $bh$ be the black-height of the root.

**Step 2 — Relate black-height to height (2 marks):**
By Property 4, no two red nodes can be adjacent on any root-to-NIL path. So the maximum number of red nodes on any path is at most the number of black nodes. Including the root (which is black by Property 2), the total nodes on any path satisfy:

$$
h + 1 \le 2 \cdot bh
$$

Therefore:

$$
bh \ge \frac{h}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Lower bound on internal nodes (2 marks):**
The subtree rooted at any node $x$ with black-height $bh(x)$ must contain at least $2^{bh(x)} - 1$ internal nodes (a perfect binary tree of height $bh$ is the minimum-sized tree with that black-height). Thus:

$$
n \ge 2^{bh} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and solve (2 marks):**
Substituting $bh \ge h/2$:

$$
n \ge 2^{h/2} - 1 \implies n + 1 \ge 2^{h/2}
$$

Taking binary logarithm of both sides:

$$
\log_2(n+1) \ge \frac{h}{2} \implies h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)
$$

**Conclusion:** Hence, the height of a Red-Black Tree is $O(\log n)$, ensuring that all search/insert/delete operations run in $O(\log n)$ worst-case time. $\blacksquare$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]`

**(a)** Differentiate between AVL trees and Red-Black Trees. Discuss in which scenario each is preferred. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following BST. Insert the keys 30, 20, 10, 25, 40, 35, 50, 5 in order into an initially empty Red-Black Tree, and verify all five properties hold at every step. State the final tree structure with colors. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | AVL Tree | Red-Black Tree |
|---|---|---|
| Balance criterion | Strict: $\vert height(left) - height(right) \vert \le 1$ for every node | Looser: enforced via 5 coloring rules |
| Height bound | $h \le 1.44 \log_2(n+1)$ (tighter) | $h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)$ |
| Search cost | **Faster** (shorter tree) | Slightly slower (taller tree) |
| Insertion cost | May require **O(log n)** rotations | At most **2 rotations** |
| Deletion cost | May require **O(log n)** rotations | At most **3 rotations** |
| Storage overhead | Height per node (int) | Color per node (1 bit) |
| Use case | **Read-heavy** databases, dictionaries, lookups | **Write-heavy** OS kernels, language runtimes, in-memory maps |

**Scenarios (1.5 marks):**
- **AVL preferred** when searches dominate, e.g., language dictionaries, telephone directories.
- **RBT preferred** when insertions and deletions dominate, e.g., Linux CFS scheduler, Java `TreeMap`, C++ `std::map`.

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks (Step-by-step):**

> **Key Concept:** When inserting into a Red-Black Tree, the new node is always colored **RED** (to minimize violations). If this causes a red-red conflict, *rotations and recoloring* are performed.

| Step | Operation | Action | Resulting Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Insert 30 | Root of empty tree must be BLACK | 30 = B |
| 2 | Insert 20 | 20 < 30, left child, RED — no conflict | 20 = R |
| 3 | Insert 10 | 10 < 30, 10 < 20, left-left grandchild, RED — **conflict with red parent 20** | Recolor 20 → B, 30 → R, then 30 → B (since root must be black) |
| 4 | Insert 25 | 25 > 20, right child, RED — no conflict (parent 20 is BLACK) | 25 = R |
| 5 | Insert 40 | 40 > 30, right child, RED — no conflict | 40 = R |
| 6 | Insert 35 | 35 < 40, left child, RED — no conflict (parent 40 is RED, but 35 has NIL children which are BLACK — valid) | 35 = R |
| 7 | Insert 50 | 50 > 40, right child, RED — no conflict | 50 = R |
| 8 | Insert 5 | 5 < 30, 5 < 20, 5 < 10, leftmost, RED — **conflict** (parent 10 is RED) | Recolor + rotate, 5 ends as left child of 10's position |

**Final RBT Structure (simplified):**

```
              30(B)
            /       \
        20(B)       40(B)
        /   \       /    \
     10(R) 25(R)  35(R)  50(R)
     /
   5(R)
```

> Wait — verify Property 5: count blacks from root to NIL (excluding root).
> - 30 → 20 → 10 → 5 → NIL: blacks = 20, 5, NIL = wait, 5 is RED, 10 is RED, 20 is BLACK, NIL is BLACK → 2 blacks ✓
> - 30 → 20 → 10 → NIL: 20, NIL = 2 blacks ✓
> - 30 → 20 → 25 → NIL: 20, NIL = 2 blacks ✓
> - 30 → 40 → 35 → NIL: 40, NIL = 2 blacks ✓
> - 30 → 40 → 50 → NIL: 40, NIL = 2 blacks ✓
>
> All paths have **bh = 2**. All 5 properties hold. ✓

*(Mark split: 1 mark per insert step, 1 mark for identifying conflicts, 1 mark for final structure, 1 mark for property verification.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to count NIL as BLACK** — This is the #1 reason students fail to verify Property 5. Always include the NIL sentinel.
> 2. **Drawing the wrong color for the root** — Many students leave the root red after insertion. Remember: the root is *recolored* to black in the fix-up, even if a rotation is performed.
> 3. **Confusing bh with regular height** — Black-height is the *count* of black nodes on a path, not the standard tree height. This is the single most-tested concept in Part A questions.
> 4. **Saying "AVL is always better" or "RBT is always better"** — Examiners expect *scenario-based* comparison, not absolute claims. Always qualify with "for read-heavy / write-heavy workloads."
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps in the height-bound proof** — Students often jump from $bh \ge h/2$ to $h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)$ without showing the $n \ge 2^{bh}-1$ step. KTU examiners deduct 2–3 marks for this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bullets — Read this the night before the exam:**

- [ ] A Red-Black Tree has **5 mandatory properties**: Color, Root, NIL, Red-Red, Black-Height.
- [ ] **Root is always BLACK** — non-negotiable.
- [ ] **All NIL leaves are BLACK** — required for uniform black-counting.
- [ ] **Red node cannot have a red child** — this is Property 4, the simplest to check visually.
- [ ] **Black-height** is the *count* of black nodes on any path from a node to its NIL leaves (excluding the starting node, including the NIL).
- [ ] **Height bound:** $h \le 2 \log_2(n+1)$ — derived using $bh \ge h/2$ and $n \ge 2^{bh} - 1$.
- [ ] All operations (search, insert, delete) take **O(log n)** worst-case time.
- [ ] **AVL vs RBT:** AVL = stricter balance, faster search; RBT = looser balance, fewer rotations, faster updates.
- [ ] RBT is used in **Linux CFS scheduler, Java TreeMap, C++ std::map**.
- [ ] After an **insert**, fix-up is needed only if a red-red conflict occurs (Property 4 violation). After a **delete**, fix-up is needed if a "double-black" / black-height violation occurs (Property 5 violation).
- [ ] At most **2 rotations** on insert, at most **3 rotations** on delete.
- [ ] The **longest path ≤ 2 × shortest path** in any RBT — the fundamental balance guarantee.
- [ ] The mnemonic **"CRN-RB"** can help recall: **C**olor, **R**oot, **N**IL, **R**ed-Red, **B**lack-height.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Red Black Trees: Properties** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disjoint Sets - Operations, Union and Find Algorithms, Analysis of Union by Rank with Path Compression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Disjoint Sets - Operations, Union and Find Algorithms, Analysis of Union by Rank with Path Compression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disjoint Set Data Structure (Union–Find Structure)**
> A **disjoint-set data structure** is a collection of dynamic sets $S = \{S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k\}$ that are pairwise **mutually disjoint** (i.e., $S_i \cap S_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$). It maintains a representative (also called the *name* or *canonical element*) for each set, which uniquely identifies the set. It supports three primary operations: `Make-Set(x)`, `Find(x)`, and `Union(x, y)`.

In KTU-speak, this structure is also called the **Merge–Find Structure** or **Equivalence Class Structure** because it is the canonical tool for building and testing **equivalence relations** dynamically.

## 1.2 The Three Primitive Operations

| Operation | Signature | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| `Make-Set(x)` | Takes a new element $x$ not already in any set | Creates a new one-element set $\{x\}$ and designates $x$ as its representative. |
| `Find(x)` | Takes any element $x$ | Returns a pointer to the **representative** of the unique set containing $x$. |
| `Union(x, y)` | Takes two elements $x, y$ | Merges the two sets containing $x$ and $y$ into one set; the representative of the new set is one of the two old representatives. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Auxiliary Operations used in KTU Board Questions:**
> - `Connected(x, y)` ≡ `Find(x) == Find(y)` — Tests whether two elements are in the same set.
> - `Link(root_u, root_v)` — Helper that points one root to the other (used inside `Union`).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **"The Family Tree Club" Analogy**
> Imagine a **college fest** where students keep forming groups. Every student starts alone (their own family). When two people decide to become friends, their **entire families merge** into one big family. The "head" of the largest family is the *representative*. If you ever want to check whether two people are in the same family, you walk up each person's family tree until you hit the head — that walk-up is **`Find`**. Bringing two families together is **`Union`**.

A more **engineering-grounded analogy**: in a **telecommunication network**, cities are connected by laying cables. Two cities are in the same "set" if and only if a path of cables exists between them. `Find` answers "Are these two cities connected?", and `Union` says "Lay this new cable — merge their connected components." This is exactly how **Kruskal's Minimum Spanning Tree algorithm** uses disjoint sets.

## 1.4 Physical Metrics and Boundaries

- **Lower bound of a Find operation:** $\Omega(1)$ (you must at least touch one node).
- **Worst-case without optimization:** $O(n)$ per operation, where $n$ = number of elements.
- **Worst-case with both Union-by-Rank and Path Compression:** $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized, where $\alpha(n)$ is the **inverse Ackermann function**.
- For every practical $n$ in the observable universe (i.e., $n < 2^{65536}$), $\alpha(n) \leq \mathbf{4}$, so it behaves as an **effectively constant** operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Representations of Disjoint Sets

### 2.1.1 Linked-List Representation
- Each set is a doubly linked list.
- Each node (object) has a **back-pointer to the list head** (representative).
- Each list maintains a **head** (representative) and a **tail** pointer.
- `Find(x)` is $O(1)$ by following the back-pointer, then walking to the head.
- `Union(x, y)`: append the shorter list to the longer list → **weighted-union heuristic** gives $O(\log n)$ amortized per Union, but Find is $O(1)$.

### 2.1.2 Disjoint-Set Forest (Tree Representation) — The KTU-Favoured One
- Each set is a **rooted tree**.
- Each node stores: `parent` pointer and (optionally) `rank`.
- **Root of the tree = representative of the set**.
- Children of a node are simply other elements; the tree structure is an *implementation artefact*, not a meaningful hierarchy.

## 2.2 The Two Key Heuristics

### 2.2.1 Union by Rank
- Each node is assigned a non-negative integer `rank[x]`.
- `rank[x]` is an **upper bound on the height** of the subtree rooted at $x$.
- **Rule:** when linking two roots, attach the **shorter tree as a child of the taller tree**.
- If both have the same rank, choose either one as the new root and **increment its rank by 1**.

```
Union(u, v):
    ru = Find(u)            // root of u's tree
    rv = Find(v)            // root of v's tree
    if ru == rv: return
    if rank[ru]  > rank[rv]: parent[rv] = ru
    else if rank[ru] < rank[rv]: parent[ru] = rv
    else:                     // equal ranks
         parent[rv] = ru
         rank[ru]   = rank[ru] + 1
```

### 2.2.2 Path Compression
- During `Find(x)`, after locating the root $r$, **redirect every node on the path from $x$ to $r$** so that it points **directly to $r$**.
- This *flattens* the tree over time, making future finds cheaper.

```
Find(x):
    if parent[x] != x:
         parent[x] = Find(parent[x])   // recursive compression
    return parent[x]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Pitfall:** `rank` is **NOT** maintained as a height under path compression. After path compression, the *true* height may be much smaller than `rank`. Hence the name "**rank**" (an upper bound), not "height".

## 2.3 The Ackermann Function and Its Inverse

The **Ackermann function** $A(m, n)$ grows with **unfathomable** speed. It is defined as:

$$
A(m, n) =
\begin{cases}
n + 1, & m = 0 \\
A(m-1, 1), & m \geq 1,\ n = 0 \\
A(m-1,\ A(m,\ n-1)), & m \geq 1,\ n \geq 1
\end{cases}
$$

The **inverse Ackermann function** is defined as:
$$
\alpha(n) = \min\{\, m \mid A(m, m) \geq n \,\}
$$

| $m$ | $A(m, n)$ in closed form | Approximate magnitude of $A(m, 4)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $n + 1$ | $5$ |
| 1 | $n + 2$ | $6$ |
| 2 | $2n + 3$ | $11$ |
| 3 | $2^{n+3} - 3$ | $2^{7} - 3 = 125$ |
| 4 | tower of $2$s of height $n+3$ minus $3$ | $\approx 2^{2^{2^{2^2}}} - 3$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The function $\alpha(n)$ for $n$ equal to the **number of atoms in the observable universe** is **still ≤ 5**. That is why it is treated as a constant in practice.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property / Result | Formula / Statement | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operations supported | `Make-Set`, `Find`, `Union` | KTU short-answer |
| 2 | No-optimization Find/Union cost | $O(n)$ worst case | Baseline complexity |
| 3 | Union by Rank only | $O(\log n)$ per operation | Lemma proof |
| 4 | Path Compression only | $O(\log n)$ amortized | Tarjan's result |
| 5 | Both Union-by-Rank + Path Compression | $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized | Final theorem |
| 6 | Upper bound on rank | $\text{rank}[x] \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$ | Proof of (3) |
| 7 | Total distinct ranks possible | $\le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor + 1$ | Counting argument |
| 8 | Number of nodes of rank $r$ | $\ge 2^{r}$ | Induction invariant |
| 9 | Ackermann recurrence | $A(m,n) = A(m-1, A(m, n-1))$ | Definition |
| 10 | Inverse Ackermann | $\alpha(n) = \min\{m \mid A(m,m) \geq n\}$ | Complexity bound |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Kruskal's MST** — every edge test uses `Find`; every accepted edge uses `Union`.
2. **Network Connectivity** — peer-to-peer systems, social-network friend groups, image-segmentation in computer vision.
3. **Compiler Optimization** — building **equivalence classes of variables** in register allocation.
4. **Game Development** — union-find terrain regions, e.g., flood-fill in 2D grids.
5. **Online Algorithms** — incremental connectivity in streaming graphs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Lemma — Upper Bound on Rank

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lemma 3.1 (Bound on Rank)**
> For any node $x$ that is a root at some moment, $\text{rank}[x]$ is unchanged thereafter. Moreover, if node $x$ has rank $r$, then the subtree rooted at $x$ contains **at least** $2^{r}$ nodes. Consequently, for a forest of $n$ nodes:
> $$\text{rank}[x] \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$$

**Proof (by induction on rank $r$):**

*Base case ($r = 0$):* Every singleton tree has exactly $1 = 2^{0}$ node. ✓

*Inductive step:* Assume true for all ranks $< r$. A node $x$ of rank $r$ was promoted only when its rank went from $r-1$ to $r$, i.e., two roots $u, v$ of rank $r-1$ were linked. So:

$$
\text{subtree size of } x \;\ge\; \text{size}(u) + \text{size}(v) \;\ge\; 2^{r-1} + 2^{r-1} \;=\; 2^{r}
$$

Therefore, if rank $r$ exists, the forest has at least $2^{r}$ nodes, forcing $r \le \log_2 n$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Lemma — Tree Height with Union by Rank

$$
\text{Height of any tree} \;\le\; \text{rank of its root} \;\le\; \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor
$$

Thus a single `Find` traverses at most $O(\log n)$ nodes.

## 3.3 Amortized Analysis with Path Compression Alone

We use the **aggregate method**. Consider a sequence of $m$ `Make-Set`/`Find` operations.

For each node $x$, define the **block size** of $x$ as the number of consecutive ancestors of $x$ (including $x$) that have the same rank.

A standard result (Tarjan, 1975):

$$
\text{Total cost of } m \text{ Finds} \;\le\; (m + n) \cdot \log^{*} n + O(m)
$$

where $\log^{*} n$ is the **iterated logarithm** (number of times you must take $\log_2$ to drop below 1). With Union by Rank added, the iterated-logarithm tightens to the **inverse Ackermann** $\alpha(n)$.

## 3.4 Full Amortized Analysis — Union by Rank + Path Compression

We use the **potential method**.

**Potential function:** $\Phi = \sum_{x} \log_2 (\text{subtree size of } x)$? No — the standard potential uses **ranks grouped by levels** of the Ackermann function. Tarjan's choice is:

$$
\Phi = \sum_{x \text{ is a child of a root}} \phi(x)
$$

where $\phi(x)$ depends on `rank[x]` and `rank[parent[x]]`. For brevity, the **master theorem** for this data structure is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem (Tarjan, 1975) — Final Master Result**
> A sequence of $m$ `Make-Set`, `Find`, and `Union` operations on $n$ elements runs in $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ time.

Since $\alpha(n)$ is bounded by **4** for all practical inputs, this is **effectively linear in $m$**.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Trace of Operations

Consider elements $\{a, b, c, d, e, f, g, h\}$. We apply a sequence and show the forest state.

**Initial state:** Each element is its own root of rank 0.

$$
\{\{a\},\ \{b\},\ \{c\},\ \{d\},\ \{e\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\},\ \{h\}\}
$$

**Operation sequence:**

| Step | Operation | Roots Linked | Rank Update | Resulting Forest |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Union(a, b)` | rank 0 = rank 0 → $a$ becomes root, rank[$a$] = 1 | rank[$a$] = 1 | $\{a \to b\},\ \{c\},\ \{d\},\ \{e\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\},\ \{h\}$ |
| 2 | `Union(c, d)` | rank 0 = 0 → $c$ root, rank[$c$] = 1 | rank[$c$] = 1 | $\{a \to b\},\ \{c \to d\},\ \{e\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\},\ \{h\}$ |
| 3 | `Union(a, c)` | rank 1 = 1 → $a$ root, rank[$a$] = 2 | rank[$a$] = 2 | $a$ tree of 4 nodes, $\{e\},\ \{f\},\ \{g\},\ \{h\}$ |
| 4 | `Union(e, f)` | rank 0 = 0 → $e$ root, rank[$e$] = 1 | rank[$e$] = 1 | …, $\{e \to f\},\ \{g\},\ \{h\}$ |
| 5 | `Union(g, h)` | rank 0 = 0 → $g$ root, rank[$g$] = 1 | rank[$g$] = 1 | …, $\{g \to h\}$ |
| 6 | `Find(g)` with Path Compression | $g \to g$ (already root) | unchanged | no change |
| 7 | `Find(b)` with Path Compression | Path: $b \to a$, redirects $b$ to point to $a$ directly | unchanged | $a$ tree flattened at level 1 |

**Step 3 detailed justification (Union by Rank tie-break):**

$$
\text{rank}(a) = 1, \quad \text{rank}(c) = 1
$$

Since ranks are equal, we may choose either as the new root. Convention: pick the first argument's root. So:

$$
\text{parent}(c) \leftarrow a, \quad \text{rank}(a) \leftarrow 1 + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 7 — Path Compression on $b$:**

Before: $b \to a$ (with $a$ as root).

$$
\text{Find}(b): \text{ parent}(b) = a \neq b, \text{ so } \text{parent}(b) \leftarrow \text{Find}(a) = a
$$

After: $b \to a$ directly, but in deeper scenarios the path from $b$ all the way up to the root is compressed.

## 3.6 Complexity Derivation Summary

Let $m$ = total number of operations, $n$ = number of elements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\text{sequence of } m \text{ ops on } n \text{ elements}) &= O\bigl(m \cdot \alpha(n)\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

The constant hidden inside the big-O is small (typically 3-4 in practice). For **KTU 14-mark problems**, the expected answer is this single line plus the **proof sketch** of Lemma 3.1 and a brief explanation of $\alpha(n)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Initial Forest — Eight Singleton Sets

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[Node a, rank 0]:::root
    B1[Node b, rank 0]:::root
    C1[Node c, rank 0]:::root
    D1[Node d, rank 0]:::root
    E1[Node e, rank 0]:::root
    F1[Node f, rank 0]:::root
    G1[Node g, rank 0]:::root
    H1[Node h, rank 0]:::root

    classDef root fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading:** Each element is its own root (representative). No parents are set.

## 4.2 After Union(a,b) and Union(c,d)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A2[Node a, rank 1]:::root
    B2[Node b, rank 0]:::child
    C2[Node c, rank 1]:::root
    D2[Node d, rank 0]:::child
    E2[Node e, rank 0]:::root
    F2[Node f, rank 0]:::root
    G2[Node g, rank 0]:::root
    H2[Node h, rank 0]:::root

    A2 --> B2
    C2 --> D2

    classDef root fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef child fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading:** Two mini-trees of height 1 have formed. The roots are $a$ and $c$, both rank 1.

## 4.3 After Union(a, c) — Rank Tie-Break Promotes $a$ to Rank 2

```mermaid
graph TD
    A3[Node a, rank 2]:::root
    B3[Node b, rank 0]:::child
    C3[Node c, rank 1]:::child
    D3[Node d, rank 0]:::child
    E3[Node e, rank 0]:::root
    F3[Node f, rank 0]:::root
    G3[Node g, rank 0]:::root
    H3[Node h, rank 0]:::root

    A3 --> B3
    A3 --> C3
    C3 --> D3

    classDef root fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef child fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading:** $a$ (rank 2) is now the root of $\{a, b, c, d\}$. Note that **$a$ has two children** ($b$ and $c$); the tree is **not** binary. The rank is the height, not the branching factor.

## 4.4 Path Compression on Find(b) — After Flattening

```mermaid
graph TD
    A4[Node a, rank 2]:::root
    B4[Node b, rank 0]:::child
    C4[Node c, rank 1]:::child
    D4[Node d, rank 0]:::child
    E4[Node e, rank 0]:::root
    F4[Node f, rank 0]:::root
    G4[Node g, rank 0]:::root
    H4[Node h, rank 0]:::root

    A4 --> C4
    C4 --> D4
    A4 -.-> B4

    classDef root fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef child fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#B7791F,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

**Reading:** The dotted arrow `$A$ -.$-> $B$` represents the **direct link** established by path compression. Future `Find(b)` is now $O(1)$.

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Union-Find Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Caller Invokes Operation] --> B{Operation Type}
    B -->|Make-Set x| C[Allocate new node]
    C --> C1[Set parent x = x]
    C1 --> C2[Set rank x = 0]
    C2 --> Z[Return]

    B -->|Find x| D{parent x = x}
    D -->|Yes| E[Return x]
    D -->|No| F[Recursive Call Find parent x]
    F --> G[Update parent x = returned root]
    G --> E

    B -->|Union x y| H[ru = Find x]
    H --> I[rv = Find y]
    I --> J{ru = rv}
    J -->|Yes| Z
    J -->|No| K{Compare rank ru and rank rv}
    K -->|rank ru > rank rv| L[parent rv = ru]
    L --> Z
    K -->|rank ru < rank rv| M[parent ru = rv]
    M --> Z
    K -->|rank ru = rank rv| N[parent rv = ru]
    N --> O[rank ru = rank ru + 1]
    O --> Z
```

**Reading:** A complete call-routing diagram of the three operations. Note the path-compression step inside `Find` and the rank-comparison branch inside `Union`.

## 4.6 Rank-Increment Tree — A Worst-Case Construction

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[Node r0, rank 0]:::root
    R1[Node r1, rank 1]:::root
    R2[Node r2, rank 2]:::root
    R3[Node r3, rank 3]:::root
    R4[Node r4, rank 4]:::root

    R3 --> R2
    R2 --> R1
    R1 --> R0

    R4 --> R3
```
