```mermaid
    R0 ~~~ A1[a, leaf]
    R1 ~~~ A2[b, leaf]
    R2 ~~~ A3[c, leaf]
    R3 ~~~ A4[d, leaf]
    R4 ~~~ A5[e, leaf]

    classDef root fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading:** A linear chain of roots. The depth of the tree is 4. Even **without** path compression, the height is $O(\log n)$. This is the **worst-case** structure for Union by Rank alone.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> Define the disjoint-set data structure. List its three fundamental operations and briefly state the purpose of each.

**Model Answer:**

> A **disjoint-set data structure** maintains a collection of pairwise disjoint dynamic sets. Each set is identified by one of its members called the **representative**.
>
> - **`Make-Set(x)`** — Creates a new set whose only member (and representative) is $x$. Pre-condition: $x$ is not already in any existing set.
> - **`Find(x)`** — Returns a pointer to the representative of the (unique) set containing $x$.
> - **`Union(x, y)`** — Merges the two sets containing $x$ and $y$ into a single set, destroying the old sets.
>
> Application: building equivalence classes, Kruskal's MST, network connectivity.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> What is **path compression**? How does it improve the amortized cost of `Find` operations?

**Model Answer:**

> **Path compression** is a heuristic applied during `Find(x)`. After locating the root $r$ of the tree containing $x$, it redirects the `parent` pointer of every node on the path from $x$ to $r$ directly to $r$, effectively flattening the tree.
>
> While a single `Find` may cost $O(\log n)$ in the worst case, subsequent `Find` operations on the same elements become $O(1)$. Over a sequence of operations, the **amortized cost** with **both** union by rank and path compression is $O(\alpha(n))$, where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function — practically a constant.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **linked-list representation** of disjoint sets. Discuss its time complexity with and without the weighted-union heuristic. Show that the **weighted union** (union by size) gives an $O(m + n \log n)$ total cost for $m$ operations on $n$ elements.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **disjoint-set forest** representation. With a clear pseudocode, describe the **union-by-rank** heuristic. Prove the lemma that the rank of any node is at most $\lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$.

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Linked-List Representation

In this representation, each set is a **doubly linked list**.

- Each list has a **head pointer** (the representative) and a **tail pointer** (for O(1) append).
- Each object/node has a **back-pointer to the head** of its list, so `Find(x)` is $O(1)$: just follow the back-pointer and return the head.

**Operations:**
- `Make-Set(x)`: create a list of one node, head = tail = $x$, back-pointer of $x$ = $x$. Cost = $O(1)$.
- `Find(x)`: return `list[x].head`. Cost = $O(1)$.
- `Union(x, y)`:
  1. Find heads $h_x, h_y$ and lengths $\ell_x, \ell_y$.
  2. **Without weighted union**: append list $y$ at the tail of list $x$.
  3. **Cost** = $O(\ell_y)$ (we must update the back-pointers of every node in list $y$).

**Worst case (no heuristic):** Append a list of length $\Theta(n)$ to another of length 1 → $O(n)$ per union. A sequence of $n-1$ such unions is $O(n^2)$.

**Weighted Union (Union by Size):**
- Always append the **shorter** list to the longer one.
- Each element's back-pointer is updated at most $\log_2 n$ times (each time it is in the shorter list, the list size at least doubles).
- **Total cost** over $m$ operations:
$$
T(m, n) = O\bigl(n + \sum \log_2 n\bigr) = O(n + m \log n)
$$

> **Drawback:** `Find` in linked-list is $O(1)$, but `Union` is not symmetric. The **forest** representation is preferred because it gives $O(\alpha(n))$ for *all* operations, not just `Union`.

#### Part (b) — Forest Representation and Union by Rank

**Forest representation:** Each set is a rooted tree. The root = representative. Each node $x$ has fields `parent[x]` and `rank[x]`.

**Pseudocode for `Make-Set`, `Find`, `Union`:**

```
Make-Set(x):
    parent[x] = x
    rank[x]   = 0

Find(x):
    if parent[x] != x:
        parent[x] = Find(parent[x])     // path compression
    return parent[x]

Union(x, y):
    rx = Find(x)
    ry = Find(y)
    if rx == ry: return
    if rank[rx] > rank[ry]:
        parent[ry] = rx
    else if rank[rx] < rank[ry]:
        parent[rx] = ry
    else:
        parent[ry] = rx
        rank[rx]   = rank[rx] + 1
```

**Proof of Lemma 3.1:** A node $x$ of rank $r$ was created when **two** roots of rank $r-1$ were linked to form $x$. By the induction hypothesis, each had at least $2^{r-1}$ nodes. Thus:

$$
\text{size}(x) \ge 2^{r-1} + 2^{r-1} = 2^{r}
$$

So if rank $r$ exists in a forest of $n$ nodes, we need $2^{r} \le n$, giving $r \le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$. $\blacksquare$

**Consequence:** The height of any tree $\le \lfloor \log_2 n \rfloor$, so without path compression, a single `Find` is $O(\log n)$.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, Module 2]`
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Define the **Ackermann function** $A(m, n)$ and its **inverse** $\alpha(n)$. Tabulate the values of $A(m, n)$ for $m = 0, 1, 2$ and explain why $\alpha(n)$ grows so slowly.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** State and prove Tarjan's bound: a sequence of $m$ `Make-Set`, `Find`, and `Union` operations on $n$ elements using **both** union-by-rank and path-compression runs in $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ amortized time. Briefly explain why this is treated as a **practical constant** in real systems.

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Ackermann Function

$$
A(m, n) =
\begin{cases}
n + 1, & m = 0 \\
A(m-1, 1), & m \ge 1, n = 0 \\
A(m-1, A(m, n-1)), & m \ge 1, n \ge 1
\end{cases}
$$

**Tabulated values for $m = 0, 1, 2$:**

| $m$ | General form | $A(m, 0)$ | $A(m, 1)$ | $A(m, 2)$ | $A(m, 3)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $n+1$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| 1 | $n+2$ | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
| 2 | $2n+3$ | 3 | 5 | 7 | 9 |

**Derivation for $m = 1$:** $A(1, n) = A(0, A(1, n-1)) = A(1, n-1) + 1$, with base $A(1, 0) = A(0, 1) = 2$. Solving: $A(1, n) = n + 2$.

**Derivation for $m = 2$:** $A(2, n) = A(1, A(2, n-1)) = A(2, n-1) + 2$, with base $A(2, 0) = A(1, 1) = 3$. Solving: $A(2, n) = 2n + 3$.

**Why $\alpha(n)$ grows slowly:** $A(3, n) = 2^{n+3} - 3$, $A(4, n) = 2^{2^{\cdot^{\cdot^{2}}}} - 3$ (tower of $2$s of height $n+3$). So $A(m, m)$ quickly exceeds any conceivable $n$. The inverse $\alpha(n) = \min\{m \mid A(m, m) \ge n\}$ therefore stays $\le 4$ for all $n$ up to a tower of 2s — practically forever.

#### Part (b) — Tarjan's Theorem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tarjan's Theorem (1975):**
> A sequence of $m$ `Make-Set`, `Find`, `Union` operations on $n$ elements, with both union-by-rank and path compression, runs in $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ time.

**Proof Sketch (Potential Method):**

1. **Define ranks precisely** and partition nodes by rank into "rank groups" indexed by a function of the Ackermann levels.
2. **Potential function** $\Phi$ = sum over all nodes of a carefully chosen function of `rank[x]` and `rank[parent[x]]`.
3. **Show that** each `Make-Set` increases $\Phi$ by $O(1)$.
4. **Show that** a `Link` (union of two trees) does not increase $\Phi$ except when ranks are equal (which bumps a rank by 1, increasing $\Phi$ by $O(1)$).
5. **Show that** a `Find` with path compression decreases $\Phi$ by an amount that **amortizes** the path-walk cost, leaving only $O(\alpha(n))$ per operation.
6. **Total amortized cost** = sum of (true cost + $\Delta \Phi$) = $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$.

> **Practical Constancy:** For all $n \le 2^{65536}$ (more than the number of atoms in the universe), $\alpha(n) \le 4$. Hence $m \cdot \alpha(n) \le 4m$ — effectively a constant factor. This is why disjoint-set operations are considered "free" in practice.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation**
> 1. **Confusing `rank` with `height`** — After path compression, the *true* height is less than `rank`. The `rank` field is **not updated** during compression; it remains a static upper bound. Students who update `rank` during `Find` will **lose 2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the rank-increment on tie** — When two trees of equal rank are united, the new root's rank must be incremented. Omitting this is a **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Saying $O(1)$ instead of $O(\alpha(n))$** — The amortized bound is $O(\alpha(n))$, not $O(1)$, though $\alpha(n) \le 4$ in practice. Use the correct notation to score full marks.
> 4. **Writing the Ackermann function in a non-standard form** — Use the textbook 3-case definition. Any other formulation (e.g., one-case) is a **partial-mark answer**.
> 5. **In tree diagrams, drawing the tree as binary** — The disjoint-set forest is **NOT** a binary tree; a root can have arbitrarily many children. Drawing it as a binary tree is a **1-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> ✅ Disjoint-set = collection of mutually exclusive sets, each with a representative.
>
> ✅ Three operations: `Make-Set(x)`, `Find(x)`, `Union(x, y)`.
>
> ✅ Two representations: **Linked-List** (Find = $O(1)$, Union = $O(n)$) and **Forest** (more general, $O(\alpha(n))$ with both heuristics).
>
> ✅ **Union by Rank:** attach shorter tree under taller; bump rank only on equal-rank link.
>
> ✅ **Path Compression:** in `Find(x)`, make every node on the path to root point directly to root.
>
> ✅ `rank` is an **upper bound on height**, not the height itself.
>
> ✅ `rank[x] ≤ ⌊log₂ n⌋` ⇒ tree height ≤ $\log_2 n$.
>
> ✅ **Ackermann function** $A(m, n)$ — grows faster than any primitive-recursive function.
>
> ✅ **Inverse Ackermann** $\alpha(n)$ — grows so slowly that $\alpha(n) \le 4$ for all practical $n$.
>
> ✅ **Tarjan's Theorem:** $m$ ops on $n$ elements ⇒ $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ amortized.
>
> ✅ **Applications:** Kruskal's MST, network connectivity, image segmentation, register allocation.
>
> ✅ **Tree is NOT binary** — a root can have many children; ranks are heights, not arities.
>
> ✅ **Path compression does NOT change ranks** — it only changes `parent` pointers.
>
> ✅ **Amortized, not worst-case** — single operations can still be $O(\log n)$; the bound is over a sequence.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Connected Components of a Graph
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Connected Components of a Graph

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connected Component (KTU Standard Definition)**
> A **connected component** of an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a maximal subgraph $H = (V', E')$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in V'$, there exists a path between $u$ and $v$ inside $H$. The word **maximal** means that no additional vertex or edge from $G$ can be added to $H$ without breaking the connectivity property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connected Graph**
> A graph $G$ is said to be **connected** if and only if it has exactly **one** connected component (i.e., $k = 1$). Otherwise, it is called a **disconnected** graph with $k \geq 2$ components.

Let $k$ denote the total number of connected components of $G$. Then the vertex set $V$ can be partitioned as:

$$V = V_1 \cup V_2 \cup V_3 \cup \dots \cup V_k$$

where each $V_i$ is the vertex set of the $i$-th component, and the $V_i$ are pairwise disjoint. Formally:

$$V_i \cap V_j = \emptyset \quad \text{for all} \quad i \neq j$$

and there is **no edge** of $G$ joining a vertex in $V_i$ to a vertex in $V_j$ for $i \neq j$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Islands and Bridges" Intuition

Imagine a vast ocean with several islands scattered across it. Within each island, every village is connected to every other village by roads. However, between two different islands, there is **no bridge**, so you cannot walk from a village on Island A to a village on Island B without using a boat (i.e., it is impossible by land alone).

In this analogy:
- **The whole map** = the graph $G$
- **Each island** = one connected component
- **Villages** = vertices (nodes)
- **Roads within an island** = edges
- **The absence of a bridge** = no edges between two different components

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** A connected component is the largest "club" of vertices such that **every member can reach every other member** by walking only on the edges of the club. The moment you try to add a new vertex to the club and there is no path to that vertex, the club stops being maximal — meaning a new component must begin.

### 1.3 KTU-Relevant Terminology Glossary

| Term | Symbol / Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Graph Order | $n = \mid V \mid$ | Total number of vertices |
| Graph Size | $m = \mid E \mid$ | Total number of edges |
| Component Count | $k$ | Number of connected components |
| $i$-th Component | $G_i = (V_i, E_i)$ | The $i$-th connected subgraph |
| Order of $i$-th Component | $n_i = \mid V_i \mid$ | Vertices in component $i$ |
| Size of $i$-th Component | $m_i = \mid E_i \mid$ | Edges in component $i$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Connected Components

A connected component satisfies the following rigorous properties, all of which are **frequently tested in KTU examinations**:

1. **Partition Property of Vertices:**
The vertex sets of all connected components form a partition of $V$. Hence:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i = n \quad \text{where} \quad n_i = \mid V_i \mid$$

2. **Disjointness of Edges Across Components:**
No edge of $G$ connects two different components. Therefore:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = m \quad \text{where} \quad m_i = \mid E_i \mid$$

3. **No Cross-Component Edges:**
For any edge $(u, v) \in E$, both endpoints $u$ and $v$ belong to the **same** component.

4. **Each Component is Itself a Connected Graph:**
Within every $G_i$, there is a path between any two of its vertices.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations. Memorize every row.

| # | Formula / Inequality | Condition | Engineering Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i = n$ | Always | Total vertex count identity |
| 2 | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = m$ | Always | Total edge count identity |
| 3 | $m \geq n - k$ | Each component is a tree (acyclic) | Lower bound — minimum forest |
| 4 | $m \leq \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$ | Exactly $k-1$ isolated vertices, rest form $K_{n-k+1}$ | Upper bound — maximum edges |
| 5 | $m_i \leq \dfrac{n_i(n_i - 1)}{2}$ | Component $i$ is a complete graph $K_{n_i}$ | Local edge cap per component |
| 6 | $k = 1$ | $\Leftrightarrow$ $G$ is connected | Connectivity test |
| 7 | $k = n$ | $\Leftrightarrow$ $G$ has no edges (empty graph) | Total disconnectedness |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Whenever a question provides $n$, $m$, and $k$, always substitute into the inequalities $n - k \leq m \leq \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$ to verify feasibility. KTU boards **love** asking "Is the given data consistent?"

### 2.3 Derivation of the Edge Count Bounds

**Lower Bound ($m \geq n - k$):**
Each connected component $G_i$ with $n_i$ vertices, being connected, requires **at least** $n_i - 1$ edges to remain connected (a spanning tree). Summing over all $k$ components:

$$m = \sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i \geq \sum_{i=1}^{k} (n_i - 1) = \left( \sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i \right) - k = n - k$$

**Upper Bound ($m \leq \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$):**
To maximize the number of edges while keeping exactly $k$ components, we should isolate $k - 1$ vertices (so each isolated vertex forms its own component) and pack all remaining $n - (k - 1) = n - k + 1$ vertices into a single complete graph $K_{n-k+1}$. The total edges are then:

$$m_{\max} = 0 \cdot (k - 1) + \binom{n - k + 1}{2} = \frac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Connected components appear extensively in:

- **Computer Networks:** Each connected component is a *broadcast domain* — packets cannot cross components without a router.
- **VLSI Circuit Design:** Identifying isolated sub-circuits for power-gating.
- **Social Network Analysis:** Communities and friend-clusters on platforms like Facebook, LinkedIn.
- **Image Processing:** Connected component labelling to detect distinct objects in a binary image.
- **Compiler Optimization:** Finding strongly connected components (directed analogue) for loop detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The DFS-Based Connected Components Algorithm

The standard KTU-expected algorithm to find all connected components uses **Depth-First Search (DFS)**. Below is the complete pseudocode with exhaustive step-by-step logic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm: Connected-Components(G)**
> **Input:** A graph $G = (V, E)$ in adjacency-list representation.
> **Output:** A label $comp[v]$ for every $v \in V$ indicating its component number, plus the count $k$.

```
1.  for each vertex v in V:
2.       visited[v] ← FALSE
3.       comp[v]    ← 0
4.  k ← 0
5.  for each vertex v in V:
6.       if visited[v] = FALSE:
7.            k ← k + 1
8.            DFS-Visit(G, v, k)
9.  return (k, comp[·])
```

```
DFS-Visit(G, u, k):
1.  visited[u] ← TRUE
2.  comp[u]    ← k
3.  for each neighbour w of u in Adj[u]:
4.       if visited[w] = FALSE:
5.            DFS-Visit(G, w, k)
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Explanation

| Line | What It Does | Why It Matters |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1–3 | Initialize every vertex as unvisited and unlabelled. | Ensures no vertex is wrongly skipped during discovery. |
| 4 | Set component counter to zero. | The counter will be incremented once per new component found. |
| 5 | Outer loop iterates through every vertex. | Necessary because a vertex may belong to a component not yet discovered. |
| 6 | Check if current vertex is unvisited. | If already visited, it already belongs to some prior component. |
| 7 | Increment the component counter. | We are about to start a brand-new component. |
| 8 | Trigger DFS from $v$, tagging everything reachable as part of component $k$. | DFS explores the *entire* maximal connected subgraph containing $v$. |
| DFS-1 | Mark $u$ as visited. | Prevents re-processing. |
| DFS-2 | Assign component number $k$ to $u$. | Stores the result for output. |
| DFS-3,4 | Recurse into every unvisited neighbour. | Builds the DFS tree of the current component. |

### 3.3 Time-Complexity Derivation (Asymptotic Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme demands a **rigorous derivation** of the time complexity, not just a final answer.

**Step 1 — Cost of Initialization (Lines 1–4):**
The initialization loop visits each vertex once.

$$T_{\text{init}} = \Theta(n)$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Outer Loop (Line 5):**
This loop iterates over all $n$ vertices.

$$T_{\text{outer}} = \Theta(n)$$

**Step 3 — Cost of All DFS Calls (Line 8 across all iterations):**
The total work of DFS is the sum of:
- The time spent *visiting* each vertex exactly once: $\Theta(n)$.
- The time spent *scanning* each edge exactly twice (once from each endpoint) when adjacency lists are used: $\Theta(m)$.

$$T_{\text{DFS-total}} = \Theta(n + m)$$

**Step 4 — Total Time Complexity:**

$$T(n, m) = T_{\text{init}} + T_{\text{outer}} + T_{\text{DFS-total}}$$

$$T(n, m) = \Theta(n) + \Theta(n) + \Theta(n + m)$$

$$\boxed{T(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)}$$

**Space Complexity:** The DFS recursion stack plus the `visited` and `comp` arrays require $\Theta(n)$ space, but the graph itself occupies $\Theta(n + m)$ in adjacency-list form.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem:** Consider the undirected graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9\}$ and edge set $E = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (1,4), (5,6), (7,8), (8,9), (7,9)\}$. Find all connected components using DFS starting from vertex 1. Also verify the formula $n - k \leq m$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**
$n = 9$, $m = 8$.

**Step 2 — Trace DFS:**

Start DFS from vertex $1$ → **Component 1** begins.
- Visit $1$. $\text{comp}[1] = 1$.
- Visit neighbour $2$ (via edge $(1,2)$). $\text{comp}[2] = 1$.
- From $2$, visit $3$ (via $(2,3)$). $\text{comp}[3] = 1$.
- From $3$, neighbour $1$ already visited. Backtrack.
- Back at $1$, visit $4$ (via $(1,4)$). $\text{comp}[4] = 1$.
- No more reachable vertices. **Component 1 = $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$.**

Continue outer loop. Vertex $5$ is unvisited. **Component 2** begins.
- Visit $5$. $\text{comp}[5] = 2$.
- Visit $6$ (via $(5,6)$). $\text{comp}[6] = 2$.
- **Component 2 = $\{5, 6\}$.**

Continue outer loop. Vertex $7$ is unvisited. **Component 3** begins.
- Visit $7$. $\text{comp}[7] = 3$.
- Visit $8$ (via $(7,8)$). $\text{comp}[8] = 3$.
- From $8$, visit $9$ (via $(8,9)$). $\text{comp}[9] = 3$.
- **Component 3 = $\{7, 8, 9\}$.**

**Step 3 — Tabulate Results:**

| Vertex | Component Label | DFS Discovery Order |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1 | 1st |
| 2 | 1 | 2nd |
| 3 | 1 | 3rd |
| 4 | 1 | 4th |
| 5 | 2 | 5th |
| 6 | 2 | 6th |
| 7 | 3 | 7th |
| 8 | 3 | 8th |
| 9 | 3 | 9th |

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary inequality:**

$$k = 3, \quad n - k = 9 - 3 = 6, \quad m = 8$$

Check lower bound:

$$6 \leq 8 \quad \checkmark$$

Check upper bound:

$$\frac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2} = \frac{7 \cdot 6}{2} = 21$$

$$8 \leq 21 \quad \checkmark$$

The data is **consistent**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Graph with Three Connected Components

The following diagram visualizes the worked example from Section 3.4. Note: All node IDs are alphanumeric, and all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside double-quoted labels, as per the Mermaid Compilation Safeguards).

```mermaid
graph TD
    classDef comp1 fill:#FFDDC1,stroke:#FF6B35,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef comp2 fill:#C1E1FF,stroke:#1E90FF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef comp3 fill:#D4FFC1,stroke:#32CD32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000

    v1((V1)) --- v2((V2))
    v2 --- v3((V3))
    v3 --- v1
    v1 --- v4((V4))
    v5((V5)) --- v6((V6))
    v7((V7)) --- v8((V8))
    v8 --- v9((V9))
    v7 --- v9

    class v1,v2,v3,v4 comp1
    class v5,v6 comp2
    class v7,v8,v9 comp3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **orange cluster** $\{V1, V2, V3, V4\}$ is **Connected Component 1** — internally connected.
- The **blue cluster** $\{V5, V6\}$ is **Connected Component 2**.
- The **green cluster** $\{V7, V8, V9\}$ is **Connected Component 3** — forms a triangle (cycle).
- **No edge** exists between any two clusters. They are isolated from each other.

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — DFS-Based Algorithm Flowchart

The following block-level functional architecture flow captures the operational logic of the `Connected-Components(G)` algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START Algorithm]) --> B[Initialize visited array to FALSE for all vertices]
    B --> C[Initialize comp array to 0 for all vertices]
    C --> D[Set component counter k equals 0]
    D --> E{Iterate over each vertex v in V}
    E --> F{Is visited v FALSE?}
    F -->|No| E
    F -->|Yes| G[Increment k by 1]
    G --> H[Call DFS Visit G v k]
    H --> I[Mark u visited and assign comp u equals k]
    I --> J{Does u have unvisited neighbour w?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Recurse DFS Visit G w k]
    K --> J
    J -->|No| L[Return to outer loop]
    L --> E
    E -->|All vertices processed| M[Output k and comp array]
    M --> N([END Algorithm])

    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000
    style N fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000
    style F fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000000
    style J fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#FFA500,color:#000000
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Use BFS vs DFS for Component Finding

| Criterion | BFS-Based | DFS-Based |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Time Complexity | $\Theta(n + m)$ | $\Theta(n + m)$ |
| Space Complexity | $\Theta(n)$ queue | $\Theta(n)$ recursion stack |
| Output Order | Level-order (shortest path from source) | Pre-order / post-order |
| Implementation | Iterative (queue) | Recursive or iterative (stack) |
| KTU Preferred | Tie | Tie — examiner accepts both |
| Use When | You also need shortest distances | You also need topological ordering / cycle detection |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
**Define a connected component of an undirected graph. With a neat diagram, show a graph having exactly three connected components.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A **connected component** of an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a maximal connected subgraph $H = (V', E')$ of $G$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in V'$, there is a path between $u$ and $v$ inside $H$, and $V'$ cannot be enlarged by adding any other vertex of $V$ while preserving connectivity.

> **Diagram (1 mark):** A graph with three connected components (e.g., a triangle as one component, a single edge as the second, and an isolated vertex as the third).

> **Listing (1 mark):** The three components are clearly partitioned as $V_1, V_2, V_3$ with $V_1 \cap V_2 = \emptyset$, $V_1 \cap V_3 = \emptyset$, $V_2 \cap V_3 = \emptyset$, and there is no edge between any two different component vertex sets.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**State and prove the relationship between the number of vertices $n$, the number of edges $m$, and the number of connected components $k$ of a graph.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (1 mark):** For any undirected graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, $m$ edges, and $k$ connected components, the inequality $n - k \leq m \leq \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$ holds.

> **Lower bound proof (1 mark):** Each component with $n_i$ vertices, being connected, must have at least $n_i - 1$ edges. Summing gives $m = \sum m_i \geq \sum (n_i - 1) = n - k$.

> **Upper bound proof (1 mark):** To maximize $m$ with $k$ components, isolate $k - 1$ vertices and form a complete graph on the remaining $n - k + 1$ vertices, yielding $m_{\max} = \binom{n - k + 1}{2} = \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** For an undirected graph $G$ with $n = 10$ vertices, $m = 13$ edges, and $k = 3$ connected components, verify whether the data is consistent. State any necessary condition that must hold. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the DFS-based algorithm to find all connected components of the graph $G$ with vertex set $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$ and edge set $E = \{(1, 2), (2, 3), (4, 5), (5, 6), (6, 4), (4, 7)\}$. Show each step of the DFS traversal. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Verification of Consistency (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the relevant inequality (2 marks):**

For any undirected graph:

$$n - k \leq m \leq \frac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values (2 marks):**
$n = 10, \quad m = 13, \quad k = 3$

Lower bound:

$$n - k = 10 - 3 = 7$$

Upper bound:

$$\frac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2} = \frac{(8)(7)}{2} = 28$$

**Step 3 — Check feasibility (2 marks):**

$$7 \leq 13 \leq 28 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Conclude (1 mark):**
The given data is **consistent** and represents a valid undirected graph with 3 connected components. The data satisfies both the lower and upper edge-count bounds for a graph with $k = 3$ components.

---

**Part (b) — DFS Traversal to Find Components (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Setup (1 mark):**
Initialize: $\text{visited}[1..7] = \text{FALSE}$, $\text{comp}[1..7] = 0$, $k = 0$.

**Step 2 — Start DFS from vertex 1 (2 marks):**

- $k = 0 + 1 = 1$. Call `DFS-Visit(G, 1, 1)`.
- Visit $1$: $\text{comp}[1] = 1$.
- Neighbour of $1$ is $2$ (unvisited) → recurse.
- Visit $2$: $\text{comp}[2] = 1$.
- Neighbour of $2$: $1$ (visited), $3$ (unvisited) → recurse.
- Visit $3$: $\text{comp}[3] = 1$.
- No unvisited neighbours of $3$. Backtrack.
- Back at $2$, no more unvisited neighbours. Backtrack to $1$. No more unvisited neighbours.

**Component 1 = $\{1, 2, 3\}$** with $n_1 = 3, m_1 = 2$.

**Step 3 — Start DFS from vertex 4 (2 marks):**

- $k = 1 + 1 = 2$. Call `DFS-Visit(G, 4, 2)`.
- Visit $4$: $\text{comp}[4] = 2$.
- Neighbour of $4$: $5$ (unvisited), $6$ (unvisited), $7$ (unvisited).
- Pick $5$: Visit $5$: $\text{comp}[5] = 2$. Neighbour $4$ (visited), $6$ (unvisited) → recurse.
- Visit $6$: $\text{comp}[6] = 2$. Neighbour $4$ (visited), $5$ (visited) → backtrack.
- Back at $5$, no more unvisited neighbours. Backtrack to $4$.
- Pick $7$: Visit $7$: $\text{comp}[7] = 2$. Neighbour $4$ (visited) → backtrack.
- Back at $4$, no more unvisited neighbours.

**Component 2 = $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$** with $n_2 = 4, m_2 = 4$.

**Step 4 — Final Summary (2 marks):**

| Component | Vertices | $n_i$ | $m_i$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $\{1, 2, 3\}$ | 3 | 2 |
| 2 | $\{4, 5, 6, 7\}$ | 4 | 4 |
| **Total** | $V$ | $n = 7$ | $m = 6$ |

**Verification:** $k = 2$, $n - k = 7 - 2 = 5$, and $m = 6 \geq 5$ ✓. The traversal is **correct**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the DFS-based algorithm to find the connected components of an undirected graph. Analyse its time and space complexity with a full derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the graph $G$ with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F, G, H\}$ and $E = \{(A, B), (B, C), (C, A), (D, E), (F, G), (G, H)\}$. Determine the number of connected components, list each component, and state the value of $k$, $n$, and $m$. Verify the formula $n - k \leq m \leq \dfrac{(n-k+1)(n-k)}{2}$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation and Complexity Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Algorithm description (3 marks):**
Present the `Connected-Components(G)` algorithm and the `DFS-Visit` subroutine as given in Section 3.1. Explain that the outer loop iterates over all $n$ vertices, and whenever an unvisited vertex is encountered, a fresh DFS is launched which marks the *entire* connected component containing that vertex.

**Step 2 — Time complexity derivation (3 marks):**

**Initialization:** The first `for` loop touches every vertex once → $\Theta(n)$ time.

**Outer traversal:** Iterates over $n$ vertices → $\Theta(n)$ time.

**Total DFS work:** Each vertex is visited exactly once, contributing $\Theta(n)$. Each edge is examined exactly twice in the adjacency list (once per endpoint), contributing $\Theta(m)$. Hence, total DFS time is $\Theta(n + m)$.

**Total:**

$$T(n, m) = \Theta(n) + \Theta(n) + \Theta(n + m) = \Theta(n + m)$$

**Step 3 — Space complexity (1 mark):**
The `visited` and `comp` arrays require $\Theta(n)$ space, and the DFS recursion stack requires at most $\Theta(n)$ depth. Hence, total auxiliary space (excluding the graph storage) is $\Theta(n)$.

---

**Part (b) — Component Identification and Verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters (1 mark):**
$n = \mid V \mid = 8, \quad m = \mid E \mid = 6$.

**Step 2 — Find components (3 marks):**

- **Component 1:** Edges $(A,B), (B,C), (C,A)$ form a triangle. **$V_1 = \{A, B, C\}$.**
- **Component 2:** Edge $(D, E)$. **$V_2 = \{D, E\}$.**
- **Component 3:** Edges $(F, G), (G, H)$ form a path. **$V_3 = \{F, G, H\}$.**

Total components: $k = 3$.

**Step 3 — Verify the inequality (2 marks):**

Lower bound:

$$n - k = 8 - 3 = 5 \leq m = 6 \quad \checkmark$$

Upper bound:

$$\frac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2} = \frac{6 \cdot 5}{2} = 15 \geq 6 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Tabulate (1 mark):**

| Component | Vertices | $n_i$ | $m_i$ |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | $\{A, B, C\}$ | 3 | 3 |
| 2 | $\{D, E\}$ | 2 | 1 |
| 3 | $\{F, G, H\}$ | 3 | 2 |
| **Total** | $V$ | $8$ | $6$ |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting maximality (–1 mark):** Many students define a connected component merely as a "connected subgraph" without emphasizing the **maximal** property. Always write the word *maximal*.
> 2. **Wrong DFS order (–2 marks):** When multiple unvisited neighbours exist, the order depends on the **adjacency list ordering**. State your assumption clearly (e.g., "neighbours processed in ascending numeric order").
> 3. **Misapplying the formula (–1 mark):** Students sometimes write $m \leq \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ which is only valid for a *connected* graph. For $k$ components, you **must** use $m \leq \dfrac{(n-k+1)(n-k)}{2}$.
> 4. **Skipping the verification step (–1 mark):** Always plug numbers into the boundary inequality and confirm the data is self-consistent. KTU examiners award marks for this concluding check.
> 5. **Confusing components with spanning trees (–1 mark):** A connected component is a *maximal connected subgraph*; a spanning tree is a *minimal connected subgraph* of a connected graph. They are distinct concepts.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- **Definition:** A *connected component* of $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal** connected subgraph — maximal in the sense that you cannot add any vertex or edge without destroying connectivity.
- **Connected graph:** A graph with $k = 1$ component.
- **Disconnected graph:** A graph with $k \geq 2$ components.
- **Vertex partition identity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} n_i = n$, where $n_i = \mid V_i \mid$.
- **Edge partition identity:** $\sum_{i=1}^{k} m_i = m$, where $m_i = \mid E_i \mid$.
- **Lower bound on edges:** $m \geq n - k$ (achieved when every component is a tree — a *forest*).
- **Upper bound on edges:** $m \leq \dfrac{(n - k + 1)(n - k)}{2}$ (achieved when $k - 1$ vertices are isolated and the rest form a complete graph).
- **Per-component bound:** $m_i \leq \dfrac{n_i(n_i - 1)}{2}$ since each component is a simple graph.
- **Algorithm:** DFS-based or BFS-based, both running in $\Theta(n + m)$ time.
- **Space:** $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space.
- **Outer loop principle:** Run DFS from every **unvisited** vertex; each such launch starts a new component.
- **Components are vertex-disjoint:** $V_i \cap V_j = \emptyset$ for $i \neq j$.
- **No cross-component edges:** Any edge in $E$ must have both endpoints in the same component.
- **Engineering applications:** Network domains, VLSI, social network clustering, image processing, compiler optimization.
- **Edge cases:** Isolated vertices each form their own component of size 1 with $m_i = 0$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Graphs - Representations, Traversals - BFS, DFS and Their Analysis, Strongly Connected Components, Topological Sorting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Graph

A **Graph** $G = (V, E)$ is a non-linear data structure consisting of a finite, non-empty set of **vertices** (or nodes) $V$ and a set of **edges** $E$ which are pairs of vertices. Graphs are the foundational mathematical abstraction used to model pairwise relationships between objects.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, a graph is formally defined as an ordered pair $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the vertex set and $E \subseteq \{(u,v) \mid u, v \in V\}$ is the edge set. The cardinality of $V$ is denoted $n = \vert V \vert$ and the cardinality of $E$ is denoted $m = \vert E \vert$.

### 1.2 Essential Terminology

* **Vertex (Node):** A fundamental unit of a graph, representing an entity.
* **Edge:** A connection between two vertices. In a directed graph, edges are ordered pairs; in an undirected graph, they are unordered pairs.
* **Adjacency:** Two vertices $u$ and $v$ are adjacent if there is an edge between them.
* **Path:** A sequence of vertices $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_k$ such that each consecutive pair is connected by an edge.
* **Cycle:** A path that starts and ends at the same vertex, with no repeated edges or vertices (except the start/end).
* **Degree:** The number of edges incident on a vertex. In directed graphs, we distinguish **in-degree** (incoming edges) and **out-degree** (outgoing edges).
* **Connected Graph:** A graph where a path exists between every pair of vertices.
* **Weighted Graph:** A graph where each edge carries an associated numerical value (weight/cost).
* **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph):** A directed graph with no directed cycles — the prerequisite for topological sorting.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the **Kerala State Road Network**. Every city is a **vertex**, and every road connecting two cities is an **edge**. If roads are one-way (like National Highway flyovers with restricted entry), the graph becomes **directed**. If each road has a distance label, it becomes a **weighted graph**. The shortest path from Kochi to Kasaragod is simply a problem of finding the minimum-weight path between two vertices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Graphs Matter in Algorithm Design:** Graphs unify a vast landscape of problems — shortest paths (Google Maps), social network suggestions (Facebook friends), dependency resolution (KTU course prerequisites), compiler design (DAGs for instruction scheduling), and network flow optimization. Mastering graph representations and traversals is non-negotiable for any software engineer.

### 1.4 Types of Graphs

| Graph Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Undirected Graph** | Edges have no direction | Facebook friendships |
| **Directed Graph (Digraph)** | Edges have a specific direction | Twitter follows, web hyperlinks |
| **Weighted Graph** | Each edge has a numerical weight | Road networks with distances |
| **Unweighted Graph** | All edges have equal weight | Simple connectivity networks |
| **Cyclic Graph** | Contains at least one cycle | Feedback loops in systems |
| **Acyclic Graph** | Contains no cycles | Tree structures, DAGs |
| **Complete Graph** | Every pair of vertices is connected | $K_n$ with $n(n-1)/2$ edges |
| **Bipartite Graph** | Vertices partitionable into two sets | Job assignment, matching problems |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Graph Representations

The choice of representation directly impacts the time and space complexity of every algorithm. KTU expects students to master the trade-offs between the three primary representations.

#### 2.1.1 Adjacency Matrix Representation

An **Adjacency Matrix** $A$ is a 2D array of size $n \times n$ where $n = \vert V \vert$. The entry $A[i][j] = 1$ (or weight $w$) if there is an edge from vertex $i$ to vertex $j$, else $0$.

* **Space Complexity:** $O(n^2)$ — irrespective of the number of edges.
* **Edge Lookup Time:** $O(1)$ — direct array access.
* **Finding All Neighbors:** $O(n)$ — must scan the entire row.
* **Best Suited For:** Dense graphs where $m \approx n^2$.

For an **undirected graph**, the matrix is symmetric: $A[i][j] = A[j][i]$.

For a **weighted graph**, the matrix stores weights instead of 1s, with a sentinel value (e.g., $\infty$ or $0$) for non-edges.

#### 2.1.2 Adjacency List Representation

An **Adjacency List** is a collection of $n$ linked lists (or dynamic arrays) where the $i$-th list contains all vertices adjacent to vertex $i$.

* **Space Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ — efficient for sparse graphs.
* **Edge Lookup Time:** $O(\deg(v))$ — must search the list.
* **Finding All Neighbors:** $O(\deg(v))$ — iterate the list.
* **Best Suited For:** Sparse graphs where $m \ll n^2$.

#### 2.1.3 Incidence Matrix Representation

An **Incidence Matrix** is a 2D array of size $n \times m$. Entry $B[i][j] = 1$ if vertex $i$ is incident to edge $j$, else $0$. For directed graphs, $B[i][j] = -1$ for the tail and $+1$ for the head.

* **Space Complexity:** $O(n \cdot m)$.
* **Use Case:** Less common in algorithm design; more common in combinatorial proofs.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Property | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List | Incidence Matrix |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Storage Space** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n \cdot m)$ |
| **Add Edge** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ (amortized) | $O(1)$ |
| **Remove Edge** | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ | $O(m)$ |
| **Edge Exists? $(u,v)$** | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg(u))$ | $O(m)$ |
| **Iterate Neighbors of $v$** | $O(n)$ | $O(\deg(v))$ | $O(m)$ |
| **Sum of Degrees** | $2m = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$ | $2m = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$ | $2m = \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v)$ |
| **Max Edges (Undirected)** | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\binom{n}{2} = \dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | $\binom{n}{2}$ |
| **Max Edges (Directed)** | $n(n-1)$ | $n(n-1)$ | $n(n-1)$ |
| **Handshaking Lemma** | $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ | $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ | $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Handshaking Lemma (Sum of Degrees Theorem):** For any undirected graph,
> $$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$$
> This is a high-yield KTU question. The "handshake" is that every edge contributes 1 to the degree of each of its two endpoints.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

* **Computer Networks:** Routers as vertices, links as edges — BFS computes minimum-hop paths.
* **Social Networks:** Users as vertices, friendships as edges — DFS/connected components detect communities.
* **VLSI Design:** Circuit components as vertices, wires as edges — topological sort determines component placement order.
* **Operating Systems:** Resource allocation graphs for deadlock detection.
* **Compiler Design:** Dependency graphs for instruction scheduling and optimization.
* **GPS Navigation:** Weighted graphs with edge costs as distances — Dijkstra's and A* algorithms derive directly from these representations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Breadth-First Search (BFS)

#### 3.1.1 Concept

**BFS** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores all vertices at the **current depth** before moving to vertices at the **next depth level**. It uses a **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) Queue** to track the frontier of exploration.

#### 3.1.2 BFS Algorithm (Pseudocode)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{BFS}(G, s): \\
&1.\ \text{For each vertex } v \in V: \\
&\quad \text{color}[v] \leftarrow \text{WHITE}; \quad \text{dist}[v] \leftarrow \infty; \quad \text{parent}[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL} \\
&2.\ \text{color}[s] \leftarrow \text{GRAY}; \quad \text{dist}[s] \leftarrow 0; \quad \text{parent}[s] \leftarrow \text{NIL} \\
&3.\ Q \leftarrow \emptyset; \quad \text{Enqueue}(Q, s) \\
&4.\ \text{While } Q \neq \emptyset: \\
&\quad u \leftarrow \text{Dequeue}(Q) \\
&\quad \text{For each } v \in \text{Adj}[u]: \\
&\quad\quad \text{If } \text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}: \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{color}[v] \leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{dist}[v] \leftarrow \text{dist}[u] + 1 \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{parent}[v] \leftarrow u \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{Enqueue}(Q, v) \\
&\quad \text{color}[u] \leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
&5.\ \text{Return}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.3 BFS Time Complexity Analysis

Let $n = \vert V \vert$ and $m = \vert E \vert$.

* **Initialization Loop:** Visits every vertex once → $O(n)$.
* **Enqueue/Dequeue:** Each vertex is enqueued and dequeued exactly once → $O(n)$.
* **Adjacency Examination:** For each vertex $u$, we examine all its neighbors. The total work across all vertices equals the sum of degrees, which is $2m$ for an undirected graph → $O(m)$.

**Total Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ — optimal for graph traversal.

**Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ — for the color, distance, parent arrays, and the queue.

#### 3.1.4 BFS Correctness Argument (Sketch)

BFS computes the **shortest path** (in number of edges) from source $s$ to every reachable vertex. This is because BFS explores vertices in **non-decreasing order of their distance from $s$**. The queue invariant — that elements are ordered by their `dist[]` value — is preserved by the algorithm.

---

### 3.2 Depth-First Search (DFS)

#### 3.2.1 Concept

**DFS** is a graph traversal algorithm that explores as **deep** as possible along each branch before backtracking. It uses a **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) Stack** (or recursion, which uses the call stack).

#### 3.2.2 DFS Algorithm (Pseudocode)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{DFS}(G): \\
&1.\ \text{For each vertex } v \in V: \\
&\quad \text{color}[v] \leftarrow \text{WHITE}; \quad \text{parent}[v] \leftarrow \text{NIL} \\
&2.\ \text{time} \leftarrow 0 \\
&3.\ \text{For each vertex } v \in V: \\
&\quad \text{If } \text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}: \\
&\quad\quad \text{DFS-Visit}(v) \\
& \\
&\text{DFS-Visit}(u): \\
&1.\ \text{color}[u] \leftarrow \text{GRAY} \\
&2.\ \text{time} \leftarrow \text{time} + 1; \quad d[u] \leftarrow \text{time} \quad \text{(discovery time)} \\
&3.\ \text{For each } v \in \text{Adj}[u]: \\
&\quad \text{If } \text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}: \\
&\quad\quad \text{parent}[v] \leftarrow u \\
&\quad\quad \text{DFS-Visit}(v) \\
&4.\ \text{color}[u] \leftarrow \text{BLACK} \\
&5.\ \text{time} \leftarrow \text{time} + 1; \quad f[u] \leftarrow \text{time} \quad \text{(finishing time)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.2.3 DFS Time Complexity Analysis

* **DFS-Visit is called once per vertex** → $O(n)$ for the calls themselves.
* **Inner loop scans adjacency lists.** The total work across all `DFS-Visit` calls equals the sum of degrees → $O(m)$.

**Total Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$.

**Space Complexity:** $O(n)$ — for color, parent, discovery, finish arrays, and the recursion stack (worst case $O(n)$ for a path graph).

#### 3.2.4 Parenthesis Theorem

For any two vertices $u$ and $v$, exactly one of the following three conditions holds:

1. The intervals $[d[u], f[u]]$ and $[d[v], f[v]]$ are **entirely disjoint** — neither is a descendant of the other in the DFS tree.
2. The interval $[d[u], f[u]]$ is **entirely contained** within $[d[v], f[v]]$ — $u$ is a descendant of $v$.
3. The interval $[d[v], f[v]]$ is **entirely contained** within $[d[u], f[u]]$ — $v$ is a descendant of $u$.

---

### 3.3 BFS vs DFS — The Comparative Matrix

| Parameter | BFS | DFS |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Structure** | Queue (FIFO) | Stack / Recursion (LIFO) |
| **Exploration Strategy** | Level-by-level (breadth) | Branch-by-branch (depth) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n + m)$ | $O(n + m)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ worst case |
| **Shortest Path** | Yes (unweighted) | No guarantee |
| **Back Edges** | Does not detect cycles in undirected directly | Detects via back edges |
| **Memory for Path** | Stores all neighbors at level | Stores one path at a time |
| **Completeness** | Complete (finds goal if exists) | Complete for finite graphs |
| **Optimality** | Optimal for unweighted graphs | Not optimal |
| **Applications** | Shortest path, bipartite check, level order | Topological sort, SCC, cycle detection, maze solving |

---

### 3.4 Strongly Connected Components (SCC)

#### 3.4.1 Definition

A **Strongly Connected Component** of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ is a **maximal** set of vertices $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair of vertices $u, v \in C$, there is a path from $u$ to $v$ and from $v$ to $u$.

The **SCC graph** (or condensation graph) $G^{SCC}$ contracts each SCC into a single vertex; this graph is always a **DAG**.

#### 3.4.2 Kosaraju's Algorithm (Two-Pass DFS)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Kosaraju-SCC}(G): \\
&1.\ \text{DFS}(G) \text{ to compute finishing times } f[u] \text{ for all } u \in V \\
&2.\ \text{Compute the transpose graph } G^T \text{ (reverse all edges)} \\
&3.\ \text{DFS}(G^T) \text{, but in the main loop, process vertices in} \\
&\quad \text{decreasing order of } f[u] \text{ from step 1} \\
&4.\ \text{Each tree in the DFS forest of step 3 is one SCC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ — two DFS traversals and one graph transpose.

**Intuition:** Kosaraju's algorithm exploits the fact that the DFS finishing order on $G$ gives a topological ordering of $G^{SCC}$. Running DFS on $G^T$ in reverse finishing order isolates each SCC as its own DFS tree.

#### 3.4.3 Correctness Justification

* **Lemma 1:** If $C$ and $C'$ are distinct SCCs and there is an edge $(u, v)$ with $u \in C$ and $v \in C'$, then the **maximum finishing time** in $C$ is **greater than** the maximum finishing time in $C'$.
* **Conclusion:** The SCCs of $G$ and $G^T$ are identical. Running DFS on $G^T$ in decreasing $f[u]$ order ensures that each DFS tree contains exactly one SCC.

---

### 3.5 Topological Sorting

#### 3.5.1 Definition

A **Topological Sort** of a DAG $G = (V, E)$ is a linear ordering of all vertices such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, vertex $u$ appears before $v$ in the ordering.

**Existence Condition:** A topological sort exists **if and only if** the graph is a DAG (no directed cycles).

#### 3.5.2 Kahn's Algorithm (BFS-Based)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Kahn-Topological-Sort}(G): \\
&1.\ \text{Compute in-degree } \text{indeg}[v] \text{ for all } v \in V \\
&2.\ \text{Initialize a queue } Q \text{ with all vertices having } \text{indeg}[v] = 0 \\
&3.\ \text{Initialize an empty list } L \\
&4.\ \text{While } Q \neq \emptyset: \\
&\quad u \leftarrow \text{Dequeue}(Q) \\
&\quad \text{Append } u \text{ to } L \\
&\quad \text{For each } v \in \text{Adj}[u]: \\
&\quad\quad \text{indeg}[v] \leftarrow \text{indeg}[v] - 1 \\
&\quad\quad \text{If } \text{indeg}[v] = 0: \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{Enqueue}(Q, v) \\
&5.\ \text{If } \vert L \vert < n: \text{ return ERROR (graph has a cycle)} \\
&6.\ \text{Return } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$.

#### 3.5.3 DFS-Based Topological Sort

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{DFS-Topological-Sort}(G): \\
&1.\ \text{DFS}(G) \text{ with discovery and finish times} \\
&2.\ \text{As each vertex is finished (turns BLACK), push it onto a stack} \\
&3.\ \text{Pop and output the stack to get the topological order}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intuition:** When a vertex $u$ finishes, all its descendants in the DFS tree have **already finished**. Thus, $u$ is appended to the order **after** all its descendants — exactly the reverse of the required order. The stack reversal produces the correct topological sequence.

**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sample Directed Graph (Worked Example Used Throughout)

Consider the directed graph $G = (V, E)$ with:
$$V = \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$
$$E = \{(0,1), (0,3), (1,2), (1,4), (2,5), (3,4), (4,5)\}$$

```mermaid
graph LR
    node0((0)) --> node1((1))
    node0 --> node3((3))
    node1 --> node2((2))
    node1 --> node4((4))
    node2 --> node5((5))
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
```

### 4.2 Adjacency List Representation (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph "Vertex Array (Header Nodes)"
        nodeA["Vertex 0"]
        nodeB["Vertex 1"]
        nodeC["Vertex 2"]
        nodeD["Vertex 3"]

    end
    subgraph "Adjacency Lists"
        list0["Adj[0]: 1 → 3"]
        list1["Adj[1]: 2 → 4"]
        list2["Adj[2]: 5"]
        list3["Adj[3]: 4"]
        list4["Adj[4]: 5"]
        list5["Adj[5]: (empty)"]
    end
    nodeA --- list0
    nodeB --- list1
    nodeC --- list2
    nodeD --- list3
    nodeE --- list4
    nodeF --- list5
```

### 4.3 BFS Traversal Flowchart (Starting from Vertex 0)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start BFS at vertex 0]) --> enqA[Enqueue 0]
    enqA --> checkA{Queue empty?}
    checkA -->|No| deqA[Dequeue 0]
    deqA --> visitA[Visit 0: dist = 0]
    visitA --> neighA[Neighbors of 0: 1, 3]
    neighA --> enqA1[Enqueue 1, 3]
    enqA1 --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| deqB[Dequeue 1]
    deqB --> visitB[Visit 1: dist = 1]
    visitB --> neighB[Neighbors of 1: 2, 4]
    neighB --> enqB1[Enqueue 2, 4]
    enqB1 --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| deqC[Dequeue 3]
    deqC --> visitC[Visit 3: dist = 1]
    visitC --> neighC[Neighbor 4 already enqueued]
    neighC --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| deqD[Dequeue 2]
    deqD --> visitD[Visit 2: dist = 2]
    visitD --> neighD[Neighbor 5]
    neighD --> enqD1[Enqueue 5]
    enqD1 --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| deqE[Dequeue 4]
    deqE --> visitE[Visit 4: dist = 2]
    visitE --> neighE[Neighbor 5 already enqueued]
    neighE --> checkA
    checkA -->|No| deqF[Dequeue 5]
    deqF --> visitF[Visit 5: dist = 3]
    visitF --> checkA
    checkA -->|Yes| endA([BFS Complete: 0,1,3,2,4,5])
```

### 4.4 DFS Traversal Flowchart (Starting from Vertex 0)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD([Start DFS at vertex 0]) --> d0[Visit 0: d 0 = 1]
    d0 --> rec1[Recurse to neighbor 1]
    rec1 --> d1[Visit 1: d 1 = 2]
    d1 --> rec2[Recurse to neighbor 2]
    rec2 --> d2[Visit 2: d 2 = 3]
    d2 --> rec3[Recurse to neighbor 5]
    rec3 --> d5[Visit 5: d 5 = 4]
    d5 --> f5[Finish 5: f 5 = 5]
    f5 --> back2[Backtrack to 2]
    back2 --> f2[Finish 2: f 2 = 6]
    f2 --> back1a[Backtrack to 1]
    back1a --> rec4[Recurse to neighbor 4]
    rec4 --> d4[Visit 4: d 4 = 7]
    d4 --> neigh4[Neighbor 5 already finished]
    neigh4 --> f4[Finish 4: f 4 = 8]
    f4 --> back1b[Backtrack to 1]
    back1b --> f1[Finish 1: f 1 = 9]
    f1 --> back0[Backtrack to 0]
    back0 --> rec6[Recurse to neighbor 3]
    rec6 --> d3[Visit 3: d 3 = 10]
    d3 --> neigh3[Neighbor 4 already finished]
    neigh3 --> f3[Finish 3: f 3 = 11]
    f3 --> back0f[Backtrack to 0]
    back0f --> f0[Finish 0: f 0 = 12]
    f0 --> endD([DFS Complete: Discovery and Finish Times Computed])
```

### 4.5 SCC Decomposition Using Kosaraju's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph G["Original Directed Graph G"]
        g0((0)) --> g1((1))
        g1 --> g2((2))
        g2 --> g0
        g0 --> g3((3))
        g3 --> g4((4))
        g4 --> g3
    end

        s1[C1: 0,1,2] --> s2[C2: 3,4]
    end

```

### 4.6 Topological Sort Execution Flow (Kahn's Algorithm)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    kstart([Start: Compute in-degrees]) --> kinit[Indeg: 0:0, 1:1, 2:1, 3:1, 4:2, 5:2]
    kinit --> kq[Queue = 0]
    kq --> kloop{Queue empty?}
    kloop -->|No| kd0[Dequeue 0 → Output: 0]
    kd0 --> kdec0[Decrement indeg of 1 and 3]
    kdec0 --> kadd1[1 and 3 now indeg 0 → Enqueue]
    kadd1 --> kloop
    kloop -->|No| kd1[Dequeue 1 → Output: 0,1]
    kd1 --> kdec1[Decrement indeg of 2 and 4]
    kdec1 --> kadd2[2 indeg 0 → Enqueue]
    kadd2 --> kloop
    kloop -->|No| kd3[Dequeue 3 → Output: 0,1,3]
    kd3 --> kdec3[Decrement indeg of 4]
    kdec3 --> kadd4[4 indeg 0 → Enqueue]
    kadd4 --> kloop
    kloop -->|No| kd2[Dequeue 2 → Output: 0,1,3,2]
    kd2 --> kdec2[Decrement indeg of 5]
    kdec2 --> kloop
    kloop -->|No| kd4[Dequeue 4 → Output: 0,1,3,2,4]
    kd4 --> kdec4[Decrement indeg of 5]
    kdec4 --> kadd5[5 indeg 0 → Enqueue]
    kadd5 --> kloop
    kloop -->|No| kd5[Dequeue 5 → Output: 0,1,3,2,4,5]
    kd5 --> kloop
    kloop -->|Yes| kend([Topological Order: 0, 1, 3, 2, 4, 5])
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define a graph. Distinguish between a directed graph and an undirected graph with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A graph $G = (V, E)$ is a non-linear data structure consisting of a set of vertices $V$ and a set of edges $E$ connecting pairs of vertices.

* **Undirected Graph:** Edges have no direction; $(u, v) = (v, u)$. Example: A friendship network on Instagram where the connection is mutual. (1.5 Marks)
* **Directed Graph (Digraph):** Edges are ordered pairs; $(u, v) \neq (v, u)$ in general. Example: A Twitter "follow" relationship where following is one-way. (1.5 Marks)

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State and explain the Handshaking Lemma for undirected graphs.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Handshaking Lemma states that the sum of the degrees of all vertices in an undirected graph is equal to twice the number of edges:
$$\sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2m$$
where $m = \vert E \vert$. **Proof intuition:** Every edge contributes exactly 1 to the degree of each of its two endpoints, hence the total degree sum counts every edge twice. (1.5 Marks)

**Corollary:** The number of vertices with odd degree is always even. (1.5 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of graphs. Compare their space and time complexities. (7 Marks)

**(b)** For the graph $G$ with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E\}$ and $E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (C,D), (D,E)\}$, perform BFS starting from vertex $A$. Show the queue state at every step and compute the BFS tree. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 Marks]

**Adjacency Matrix:** An $n \times n$ 2D array `A` where `A[i][j] = 1` if there is an edge between $i$ and $j$, else `0`. (1 Mark)

* **Space:** $O(n^2)$ — fixed regardless of edge count. (1 Mark)
* **Edge Query:** $O(1)$ via direct array indexing. (0.5 Mark)
* **Iterate Neighbors:** $O(n)$ per vertex. (0.5 Mark)
* **Best For:** Dense graphs. (0.5 Mark)

**Adjacency List:** An array of $n$ lists where the $i$-th list contains all neighbors of vertex $i$. (1 Mark)

* **Space:** $O(n + m)$ — proportional to actual edges. (1 Mark)
* **Edge Query:** $O(\deg(v))$ in the worst case. (0.5 Mark)
* **Iterate Neighbors:** $O(\deg(v))$. (0.5 Mark)
* **Best For:** Sparse graphs. (0.5 Mark)

**Summary Table:**

| Criterion | Adjacency Matrix | Adjacency List |
|---|---|---|
| Space | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n+m)$ |
| Edge Query | $O(1)$ | $O(\deg v)$ |
| Add Vertex | Costly | Easy |

**(b)** [7 Marks]

The graph adjacency lists are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Adj}[A] = \{B, C\} \\
&\text{Adj}[B] = \{A, D\} \\
&\text{Adj}[C] = \{A, D\} \\
&\text{Adj}[D] = \{B, C, E\} \\
&\text{Adj}[E] = \{D\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**BFS Trace (Starting from $A$):**

| Step | Dequeue | Queue State | Visited Set | BFS Tree Edges |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | — | $[A]$ | $\{A\}$ | — |
| 2 | $A$ | $[B, C]$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ | $(A,B), (A,C)$ |
| 3 | $B$ | $[C, D]$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | $(B,D)$ |
| 4 | $C$ | $[D]$ | $\{A, B, C, D\}$ | — ($D$ already visited) |
| 5 | $D$ | $[E]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | $(D,E)$ |
| 6 | $E$ | $[]$ | $\{A, B, C, D, E\}$ | — |

**BFS Order:** $A, B, C, D, E$
**BFS Tree Edges:** $\{(A,B), (A,C), (B,D), (D,E)\}$

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the DFS algorithm with a suitable pseudocode. Illustrate with a worked example. (7 Marks)

**(b)** What is a Strongly Connected Component (SCC)? Explain Kosaraju's algorithm to find SCCs in a directed graph. State its time complexity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** [7 Marks]

**DFS Algorithm:** Explores as deep as possible before backtracking. Uses a stack (or recursion). Tracks `color` (WHITE/GRAY/BLACK), `discovery time d[v]`, and `finish time f[v]`. (1 Mark)

**Pseudocode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{DFS-Visit}(u): \\
&1.\ \text{color}[u] \leftarrow \text{GRAY}; \quad d[u] \leftarrow ++\text{time} \\
&2.\ \text{For each } v \in \text{Adj}[u]: \\
&\quad \text{If } \text{color}[v] = \text{WHITE}: \\
&\quad\quad \text{parent}[v] \leftarrow u; \quad \text{DFS-Visit}(v) \\
&3.\ \text{color}[u] \leftarrow \text{BLACK}; \quad f[u] \leftarrow ++\text{time}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** Consider graph $V = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$, $E = \{(1,2), (2,3), (3,1), (2,4)\}$. (2 Marks)

| Vertex $u$ | Action | $d[u]$ | $f[u]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Start, recurse to 2 | 1 | 8 |
| 2 | Recurse to 3 | 2 | 7 |
| 3 | Recurse to 1 (already GRAY → back edge) | 3 | 4 |
| 3 | Back to 2, recurse to 4 | — | — |
| 4 | No unvisited neighbors | 5 | 6 |
| 2 | Finish, back to 1 | — | — |
| 1 | Finish | — | — |

**DFS Tree:** $\{(1,2), (2,3), (2,4)\}$; Back Edge: $(3,1)$. (1 Mark)
**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$. (1 Mark)

**(b)** [7 Marks]

**Definition:** A Strongly Connected Component is a maximal subset $C \subseteq V$ such that for every pair $u, v \in C$, there is a path $u \leadsto v$ and $v \leadsto u$. (1 Mark)

**Kosaraju's Algorithm — Three Steps:** (4 Marks)

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1.\ \text{Run DFS on } G; \text{ record finishing times } f[u]. \\
&2.\ \text{Compute } G^T \text{ (transpose: reverse all edges).} \\
&3.\ \text{Run DFS on } G^T \text{ processing vertices in DECREASING order of } f[u]. \\
&\quad \text{Each DFS tree is one SCC.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why it works:** In $G$, the SCC with the highest finish time has no outgoing edges to unvisited SCCs in $G^T$. Hence, DFS on $G^T$ isolates each SCC. (1 Mark)

**Time Complexity:** $O(n + m)$ — two DFS passes + one transpose. (1 Mark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Order of SCC Algorithm:** Many students transpose **first** and then run DFS — this is **WRONG**. Kosaraju's order is: DFS on $G$ → Transpose → DFS on $G^T$ in **decreasing finish time order**.
> 2. **Topological Sort Direction:** In Kahn's algorithm, decrementing in-degree should be done **after** outputting the vertex, not before.
> 3. **BFS vs DFS Discovery Time:** BFS uses `dist[]` (shortest path length), not discovery/finish times like DFS.
> 4. **Adjacency List Edge Count:** Forgetting that undirected graphs require **two entries** per edge in the adjacency list (one in each endpoint's list).
> 5. **Handshaking Lemma:** Applying it to directed graphs without splitting into in-degree and out-degree.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Graph Definition:** $G = (V, E)$ — a set of vertices and edges; $n = \vert V \vert$, $m = \vert E \vert$.
* **Three Representations:** Adjacency Matrix $O(n^2)$, Adjacency List $O(n + m)$, Incidence Matrix $O(nm)$.
* **Handshaking Lemma:** $\sum \deg(v) = 2m$ — applies only to undirected graphs.
* **BFS:** Uses **queue**, explores level-by-level, computes **shortest path** in unweighted graphs, $O(n + m)$.
* **DFS:** Uses **stack/recursion**, explores depth-first, tracks **discovery and finish times**, $O(n + m)$.
* **BFS Applications:** Shortest path, bipartite checking, connected components in undirected graphs.
* **DFS Applications:** Cycle detection, topological sort, strongly connected components, articulation points.
* **SCC:** Maximal set where every pair is mutually reachable; the SCC graph is a **DAG**.
* **Kosaraju's Algorithm:** DFS on $G$ → Transpose $G^T$ → DFS on $G^T$ in decreasing finish order. $O(n + m)$.
* **Topological Sort:** Linear ordering of a DAG such that all edges go from earlier to later vertices.
* **Kahn's Algorithm:** Repeatedly remove in-degree 0 vertices; uses BFS-style queue; $O(n + m)$.
* **DFS-based Topo Sort:** Output vertices in **reverse order of finish time**; uses stack.
* **Topological Sort Exists $\iff$ Graph is a DAG.**
* **Parenthesis Theorem:** DFS intervals are either disjoint or nested — never partially overlapping.
* **Edge Classification in DFS:** Tree, Back, Forward, and Cross edges.
* **Maximum Edges:** Undirected: $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$; Directed: $n(n-1)$.
* **DAG Property:** Every DAG has at least one vertex with in-degree 0 and one with out-degree 0.
* **Time Complexity of BFS/DFS:** Always $O(n + m)$ — independent of representation choice in asymptotic terms, but the **constant factor** is lower for adjacency lists on sparse graphs.

---
