<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 2: DIVIDE AND CONQUER PARADIGM

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Divide and Conquer (D&C)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Divide and Conquer (D&C)** is a fundamental algorithmic paradigm in which a problem is recursively broken down into **two or more sub-problems** of the same or related type, until these sub-problems become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then **combined** to give a solution to the original problem.

The paradigm operates on a strict three-step structural model that is universally applicable across all D&C algorithms analyzed in this module:

| Step | Name | Formal Purpose |
|------|------|----------------|
| **1. DIVIDE** | Partitioning | Decompose the instance of the problem into one or more smaller instances of the *same* problem. |
| **2. CONQUER** | Recursive Solving | Solve every sub-instance recursively. If a sub-instance is *small enough*, solve it by brute force in a direct manner. |
| **3. COMBINE** | Merging Results | Combine the solutions of the sub-instances to obtain the solution of the original instance. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Alignment (CO2, KTU 2024 Scheme):** This topic directly maps to **CO2: Analyze the complexity of algorithms using asymptotic notations and recurrence relations**, with specific Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels **L3 (Apply)** and **L4 (Analyze)**.

### 1.2 Control Abstraction — The Unified Skeleton

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Abstraction** is the generalized, parametric template of a D&C algorithm. It captures *what* every D&C procedure does without committing to *how* the divide and combine steps are performed for a specific problem. It is the "design pattern" of D&C.

**Formal Pseudocode (Control Abstraction):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Algorithm } D\&C(P) \\
&\textbf{if } \text{small}(P) == \text{true} \\
&\quad \textbf{then return } S(P) \\
&\textbf{else} \\
&\quad \text{Divide } P \text{ into smaller sub-instances } P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k \ (k \geq 1) \\
&\quad \textbf{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } k \\
&\quad \quad y_i \leftarrow D\&C(P_i) \\
&\quad \textbf{end for} \\
&\quad \textbf{return } \text{Combine}(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **P** is the input problem instance.
- **small(P)** is a boolean predicate that returns *true* when P is small enough to be solved directly.
- **S(P)** is the *brute-force* (base case) solution.
- **k** is the number of sub-problems (typically **2** in classical D&C).
- **Combine(...)** is problem-specific merge logic.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Sorting a Deck of Cards" Scenario:**
> Imagine you are handed a shuffled deck of **52 playing cards** and asked to sort it in ascending order using a fixed sorting strategy.
> 1. **DIVIDE:** You split the deck into two halves of **26 cards** each.
> 2. **CONQUER (Recursive):** You hand one half to a friend and the other half to another friend, each of whom applies the *same strategy* recursively — splitting, handing off, until they are sorting only **1 or 2 cards**, which is trivial.
> 3. **COMBINE:** When each friend returns a fully sorted half-deck, you perform a **merge** by comparing the top cards of both piles and placing the smaller one on top of a new pile.
> This is precisely the **Merge Sort** algorithm. The recursive nature and the clean three-stage structure are the defining DNA of every Divide and Conquer algorithm.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Time Complexity Notation:** Big-O $O(\cdot)$, Theta $\Theta(\cdot)$, Omega $\Omega(\cdot)$.
- **Standard Recurrence Form:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$.
- **Master Theorem Threshold Polynomials:** $n^{\log_b a}$.
- **Key Constants:** Base case size threshold is typically **$n \leq n_0$** where $n_0$ is a small constant (e.g., **1** or **2**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of D&C Sub-Topics

### 2.1.1 Finding Minimum and MAXIMUM Simultaneously

The classical *Min-Max* problem requires finding both the smallest and the largest element in an array $A[0 \ldots n-1]$ with the fewest comparisons.

- **Naive Approach:** $2(n-1)$ comparisons (one pass for min, one for max).
- **D&C Approach:** Recursively find min and max in two halves, then combine.

**Recurrence for Min-Max:**
$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
0, & n = 1 \\
1, & n = 2 \\
2T(n/2) + 2, & n > 2
\end{cases}
$$

**Solution:** $T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2$ comparisons (for $n$ a power of 2). This is the **optimal** comparison count for the general Min-Max problem.

### 2.1.2 MERGE SORT (Recursive Sorting via D&C)

- **DIVIDE:** Split the array $A[low \ldots high]$ into two halves at $mid = \lfloor (low+high)/2 \rfloor$.
- **CONQUER:** Recursively sort the left half $A[low \ldots mid]$ and the right half $A[mid+1 \ldots high]$.
- **COMBINE:** Merge the two sorted halves using an auxiliary array of size $n$.

**Recurrence for Merge Sort:**
$$
T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)
$$

**Solution by Master Theorem (Case 2):** $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

### 2.1.3 QUICKSORT (Partition-Based D&C)

- **DIVIDE:** Choose a **pivot** element $A[s]$ from $A[low \ldots high]$. Partition the array into two sub-arrays $A[low \ldots (q-1)]$ and $A[(q+1) \ldots high]$ such that:
  - Every element in the *left* partition is $\leq$ pivot.
  - Every element in the *right* partition is $\geq$ pivot.
- **CONQUER:** Recursively sort the two partitions.
- **COMBINE:** **No combine step is required** — partitions are in-place.

**Recurrences for Quicksort (Three Cases):**

| Case | Condition | Recurrence | Solution |
|------|-----------|------------|----------|
| **Best / Average** | Balanced partitions | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Worst** | Highly unbalanced (sorted input, bad pivot) | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| **Best (exact median)** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | — | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |

**Partition Schemes:**
1. **Lomuto Partition** (uses last element as pivot) — simpler, less efficient.
2. **Hoare Partition** (uses first element as pivot) — more efficient, fewer swaps.

### 2.1.4 STRASSEN'S MATRIX MULTIPLICATION

The classical matrix multiplication of two $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$ requires $\Theta(n^3)$ scalar multiplications. Strassen's algorithm (1969) reduces this using D&C.

- **DIVIDE:** Partition $A$ and $B$ into four $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ block matrices:
$$
A = \begin{bmatrix} A_{11} & A_{12} \\ A_{21} & A_{22} \end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} B_{11} & B_{12} \\ B_{21} & B_{22} \end{bmatrix}
$$
- **CONQUER:** Compute **7** sub-matrix products $M_1 \ldots M_7$ (instead of 8):
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22}) \\
M_2 &= (A_{21} + A_{22})B_{11} \\
M_3 &= A_{11}(B_{12} - B_{22}) \\
M_4 &= A_{22}(B_{21} - B_{11}) \\
M_5 &= (A_{11} + A_{12})B_{22} \\
M_6 &= (A_{21} - A_{11})(B_{11} + B_{12}) \\
M_7 &= (A_{12} - A_{22})(B_{21} + B_{22})
\end{aligned}
$$
- **COMBINE:** Reconstruct $C$ from the 7 products:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7 \\
C_{12} &= M_3 + M_5 \\
C_{21} &= M_2 + M_4 \\
C_{22} &= M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Recurrence for Strassen's:**
$$
T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Solution by Master Theorem (Case 1):** $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive formula sheet** for KTU Module 2 questions on Divide and Conquer.

| Algorithm | Recurrence Relation | Time Complexity | Space | Stable? |
|-----------|---------------------|-----------------|-------|---------|
| **Min-Max (D&C)** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$ | $\Theta(3n/2 - 2)$ | $O(\log n)$ | N/A |
| **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ all cases | $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary | **Yes** |
| **Quick Sort (Avg/Best)** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ stack | **No** |
| **Quick Sort (Worst)** | $T(n) = T(n-1) + \Theta(n)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n)$ stack | No |
| **Strassen's MM** | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | N/A |
| **Classical MM** | $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | N/A |

## 2.3 Master Theorem — Quick Reference (For Recurrences $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$. Let $c_{crit} = \log_b a$.

| Case | Condition | Solution | Algorithm Match |
|------|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{c_{crit} - \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}})$ | Strassen's ($c_{crit} = \log_2 7 \approx 2.807$) |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^k n)$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$ | Merge Sort, Quicksort best/avg |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{c_{crit} + \epsilon})$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | Classical MM |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Merge Sort** is the backbone of external sorting in databases (e.g., PostgreSQL, Hadoop) because of its stable, predictable $O(n \log n)$ behavior and its capacity to handle data larger than RAM via tape/disk-based merge.
- **Quicksort** is the default sort in most standard library implementations (e.g., C `qsort`, Java's `Arrays.sort` for primitives) due to its in-place memory profile and excellent cache performance.
- **Strassen's** is foundational to fast linear-algebra libraries (BLAS, LAPACK, NumPy internal kernels) and is the precursor to modern asymptotically faster algorithms like Coppersmith-Winograd.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Min-Max D&C Recurrence — Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$

**Step 1 — Base Verification.** For $n = 2$, the algorithm performs exactly one comparison to find both min and max. So $T(2) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Assume $n = 2^k$ for some integer $k \geq 1$.** This assumption holds for all $n$ that are exact powers of 2.

**Step 3 — Recursive Expansion.** Unroll the recurrence depth by depth.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2T(n/2) + 2 \\
&= 2\big[2T(n/4) + 2\big] + 2 \\
&= 4T(n/4) + 4 + 2 \\
&= 4\big[2T(n/8) + 2\big] + 6 \\
&= 8T(n/8) + 8 + 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the Pattern.** After $i$ levels of expansion:

$$
T(n) = 2^i \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^i}\right) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2^j \cdot 2
$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Series Evaluation.** The sum $\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2 \cdot 2^j = 2(2^i - 1) = 2^{i+1} - 2$.

**Step 6 — Termination Condition.** The recursion stops when $n/2^i = 2$, i.e., $i = \log_2 n - 1$.

Substituting $i = \log_2 n - 1$ and $T(2) = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2^{\log_2 n - 1} \cdot T(2) + 2^{(\log_2 n - 1) + 1} - 2 \\
&= 2^{\log_2 n - 1} \cdot 1 + 2^{\log_2 n} - 2 \\
&= \frac{n}{2} + n - 2 \\
&= \frac{3n}{2} - 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2 = \Theta(1.5n)$ comparisons. This is a **25% improvement** over the naive $2(n-1) = 2n - 2$ comparisons.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Merge Sort Recurrence — Solving $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$

**Step 1 — Assume** $T(1) = c$ and $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ for $n \geq 2$.

**Step 2 — Recursive Tree Method.** Visualize the recursion as a binary tree where:
- The **root** cost is $cn$ (the merge step at the top level).
- The **next level** has 2 nodes, each with cost $cn/2$, summing to $cn$.
- The **next level** has 4 nodes, each with cost $cn/4$, summing to $cn$.
- This pattern continues for $\log_2 n + 1$ levels (including the leaves).

**Step 3 — Sum the Per-Level Costs.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \underbrace{cn}_{\text{level } 0} + \underbrace{cn}_{\text{level } 1} + \underbrace{cn}_{\text{level } 2} + \ldots + \underbrace{cn}_{\text{level } (\log_2 n - 1)} + \underbrace{c \cdot n}_{\text{leaves}} \\
&= cn \cdot \log_2 n + cn
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Asymptotic Result.**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n \log_2 n)
$$

**Verification via Master Theorem:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn$. Critical exponent $c_{crit} = \log_2 2 = 1$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^1 \log^0 n)$, this matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$, yielding $T(n) = \Theta(n^{1} \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n)$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quicksort Worst-Case — Solving $T(n) = T(n-1) + cn$

**Step 1 — Recursion Tree Visualization.** Worst case occurs when the pivot is always the *smallest* or *largest* element, producing one sub-array of size $n-1$ and one of size $0$.

**Step 2 — Unroll Recurrence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(n-1) + cn \\
&= T(n-2) + c(n-1) + cn \\
&= T(n-3) + c(n-2) + c(n-1) + cn \\
&= T(1) + c\sum_{k=2}^{n} k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Summation.**

$$
\sum_{k=2}^{n} k = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1
$$

**Step 4 — Final Form.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T(1) + c\left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1\right] \\
&= T(1) + \frac{cn^2 + cn - 2c}{2} \\
&= \Theta(n^2)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Strassen's Algorithm — Solving $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + cn^2$

**Step 1 — Identify Master Theorem Parameters.** $a = 7$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn^2$.

**Step 2 — Compute Critical Exponent.**

$$
c_{crit} = \log_b a = \log_2 7
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Numerical Value.**

$$
\log_2 7 = \log_2 (2 \cdot 3.5) = 1 + \log_2 3.5 \approx 1 + 1.8074 = 2.8074
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Master Theorem Case 1.** Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{2.8074}$.

Since $f(n) = n^2 = O(n^{2.8074 - \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 0.8$, Case 1 applies:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})
$$

**Step 5 — Compare to Classical MM.**

$$
T_{classical}(n) = 8T(n/2) + cn^2 = \Theta(n^{\log_2 8}) = \Theta(n^3)
$$

**Speedup at large $n$:** For $n = 1000$, classical = $10^9$ ops; Strassen = $1000^{2.8074} \approx 4.6 \times 10^8$ ops — a **~2.18x speedup**, which grows with $n$.

---

## 3.5 Master Theorem Worked Example (Comprehensive)

Solve $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2 \log n$.

**Step 1.** Compute $c_{crit} = \log_2 4 = 2$.

**Step 2.** Examine $f(n) = n^2 \log n = \Theta(n^2 \log^1 n)$.

**Step 3.** This matches **Case 2** of the Master Theorem with $k = 1$.

**Step 4.** Solution:

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(n^2 \log^2 n)
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Algorithm | Comparisons/Mults | Best Case | Avg Case | Worst Case | Extra Space |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------|------------|-------------|
| Min-Max (D&C) | $1.5n - 2$ | $1.5n - 2$ | $1.5n - 2$ | $1.5n - 2$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Merge Sort | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ | $n \log n$ | $\Theta(n)$ |
| Quicksort | varies | $n \log n$ | $1.39 n \log n$ | $n^2/2$ | $O(\log n)$ |
| Classical MM | $n^3$ | $n^3$ | $n^3$ | $n^3$ | $O(n^2)$ |
| Strassen's MM | $n^{2.8074}$ | $n^{2.8074}$ | $n^{2.8074}$ | $n^{2.8074}$ | $O(n^2)$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Divide and Conquer Control Abstraction — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT PROBLEM INSTANCE P"] --> B{"IS P SMALL ENOUGH? smallP == true"}
    B -- "YES" --> C["APPLY BRUTE FORCE S(P)"]
    B -- "NO" --> D["DIVIDE STEP: Partition P into k sub-instances P1, P2 ... Pk"]
    D --> E["RECURSIVELY SOLVE: y1 = D and C of P1"]
    E --> F["RECURSIVELY SOLVE: y2 = D and C of P2"]
    F --> G["RECURSIVELY SOLVE: yk = D and C of Pk"]
    G --> H["COMBINE STEP: Merge y1, y2 ... yk into final result"]
    H --> I["RETURN FINAL COMBINED RESULT Y"]
    C --> I
    I --> J["OUTPUT SOLUTION"]
    
    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57C00,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Min-Max D&C Recursion Tree (for n = 8)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["MinMax on 8 elements | Cost = 2"]
    R --> L["MinMax on 4 elements L | Cost = 2"]
    R --> RT["MinMax on 4 elements R | Cost = 2"]
    L --> L1["MinMax on 2 elements | Cost = 1"]
    L --> L2["MinMax on 2 elements | Cost = 1"]
    RT --> R1["MinMax on 2 elements | Cost = 1"]
    RT --> R2["MinMax on 2 elements | Cost = 1"]
    L1 --> X["Base Case | Cost = 0"]
    L2 --> X
    R1 --> X
    R2 --> X
    
    style R fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px
    style RT fill:#C5CAE9,stroke:#283593,stroke-width:2px
    style L1 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px
    style L2 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px
    style R2 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px
    style X fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.3 Merge Sort Top-Down Recursion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Array of n elements"]
    S --> S1["Divide mid = floor low plus high over 2"]
    S1 --> SA["Recursive Sort on A low to mid"]
    S1 --> SB["Recursive Sort on A mid plus 1 to high"]
    SA --> SABase["Base Case n less than or equal 1 return"]
    SB --> SBBase["Base Case n less than or equal 1 return"]
    SABase --> MERGE["COMBINE STEP: Merge two sorted halves using auxiliary array"]
    SBBase --> MERGE
    MERGE --> OUT["Sorted Array Output"]
    
    style S fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F9A825,stroke-width:2px
    style SA fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style SB fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style MERGE fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#311B92,stroke-width:2px
    style SABase fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242
    style SBBase fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242
```

## 4.4 Quicksort Partition Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["Quicksort A low to high"]
    Q --> P["PARTITION STEP: Choose pivot A s"]
    P --> C{"Pivot selection strategy"}
    C -- "Lomuto" --> CL["Use last element as pivot | Two pointer scan"]
    C -- "Hoare" --> CH["Use first element as pivot | Two pointer inward scan"]
    CL --> COMP["Compare each element with pivot"]
    CH --> COMP
    COMP --> SP["Split into Left Partition less than or equal pivot and Right Partition greater than or equal pivot"]
    SP --> QL["Recursively quicksort Left Partition"]
    SP --> QR["Recursively quicksort Right Partition"]
    QL --> OUT["In place sorted output"]
    QR --> OUT
    
    style Q fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:2px
    style CL fill:#B39DDB,stroke:#4527A0
    style CH fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#004D40
    style COMP fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px
    style SP fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    style OUT fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.5 Strassen's Matrix Multiplication Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SM["Strassen Multiply A n by n times B n by n"]
    SM --> SD["DIVIDE: Partition A and B into 4 blocks of size n over 2 by n over 2"]
    SD --> SS["DIVIDE further until 1 by 1 base case reached"]
    SS --> SC["CONQUER: Compute 7 products M1 to M7 using addition and subtraction only"]
    SC --> S1["M1 = A11 plus A22 times B11 plus B22"]
    SC --> S2["M2 = A21 plus A22 times B11"]
    SC --> S3["M3 = A11 times B12 minus B22"]
    SC --> S4["M4 = A22 times B21 minus B11"]
    SC --> S5["M5 = A11 plus A12 times B22"]
    SC --> S6["M6 = A21 minus A11 times B11 plus B12"]
    SC --> S7["M7 = A12 minus A22 times B21 plus B22"]
    S1 --> SComb["COMBINE: Reconstruct C11 C12 C21 C22 from M1 to M7"]
    S2 --> SComb
    S3 --> SComb
    S4 --> SComb
    S5 --> SComb
    S6 --> SComb
    S7 --> SComb
    SComb --> SOUT["Output matrix C with Theta of n to the power log2 7 complexity"]
    
    style SM fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px
    style SD fill:#81D4FA,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px
    style SC fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px
    style SComb fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px
    style SOUT fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Divide and Conquer paradigm. Write the general control abstraction for D&C algorithms. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> Divide and Conquer is an algorithm design paradigm where a problem is divided into smaller sub-problems of the same type, each sub-problem is solved recursively, and the results are combined to obtain the final solution. The three stages are **Divide**, **Conquer**, and **Combine**.

**Control Abstraction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{D\&C}(P): \\
&\textbf{if } \text{small}(P) \textbf{ then return } S(P) \\
&\textbf{else} \\
&\quad \text{Divide } P \text{ into } P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k \\
&\quad \text{Apply D\&C to each } P_i \text{ to get } y_i \\
&\quad \textbf{return } \text{Combine}(y_1, \ldots, y_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Q2. State the Master Theorem for solving recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

> The Master Theorem compares $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$. Let $c_{crit} = \log_b a$.
> - **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{c_{crit} - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}})$.
> - **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^k n)$ for $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$.
> - **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{c_{crit} + \epsilon})$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Merge Sort and Its Analysis [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: Write the Merge Sort algorithm and illustrate its working on the array: 38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10.**

**Solution:**

**Algorithm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{MergeSort}(A, low, high): \\
&\textbf{if } low < high \textbf{ then} \\
&\quad mid \leftarrow \lfloor (low + high) / 2 \rfloor \\
&\quad \text{MergeSort}(A, low, mid) \\
&\quad \text{MergeSort}(A, mid+1, high) \\
&\quad \text{Merge}(A, low, mid, high)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step Trace (Splitting Phase):**

| Level | Subarrays |
|-------|-----------|
| 0 | [38, 27, 43, 3, 9, 82, 10] |
| 1 | [38, 27, 43, 3] [9, 82, 10] |
| 2 | [38, 27] [43, 3] [9, 82] [10] |
| 3 | [38] [27] [43] [3] [9] [82] [10] |

**Step-by-step Trace (Merging Phase):**

| Level | Subarrays |
|-------|-----------|
| 3 | [38] [27] [43] [3] [9] [82] [10] |
| 2 | [27, 38] [3, 43] [9, 82] [10] |
| 1 | [3, 27, 38, 43] [9, 10, 82] |
| 0 | **[3, 9, 10, 27, 38, 43, 82]** |

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Set up and solve the recurrence for Merge Sort. Show that it achieves $O(n \log n)$ in all cases.**

**Solution:**

The recurrence for Merge Sort is:

$$
T(n) = 
\begin{cases}
\Theta(1), & n = 1 \\
2T(n/2) + \Theta(n), & n > 1
\end{cases}
$$

where the $2T(n/2)$ term corresponds to sorting the two halves and $\Theta(n)$ corresponds to the merge step.

**Solving using the Master Theorem:**
- $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = \Theta(n)$.
- $c_{crit} = \log_2 2 = 1$.
- $f(n) = \Theta(n^1) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}})$, matching **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**Result:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^1 \log^{0+1} n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

This bound holds for the **best, average, and worst case** because the algorithm always performs the full merge regardless of input order.

---

### **Question B: Strassen's Matrix Multiplication [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]: State and explain Strassen's matrix multiplication algorithm. How is it different from the classical divide-and-conquer matrix multiplication?**

**Solution:**

**Classical D&C Matrix Multiplication:** Divides two $n \times n$ matrices into four $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ blocks and computes **8 recursive multiplications**.

$$
T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^3)
$$

**Strassen's Algorithm:** Performs only **7 recursive multiplications** (sacrificing 1 multiplication in exchange for 18 extra additions/subtractions).

**The 7 Strassen Products:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_1 &= (A_{11} + A_{22}) \cdot (B_{11} + B_{22}) \\
M_2 &= (A_{21} + A_{22}) \cdot B_{11} \\
M_3 &= A_{11} \cdot (B_{12} - B_{22}) \\
M_4 &= A_{22} \cdot (B_{21} - B_{11}) \\
M_5 &= (A_{11} + A_{12}) \cdot B_{22} \\
M_6 &= (A_{21} - A_{11}) \cdot (B_{11} + B_{12}) \\
M_7 &= (A_{12} - A_{22}) \cdot (B_{21} + B_{22})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Sub-blocks of $C$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &= M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7 \\
C_{12} &= M_3 + M_5 \\
C_{21} &= M_2 + M_4 \\
C_{22} &= M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Difference:** Classical uses 8 multiplications yielding $\Theta(n^3)$; Strassen uses 7 yielding $\Theta(n^{2.8074})$.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]: Derive the time complexity recurrence for Strassen's algorithm and solve it using the Master Theorem.**

**Solution:**

**Recurrence Setup:**
- Number of sub-problems: $a = 7$
- Size of each sub-problem: $n/2$, so $b = 2$
- Cost of matrix addition/subtraction on $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ matrices: $\Theta(n^2)$

**Recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Master Theorem Application:**

Step 1: Compute the critical exponent.

$$
c_{crit} = \log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.8074
$$

Step 2: Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{2.8074}$.

Since $n^2 = O(n^{2.8074 - 0.8074})$, **Case 1** of the Master Theorem applies.

Step 3: Write the solution.

$$
T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})
$$

**Conclusion:** Strassen's algorithm achieves a sub-cubic complexity of approximately $\Theta(n^{2.8074})$, which is asymptotically faster than the classical $\Theta(n^3)$ for sufficiently large $n$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Recurrence Setup Mistake:** Students often write the recurrence for Merge Sort as $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ without specifying that the merge cost depends on $n$ and not on $n-1$. Always state the cost of the combine step explicitly.
> 2. **Master Theorem Parameter Confusion:** Many students compute $\log_b a$ incorrectly. For Strassen's, the answer is $\log_2 7$, **not** $\log_7 2$. Remember: $b$ is the *sub-problem size divisor*; $a$ is the *number of sub-problems*.
> 3. **Strassen's Block Algebra:** When reconstructing $C_{11}, C_{12}, C_{21}, C_{22}$, students frequently swap signs (e.g., writing $C_{11} = M_1 + M_4 + M_5 + M_7$ instead of $M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7$). Memorize the exact formula.
> 4. **Quicksort Partition Misnaming:** In KTU exams, "Hoare's partition" is the standard. Using "Lomuto" without explicitly noting it may cost a half-mark. Default to Hoare unless told otherwise.
> 5. **Stability Statement:** Merge Sort is **stable**; Quicksort is **not stable**. This is a frequent 1-mark question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print-and-Carry)**

- **Divide and Conquer** has exactly 3 phases: **Divide → Conquer → Combine**.
- **Control Abstraction** is the parametric D&C template: it captures recursion with `small(P)`, `S(P)`, and `Combine(...)`.
- **Min-Max (D&C)** requires $\frac{3n}{2} - 2$ comparisons — provably optimal.
- **Merge Sort** recurrence is $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n)$ with solution $\Theta(n \log n)$ in **all cases**; uses $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space; is **stable**.
- **Quicksort** has **no combine step**; uses partition-in-place; $\Theta(n \log n)$ on average, $\Theta(n^2)$ in the worst case; not stable; uses $\Theta(\log n)$ stack space on average.
- **Pivot selection strategies** for Quicksort: first element, last element, random, median-of-three. Random or median-of-three is preferred to avoid worst case.
- **Strassen's algorithm** uses **7 sub-multiplications** (not 8) yielding $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.8074})$.
- **Master Theorem critical exponent** is $c_{crit} = \log_b a$ in the recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.
- **Case 1** of Master Theorem: divide cost dominates → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}})$.
- **Case 2** of Master Theorem: divide and combine costs are equal → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{c_{crit}} \log^{k+1} n)$.
- **Case 3** of Master Theorem: combine cost dominates → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- **Merge Sort** is preferred for **linked lists** and **external sorting**; **Quicksort** is preferred for **arrays** in-memory.
- **Strassen's** is impractical below $n \approx 32$ due to high constant overhead; the recursion switches to classical multiplication at small sizes.
- **Recursion tree method** can always be used as a fallback when Master Theorem does not apply.
- **Substitution method** (guess and verify by induction) is the most rigorous but time-consuming KTU approach.
- **Space complexity of recursion** = depth of recursion tree = $\Theta(\log n)$ for balanced D&C, $\Theta(n)$ for unbalanced (e.g., Quicksort worst case).
- **Stability**: Merge Sort = Stable; Quicksort = Unstable; Selection/Heap = Unstable; Insertion/Bubble = Stable.
- **Lower bound** for comparison-based sorting: $\Omega(n \log n)$ — Merge Sort and Quicksort both achieve this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Abstraction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control Abstraction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Abstraction** is a generalized algorithmic template (or *meta-algorithm*) in which the **control structure** (the *order* and *type* of high-level operations such as divide, conquer, combine, select, or backtrack) is **fixed and pre-defined**, while the **specific operations** that act on the problem data are **parameterized** (passed as arguments, procedures, or predicates). It separates the *logic of flow* from the *logic of computation*, enabling a single reusable skeleton to solve an entire family of related problems.

In the KTU 2024 scheme for *Algorithm Analysis and Design*, a control abstraction is introduced as the **conceptual bridge** between an algorithm design strategy (e.g., Divide and Conquer, Greedy Method, Dynamic Programming) and a concrete algorithm. The strategy supplies the *skeleton*; the algorithm supplies the *muscle*.

The three essential ingredients of any control abstraction are:

1. **A fixed control regime** — a predetermined sequence of high-level steps (loops, recursive calls, conditionals).
2. **A set of formal parameters** — symbolic placeholders for data (the *instance*) and operations (the *primitives*) to be supplied later.
3. **A return mechanism** — typically returning a *solution object* constructed from the parameter operations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cooking Recipe Template"

Imagine a culinary institute publishes a master recipe template:

> *"Take **Ingredients A**, mix with **Ingredients B**, cook at **Temperature T** for **Time D**, plate as **Garnish G**."*

- The **template** (mix → cook → plate) is the *control abstraction* — its order never changes.
- The **ingredients, temperature, time, and garnish** are *parameters* — they change with every dish.
- Whether you are baking a chocolate cake, a sourdough loaf, or a soufflé, the **control flow is identical**, only the **data and primitive operations** differ.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners often test whether you can recognize that seemingly different algorithms (Binary Search, Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Strassen's Multiplication) all share **one and the same Divide-and-Conquer control abstraction** with different primitive operations plugged in. Identifying the *shared skeleton* is worth full marks.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notations (used throughout this module)

- **Asymptotic upper bound:** **Big-O notation** $\mathcal{O}(\cdot)$
- **Asymptotic lower bound:** **Big-Omega notation** $\Omega(\cdot)$
- **Tight bound:** **Big-Theta notation** $\Theta(\cdot)$
- **Instance size:** $\mathbf{n}$
- **Number of sub-problems generated:** $\mathbf{a}$
- **Size of each sub-problem:** $\mathbf{n / b}$
- **Cost of divide + combine step:** $\mathbf{f(n)}$

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the symbols $\mathbf{a}$, $\mathbf{b}$, $\mathbf{f(n)}$ — they are the *parameters* of the generic recurrence that arises from control abstraction analysis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Control Abstraction

Every control abstraction in this module can be broken down into **four logical phases**:

1. **Instantiation Phase** — declare the formal parameters (problem instance, primitive operations, return object).
2. **Base / Terminal Phase** — handle the smallest instance directly (the *small(P)* test).
3. **Recursive / Iterative Body** — apply the fixed control regime to break the instance apart.
4. **Composition Phase** — merge sub-solutions into a final answer (or, for greedy, *union* the chosen candidate into the running solution).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The control regime is the *fixed choreography*; the primitives are the *dancers*. Changing dancers never alters the choreography.

### 2.2 The Three Reference Control Abstractions (Module 3 Focus)

#### (a) Divide and Conquer (D&C) Control Abstraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Algorithm } & \text{DAndC}(P) \\
\text{Input:} & \text{ A problem instance } P \\
\text{Output:} & \text{ A solution } S \text{ to } P \\
& \textbf{if } \text{Small}(P) == \text{true} \textbf{ then} \\
& \qquad \text{return } \text{Solution}(P) \\
& \textbf{else} \\
& \qquad (P_1, P_2, \dots, P_k) \leftarrow \text{Divide}(P) \\
& \qquad \text{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } k \textbf{ do} \\
& \qquad \qquad y_i \leftarrow \text{DAndC}(P_i) \\
& \qquad \text{return } \text{Combine}(y_1, y_2, \dots, y_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**The "Why" behind each step:**

- `Small(P)` — every recursive scheme needs a **halting predicate**; otherwise the recursion never bottoms out.
- `Divide(P)` — the *primitive* that splits one instance into $\mathbf{k}$ smaller ones. The size of each sub-problem is the *parameter* that varies per algorithm.
- `Solution(P)` — the **base case handler** — written once, reused for every leaf.
- `Combine(...)` — the *primitive* that fuses sub-solutions. This is where the algorithm's *character* lives (e.g., `MERGE` in Merge Sort, `CONQUER` in Strassen).

#### (b) Greedy Method Control Abstraction

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Algorithm } & \text{Greedy}(a, n) \\
\text{Input:} & \text{ Candidate set } a \text{ of size } n \\
\text{Output:} & \text{ A feasible solution } S \\
& S \leftarrow \emptyset \\
& \textbf{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } n \textbf{ do} \\
& \qquad x \leftarrow \text{Select}(a) \\
& \textbf{if } \text{Feasible}(x, S) \textbf{ then} \\
& \qquad S \leftarrow S \cup \{x\} \\
& \text{return } S
\end{aligned}
$$

**The "Why" behind each step:**

- `Select(a)` — the **greedy choice function** — picks the *locally* best remaining candidate. It is the *heart* of the algorithm.
- `Feasible(x, S)` — a **predicate** that checks whether adding $x$ to the partial solution $S$ keeps the solution *admissible* (e.g., within knapsack weight, before deadline, etc.).
- `Union` — appends the chosen candidate to the running solution set.

#### (c) Dynamic Programming Control Abstraction (Brief)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Algorithm } & \text{DP}(P) \\
& \text{Initialize a table } T[0 \dots m] \\
& \textbf{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } m \textbf{ do} \\
& \qquad T[i] \leftarrow \text{Recurrence}(T, i) \\
& \text{return } \text{Traceback}(T)
\end{aligned}
$$

The control regime is a **bottom-up table-filling loop**; the *primitive* is the **recurrence relation** that defines $T[i]$ in terms of smaller indices.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ (not bare pipes) inside LaTeX to keep Markdown tables intact.**

| # | Control Abstraction | Generic Recurrence / Cost | Key Parameters | KTU-Popular Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Divide and Conquer | $T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$ | $a, b, f(n)$ | Merge Sort, Binary Search |
| 2 | Greedy (single-pass) | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ with sort + linear scan | Select, Feasible | Job Sequencing with Deadlines |
| 3 | Greedy (Huffman) | $T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ via min-heap | Two-min extract + insert | Huffman Coding |
| 4 | Dynamic Programming | $T(n,m) = \sum \text{sub-problems}$ filled once | Recurrence, Table size | 0/1 Knapsack, LCS, Floyd–Warshall |
| 5 | Backtracking | Worst $\mathcal{O}(2^n)$; pruned by constraints | Constraint, Bound | N-Queens, Subset Sum |
| 6 | Branch and Bound | Worst $\mathcal{O}(b^d)$; reduced by bound | Lower bound estimate | Travelling Salesperson (LC) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler design** — control abstractions underpin *intermediate-representation lowering* (e.g., LLVM's `Instruction::Combine` mirrors `Combine` in D&C).
- **Parallel & distributed computing** — `MapReduce` is essentially the D&C control abstraction: *map* (Divide) → *shuffle* → *reduce* (Combine).
- **Hardware description (VLSI)** — greedy control abstractions govern **resource-constrained scheduling** in chip floor-planning.
- **Operations research** — every *matroid* or *greedoid* system is certified to obey the **greedy control abstraction** (Rado–Edmonds theorem).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Deriving the Generic Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence

The D&C control abstraction produces a recurrence of the form:

$$
T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)
$$

**Step-by-step justification of every term:**

1. **`Divide(P)`** splits the instance of size $n$ into $a$ sub-instances.
2. Each sub-instance has size $n/b$ (assume $b > 1$ for shrinkage).
3. The cost to *recurse* on all $a$ sub-instances is $a \cdot T(n/b)$.
4. The cost to *split* and *merge* (i.e., `Divide` + `Combine` + the for-loop overhead) is aggregated into $f(n)$.
5. Adding the recursive cost and the non-recursive cost gives the recurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When you are told *"solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n^2$ using the Master Theorem"*, the examiner is *implicitly* testing whether you can recognize this as the cost of a D&C control abstraction where $a=3$, $b=2$, and $f(n)=n^2$.

### 3.2 Worked Application 1 — Binary Search via the D&C Abstraction

Binary Search instantiates the D&C skeleton with:
- $a = 1$ (only the relevant half is recursed into).
- $b = 2$ (search space halves each time).
- `Divide` = compare key with mid.
- `Combine` = trivial (identity function — return the recursive result as-is).
- $f(n) = \mathcal{O}(1)$ (constant-time comparison).

**Deriving the recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = 1 \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \mathcal{O}(1)
$$

**Solving the recurrence** (using repeated substitution, since $a=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \\
     &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c + c \\
     &= T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^k}\right) + k \cdot c
\end{aligned}
$$

Halting occurs when $n / 2^k = 1$, i.e., $2^k = n \Rightarrow k = \log_2 n$.

$$
\therefore \quad T(n) = T(1) + c \log_2 n = \mathcal{O}(\log n)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway for KTU:** The *same* D&C skeleton gives $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ for Binary Search, $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ for Merge Sort, and $\mathcal{O}(n^{\log_2 7})$ for Strassen's — **only** the primitives (the parameters) differ.

### 3.3 Worked Application 2 — Merge Sort via the D&C Abstraction

Merge Sort instantiates the D&C skeleton with:
- $a = 2$, $b = 2$.
- `Divide` = split array at the midpoint (cost $\Theta(1)$).
- `Combine` = `MERGE` two sorted halves (cost $\Theta(n)$).
- $f(n) = \Theta(n)$.

**Recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n)
$$

**Solving via Recursion Tree (Master Theorem verification):**

- At each level $i$ of the tree, there are $2^i$ sub-problems, each of size $n/2^i$.
- Work per node at level $i$: $2^i \cdot c \cdot (n/2^i) = c n$.
- Number of levels: $\log_2 n$ (excluding the leaves).
- Total work over all non-leaf levels: $c n \cdot \log_2 n$.
- Leaf level: $n$ leaves, each $\Theta(1)$ → $\Theta(n)$ work.

$$
\therefore \quad T(n) = \Theta(n) + c n \log_2 n = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

### 3.4 Worked Application 3 — Greedy Control Abstraction for Job Sequencing with Deadlines

**Problem:** Given $n$ jobs, each with a profit $p_i$ and a deadline $d_i$ (deadline $\in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$), schedule jobs to maximize total profit. At most one job can run in any time slot.

**Instantiation of the Greedy Abstraction:**

| Greedy Primitive | Concrete Form |
|---|---|
| `Select(a)` | Pick the *unscheduled* job with the **highest profit** |
| `Feasible(x, S)` | A job with deadline $d_x$ is feasible iff there exists a free slot in $[1, d_x]$ |
| `Union` | Insert job $x$ into the *latest available* slot $\le d_x$ |

**Step-by-step cost derivation:**

1. **Sort jobs** by decreasing profit: $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ — *pre-processing*, outside the loop.
2. **Loop** over sorted jobs, $n$ iterations: $\mathcal{O}(n)$.
3. **Feasibility check** — find latest free slot using **Disjoint Set Union (DSU)** with path compression: $\mathcal{O}(\alpha(n))$ per call, where $\alpha$ is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively a constant).

$$
\therefore \quad T(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n) + n \cdot \mathcal{O}(\alpha(n)) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)
$$

### 3.5 Master Theorem Quick-Reference (for D&C control abstractions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU University Exam — memorize these three cases:**

$$
\text{If } f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon}\right) \text{ for some } \epsilon > 0, \text{ then } T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)
$$

$$
\text{If } f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right), \text{ then } T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \log n\right)
$$

$$
\text{If } f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon}\right) \text{ and } a \, f(n/b) \le c \, f(n) \text{ for some } c < 1, \text{ then } T(n) = \Theta(f(n))
$$

**Quick check examples for the exam:**

| Recurrence | $a$ | $b$ | $n^{\log_b a}$ | Case | $T(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $T(n)=2T(n/2)+n$ | 2 | 2 | $n$ | 2 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| $T(n)=4T(n/2)+n$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^2)$ |
| $T(n)=4T(n/2)+n^3$ | 4 | 2 | $n^2$ | 3 | $\Theta(n^3)$ |
| $T(n)=7T(n/2)+n^2$ | 7 | 2 | $n^{\log_2 7}$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ |

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Control Abstraction

| Problem Property | Recommended Abstraction | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Optimal substructure + overlapping sub-problems | Dynamic Programming | Sub-problems recur; cache results |
| Optimal substructure + **greedy choice property** | Greedy Method | Local optimum ⇒ global optimum |
| Sub-problems are **independent** | Divide and Conquer | No overlapping; recursion is safe |
| Feasibility with **backtracking on failure** | Backtracking | Need to undo choices |
| Combinatorial explosion + **bounding is possible** | Branch and Bound | Prune using lower/upper bounds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Divide-and-Conquer Control Abstraction — Recursive Flow

The diagram below maps the *fixed control regime* of the D&C abstraction, with explicit slots for the **parameterized primitives** (`Divide`, `Solution`, `Combine`).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Receive Problem P]) --> smallCheck{Small P ?}
    smallCheck -- YES --> baseCase["Solution of P (Base Case)"]
    baseCase --> returnA([Return Solution])
    smallCheck -- NO --> divideStep["Divide P into k sub-problems P1, P2, ..., Pk"]
    divideStep --> loopStart[For i = 1 to k]
    loopStart --> recurse["DAndC on Pi  (Recursive Call)"]
    recurse --> nextIter{i < k ?}
    nextIter -- YES --> recurse
    nextIter -- NO --> combineStep["Combine y1, y2, ..., yk"]
    combineStep --> returnA
    returnA([Return Final Result]) --> stopZ([End])

    style startA fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:2px
    style stopZ fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:2px
    style baseCase fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px
    style combineStep fill:#9d0208,color:#ffffff,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px
    style smallCheck fill:#ee9b00,color:#000000,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px
    style divideStep fill:#ee9b00,color:#000000,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px
    style recurse fill:#ee9b00,color:#000000,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **amber nodes** are *parameterized primitive slots* (the *what* that changes per algorithm).
- The **green base case** and **red combine step** are the two extremities of the recursion.
- The **navy ovals** mark entry and exit; the *flow* between them is the **fixed control regime**.

### 4.2 Greedy Method Control Abstraction — Iterative Selection Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start: Candidate Set a, Size n]) --> initS["Initialize Solution S = EMPTY"]
    initS --> loopB[For i = 1 to n]
    loopB --> selectStep["Select best candidate x from a (Greedy Choice)"]
    selectStep --> feasibleCheck{Feasible x with S ?}
    feasibleCheck -- YES --> unionStep["S = S UNION x"]
    feasibleCheck -- NO --> rejectStep[Discard x and Continue]
    unionStep --> incrCheck{i < n ?}
    rejectStep --> incrCheck
    incrCheck -- YES --> loopB
    incrCheck -- NO --> returnB([Return Solution S])
    returnB --> stopY([End])

    style startB fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:2px
    style stopY fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0b1f33,stroke-width:2px
    style selectStep fill:#ee9b00,color:#000000,stroke:#ca6702,stroke-width:2px
    style feasibleCheck fill:#bb3e03,color:#ffffff,stroke:#9d0208,stroke-width:2px
    style unionStep fill:#2d6a4f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1b4332,stroke-width:2px
    style rejectStep fill:#6a040f,color:#ffffff,stroke:#370617,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Mapping Algorithms to the D&C Skeleton

The block diagram below shows **how different concrete algorithms plug into the same D&C skeleton** by supplying different primitive operations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Skeleton["Fixed D&C Control Regime"]
        A1[Base Test] --> A2[Divide]
        A2 --> A3[Recursive Call]
        A3 --> A4[Combine]
    end

    subgraph BS["Binary Search"]
        B1["Divide: Compare key with mid"] -.-> A2
        B2["Combine: Return sub-result directly"] -.-> A4
    end

    subgraph MS["Merge Sort"]
        C1["Divide: Split at midpoint"] -.-> A2
        C2["Combine: MERGE two sorted halves"] -.-> A4
    end

    subgraph QS["Quick Sort"]
        D1["Divide: Partition around pivot"] -.-> A2
        D2["Combine: Concatenate partitions"] -.-> A4
    end

    subgraph ST["Strassen Multiplication"]
        E1["Divide: 4-block matrix split"] -.-> A2
        E2["Combine: 7-product formula"] -.-> A4
    end

    style Skeleton fill:#fefae0,stroke:#283618,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#283618,color:#ffffff
    style A2 fill:#bc6c25,color:#ffffff
    style A3 fill:#283618,color:#ffffff
    style A4 fill:#bc6c25,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **left column** (`BS`, `MS`, `QS`, `ST`) lists the *parameter sets* — i.e., the concrete *what* each algorithm supplies.
- The **middle** is the *unchanging skeleton* — the abstract *how*.
- The dashed arrows show that **the same D&C control regime is reused four times** with four different sets of primitives.

> [!TIP]
> **Visualize the answer this way in the exam** — examiners award full marks for *recognizing the abstraction*, not just for computing the recurrence.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

**Q1.** Define *control abstraction* in the context of algorithm design. List the **four essential ingredients** of any control abstraction and briefly state the role of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *control abstraction* is a generalized algorithm template in which the **control structure** (the order and type of high-level operations) is **fixed**, but the **specific operations** acting on the data are **parameterized**. The four ingredients are:

1. **Formal parameters** — placeholders for the problem instance and primitive operations. *(1 mark)*
2. **Base / Terminal condition** — predicate that detects the smallest instance and returns a direct solution, halting recursion. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Recursive / Iterative body** — the fixed sequence of high-level steps (divide, recurse, combine) that defines the algorithm family. *(1 mark)*
4. **Return mechanism** — assembles sub-solutions and returns the final answer. *(0.5 mark)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between the **Divide-and-Conquer** and **Greedy** control abstractions. State one problem solved optimally by each, and the corresponding recurrence / cost.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **D&C abstraction** solves a problem by *recursively splitting* it into independent sub-problems and combining their results. **Greedy** solves it by making a *sequence of irrevocable locally-optimal choices*.

- **D&C example:** Merge Sort — recurrence $T(n)=2T(n/2)+\Theta(n) \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Greedy example:** Huffman Coding — cost $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ using a min-heap; the greedy choice is to repeatedly merge the two lowest-frequency nodes. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive on D&C Control Abstraction

**(a)** Write the **generic Divide-and-Conquer control abstraction** in pseudocode. Identify the **parameter slots** (the parts that change from one concrete algorithm to another). For each slot, name the **concrete operation** used in: **(i) Binary Search**, **(ii) Merge Sort**, **(iii) Strassen's Matrix Multiplication**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The generic D&C control abstraction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \textbf{Algorithm} \text{ DAndC}(P) \\
& \textbf{if } \text{Small}(P) \textbf{ then return Solution}(P) \\
& \textbf{else} \\
& \qquad (P_1, \ldots, P_k) \leftarrow \text{Divide}(P) \\
& \qquad \textbf{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } k \textbf{ do } y_i \leftarrow \text{DAndC}(P_i) \\
& \qquad \textbf{return } \text{Combine}(y_1, \ldots, y_k)
\end{aligned}
$$

The **parameter slots** are: `Small`, `Solution`, `Divide`, and `Combine`. *(1 mark for naming slots.)*

| Algorithm | `Small(P)` | `Solution(P)` | `Divide(P)` | `Combine(...)` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary Search | $\vert P \vert \le 1$ | Return position of key in $P$ | Compare key with mid; recurse on left or right half (1 sub-problem, $k=1$) | Identity (return the recursive result as-is) |
| Merge Sort | $\vert P \vert \le 1$ | Return $P$ (trivially sorted) | Split array at midpoint into 2 halves ($k=2$) | `MERGE` the two sorted halves |
| Strassen's | Matrix is $1 \times 1$ | Return scalar | Split each $n \times n$ matrix into 4 blocks of $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ ($k=8$ sub-products, but 7 of them via 7 helpers) | 7-product Strassen formula to assemble the 4 result blocks |

*(6 marks for the table — 1.5 marks per row of three algorithms.)*

**(b)** Derive the **time-complexity recurrence** for Strassen's Matrix Multiplication as an instantiation of the D&C control abstraction. Using the **Master Theorem**, solve the recurrence and comment on the gain over the classical $\Theta(n^3)$ algorithm. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

Strassen's method replaces the 8 scalar multiplications of the classical divide-and-conquer with **7** scalar multiplications (using 18 additions/subtractions). The cost breakdown of the D&C skeleton is:

- **Number of sub-problems:** $a = 7$
- **Sub-problem size factor:** $b = 2$ (each sub-matrix is $n/2 \times n/2$)
- **Cost of divide + combine:** $f(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ (the 18 auxiliary matrix additions/subtractions are each $\Theta(n^2)$)

Hence the recurrence is:

$$
T(n) = 7 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^2)
$$

**Applying the Master Theorem:**

- $\log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.807$
- Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$.
- Since $n^2 = \mathcal{O}\!\left(n^{\log_2 7 - \epsilon}\right)$ with $\epsilon = 0.807 > 0$, **Case 1** applies.

$$
\therefore \quad T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_2 7}\right) \approx \Theta\!\left(n^{2.807}\right)
$$

**Gain over classical:** For large $n$, $n^{2.807} \ll n^3$. Concretely, for $n = 1024$, the ratio is approximately $1024^{0.193} \approx 4.7\times$ faster. *(1 mark for the gain comment.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive on Greedy Control Abstraction

**(a)** Write the **generic Greedy Method control abstraction** in pseudocode. Explain the role of each of the three core primitives — `Select`, `Feasible`, and `Union`. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The generic Greedy control abstraction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \textbf{Algorithm} \text{ Greedy}(a, n) \\
& S \leftarrow \emptyset \\
& \textbf{for } i \leftarrow 1 \textbf{ to } n \textbf{ do} \\
& \qquad x \leftarrow \text{Select}(a) \\
& \qquad \textbf{if } \text{Feasible}(x, S) \textbf{ then } S \leftarrow S \cup \{x\} \\
& \textbf{return } S
\end{aligned}
$$

- **`Select(a)`** *(2 marks)* — chooses the *locally best* candidate from the remaining set $a$. This is the **decision function** of the algorithm; it encodes the greedy criterion (e.g., *highest profit* in Job Sequencing, *shortest edge* in Prim's MST, *highest value-to-weight ratio* in Fractional Knapsack).
- **`Feasible(x, S)`** *(2 marks)* — a **boolean predicate** that checks whether appending candidate $x$ to the current partial solution $S$ keeps the solution *admissible* (e.g., within knapsack capacity, before deadline, no cycle formed).
- **`Union`** *(1.5 marks)* — appends the chosen, feasible candidate to $S$. In set-theoretic terms, $S \leftarrow S \cup \{x\}$; the data structure for $S$ may be a set, a list, a bitmask, or a heap, depending on the problem.
- The **overall control flow** (init → loop → return) is *fixed* across all greedy algorithms. *(1.5 marks for explaining the fixed flow and that primitives are parameterized.)*

**(b)** Apply the **Greedy control abstraction** to the **Huffman Coding problem**. Clearly state the choice of `Select`, the feasibility test, and derive the **time complexity** of the resulting algorithm. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Problem restatement:** Given an alphabet of $n$ characters with frequencies $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_n$, construct a prefix-free binary code minimizing the *weighted path length* $\sum_i f_i \cdot d_i$, where $d_i$ is the depth (length) of the code for character $i$.

**Instantiation of the Greedy Abstraction:**

| Primitive | Concrete Form |
|---|---|
| `Select(a)` | Extract the **two nodes with the minimum frequency** from the forest |
| `Feasible(x, S)` | Always `true` (merging two sub-trees never violates the prefix-free property) |
| `Union` | Create a new internal node whose frequency is the **sum** of the two minimums; make them its children; insert the new node back into the forest |

**Step-by-step execution** (high-level):

1. Initialize a **min-heap (priority queue)** $H$ with all $n$ leaf nodes, keyed by frequency. Cost: $\mathcal{O}(n)$ heapify. *(1 mark)*
2. Repeat until $H$ has exactly one node: *(3 marks)*
   - $x \leftarrow H.\text{extract-min}()$
   - $y \leftarrow H.\text{extract-min}()$
   - $z.\text{freq} \leftarrow x.\text{freq} + y.\text{freq}$
   - $H.\text{insert}(z)$
3. The remaining node is the **root** of the Huffman tree; the codes are the leaf-to-root paths.

**Time-complexity derivation:**

- The loop runs $n - 1$ times (each iteration reduces the heap size by 1).
- Each `extract-min` and `insert` costs $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
- Total cost:

$$
T(n) = (n-1) \cdot 2 \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log n) + \mathcal{O}(n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)
$$

*(2 marks for the derivation; 1 mark for the final bound.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes examiners penalize (KTU valuation):**
> 1. **Conflating `Select` with `Feasible`.** Many students write the *whole* greedy criterion inside `Select` and forget that `Feasible` is a *separate* boolean check. Marks are awarded for *both* primitives being correctly named and described.
> 2. **Forgetting the heap construction cost.** Writing $T(n) = (n-1) \cdot \mathcal{O}(\log n) = \mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ *without* the $\mathcal{O}(n)$ heapify term loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Using the same recurrence for *different* D&C algorithms without explaining the parameter change.** For Strassen's vs. classical matrix multiplication, students often write $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2)$ and *claim* this is Strassen's. It is not — Strassen's is **7**, not 8. This is a 2-mark deduction in the KTU answer key.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — read this 10 minutes before the exam.**

- **Control abstraction** = *fixed control flow* + *parameterized primitives*.
- The **three core control abstractions** in Module 3 are: **D&C**, **Greedy**, **Dynamic Programming** (with **Backtracking** and **Branch & Bound** as secondary).
- The **generic D&C recurrence** is $T(n) = a T(n/b) + f(n)$; identify $a$, $b$, $f(n)$ for every algorithm.
- The **generic Greedy abstraction** is: initialize $S = \emptyset$; loop { `Select`; if `Feasible` then `Union` }; return $S$.
- **Binary Search** → $T(n) = T(n/2) + \mathcal{O}(1) = \mathcal{O}(\log n)$.
- **Merge Sort** → $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.
- **Strassen's Multiplication** → $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + \Theta(n^2) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7})$ — a D&C instantiation, **not** a separate algorithm class.
- **Huffman Coding** → Greedy instantiation; cost $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ using a min-heap.
- **Master Theorem has three cases**; match the recurrence to the correct case based on $f(n)$ vs. $n^{\log_b a}$.
- **Greedy ≠ optimal for all problems.** Greedy works only when the problem satisfies the **greedy choice property** and **optimal substructure** simultaneously. Counter-example: 0/1 Knapsack is **not** solvable by greedy (use DP instead).
- **D&C assumes independent sub-problems.** If sub-problems overlap, use DP, not D&C.
- Always state the **base case** explicitly — a missing `Small(P)` check is a common 1-mark deduction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fractional Knapsack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fractional Knapsack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Fractional Knapsack Problem** is a classic combinatorial optimization problem in which a thief (or decision-maker) must select items—each with an associated weight $w_i$ and value (profit) $v_i$—to load into a knapsack of finite capacity $W$ such that the **total value accumulated is maximized** and the **total weight does not exceed $W$**. The defining feature is that items may be **broken into arbitrary fractions**, and a fraction of an item contributes a proportionally scaled value and weight.

Formally, let $x_i \in [0, 1]$ be the fraction of item $i$ selected. The optimization model is:

$$\text{Maximize } \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \, x_i$$

subject to the constraints:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \, x_i \leq W, \quad 0 \leq x_i \leq 1, \quad \forall i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The Fractional Knapsack Problem is taught under the **Greedy Algorithms** paradigm. It is the canonical example that proves a greedy strategy *can* yield an optimal solution when the underlying problem satisfies the **Greedy-Choice Property** and **Optimal Substructure**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a goldsmith packing a suitcase for an international trip. Your suitcase has a strict weight limit (say, **23 kg**). You have on the table several gold bars, each with a *different weight* and a *different market value*. 

You *could* take whole bars only — but what if the most valuable bar (per gram) is just too heavy to fit whole? In real life, you would simply **saw off a piece** of that bar to fill the remaining capacity, gaining exactly the proportional value. 

That is the heart of the **Fractional Knapsack Problem**: you are allowed to take slices of items, so the best strategy is always to **greedily pick the item that gives the highest value per unit weight first**. There is no reason to "save" the best item for later — you can always grab a fraction of it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Greedy-Choice Property** states that a globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice. Fractional Knapsack is the **textbook example** where this property holds, in stark contrast to the **0/1 Knapsack Problem**, where it does not.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Standard Metrics

For exam purposes, three quantities are central:

- **Profit-to-Weight Ratio:** $\rho_i = \dfrac{v_i}{w_i}$ — measures "value density".
- **Knapsack Capacity:** $W$ (units of weight, typically kg, g, or abstract units).
- **Fractional Take:** $x_i \in [0,1]$ — strictly bounded, never exceeding **1** (cannot take more than the whole item).

The **profit-to-weight ratio** is the single most important metric for this problem.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Greedy Works

The Fractional Knapsack algorithm relies on a simple but powerful idea: **always take the maximum possible quantity of the item with the highest value-per-unit-weight first**. Here is the structured logic:

1. **Compute Ratios:** For every item $i$, calculate $\rho_i = v_i / w_i$.
2. **Sort Descending:** Arrange items so that $\rho_1 \geq \rho_2 \geq \dots \geq \rho_n$.
3. **Iterate & Pack:** Walk through items in sorted order:
   - If the entire item fits ($w_i \leq W_{\text{remaining}}$), take it whole: $x_i = 1$.
   - Otherwise, take only the fraction that exactly fills the knapsack: $x_i = W_{\text{remaining}} / w_i$, then **stop**.
4. **Accumulate Value:** Sum $v_i \cdot x_i$ across all taken items.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** Suppose item $j$ has a lower ratio than item $k$. Replacing any portion of item $j$ in the knapsack with an equal weight of item $k$ strictly increases (or maintains) the total value. Therefore, an optimal solution must **never include weight from a "worse" item while leaving capacity unused that could have been filled by a "better" item**. This exchange argument proves optimality.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Mathematical Form | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Profit-to-Weight Ratio | $\rho_i = \dfrac{v_i}{w_i}$ | Sorting key; higher is better |
| 2 | Objective (Maximize) | $Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i$ | Total knapsack value |
| 3 | Capacity Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i \leq W$ | Cannot exceed capacity |
| 4 | Fractional Bound | $0 \leq x_i \leq 1$ | $x_i = 0$ (skip), $x_i = 1$ (whole), or $x_i \in (0,1)$ (partial) |
| 5 | Fraction of Last Item | $x_k = \dfrac{W_{\text{remaining}}}{w_k}$ | The item that fills the leftover capacity |
| 6 | Optimal Value | $Z^* = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} v_i + \rho_k \cdot W_{\text{remaining}}$ | Closed-form for sorted items |
| 7 | Time Complexity | $O(n \log n)$ | Dominated by sorting step |
| 8 | Space Complexity | $O(n)$ | Storing the sorted item list |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check (Exam Tip):** If weight is in kg and value in ₹ (rupees), then $\rho_i$ has units of **₹/kg**. Always include units in ratio computations when the problem provides explicit units — examiners award partial marks for unit awareness.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Fractional Knapsack model appears in numerous practical scenarios:

- **Resource Allocation in Cloud Computing:** Packing workloads of varying priority-per-CPU-cycle into a finite VM.
- **Cargo Loading (Aviation / Shipping):** When goods can be split across containers, maximizing revenue per ton.
- **Investment Portfolio Trimming:** Allocating a fixed budget across assets with varying returns-per-rupee, where fractional shares (mutual funds) are allowed.
- **Bandwidth Allocation in Networks:** Maximizing throughput by prioritizing packets with the highest value-per-byte.
- **Material Cutting in Manufacturing:** Cutting bars/rolls of material where partial pieces are commercially acceptable.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example

Consider **4 items** and a knapsack of capacity **$W = 10$**.

| Item $i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Value $v_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 2 | 10 |
| 2 | 3 | 5 |
| 3 | 4 | 15 |
| 4 | 5 | 7 |

### Step 1: Compute Profit-to-Weight Ratios

$$\rho_1 = \frac{v_1}{w_1} = \frac{10}{2} = 5.0$$

$$\rho_2 = \frac{v_2}{w_2} = \frac{5}{3} \approx 1.667$$

$$\rho_3 = \frac{v_3}{w_3} = \frac{15}{4} = 3.75$$

$$\rho_4 = \frac{v_4}{w_4} = \frac{7}{5} = 1.4$$

### Step 2: Sort Items by $\rho_i$ in Descending Order

The sorted order is: **Item 1 → Item 3 → Item 2 → Item 4**, with ratios $5.0 > 3.75 > 1.667 > 1.4$.

### Step 3: Iteratively Pack the Knapsack

Let $W_{\text{rem}}$ denote the remaining capacity, initialized as $W_{\text{rem}} = 10$. Let $Z$ denote the accumulated value, initialized as $Z = 0$.

**Iteration 1 — Pick Item 1 ($\rho = 5.0$, $w = 2$, $v = 10$):**

$$w_1 = 2 \leq W_{\text{rem}} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \text{take whole}$$

$$Z \leftarrow Z + v_1 = 0 + 10 = 10$$

$$W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow W_{\text{rem}} - w_1 = 10 - 2 = 8$$

**Iteration 2 — Pick Item 3 ($\rho = 3.75$, $w = 4$, $v = 15$):**

$$w_3 = 4 \leq W_{\text{rem}} = 8 \quad \Rightarrow \text{take whole}$$

$$Z \leftarrow Z + v_3 = 10 + 15 = 25$$

$$W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow W_{\text{rem}} - w_3 = 8 - 4 = 4$$

**Iteration 3 — Pick Item 2 ($\rho = 1.667$, $w = 3$, $v = 5$):**

$$w_2 = 3 \leq W_{\text{rem}} = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \text{take whole}$$

$$Z \leftarrow Z + v_2 = 25 + 5 = 30$$

$$W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow W_{\text{rem}} - w_2 = 4 - 3 = 1$$

**Iteration 4 — Try Item 4 ($\rho = 1.4$, $w = 5$, $v = 7$):**

$$w_4 = 5 > W_{\text{rem}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \text{take a fraction}$$

$$x_4 = \frac{W_{\text{rem}}}{w_4} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

$$Z \leftarrow Z + x_4 \cdot v_4 = 30 + 0.2 \times 7 = 30 + 1.4 = 31.4$$

$$W_{\text{rem}} \leftarrow 0 \quad \Rightarrow \text{STOP}$$

### Step 4: Final Answer

$$\boxed{Z^* = 31.4, \quad \text{Total Weight} = 10, \quad x = (1,\, 1,\, 1,\, 0.2)}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Verification via Exchange Argument

Suppose, for contradiction, an optimal solution $\mathcal{O}$ does **not** include the full weight of item 1 ($\rho = 5.0$, the highest ratio) while *not* being full. Then $\mathcal{O}$ must contain some weight from a lower-ratio item $j$, say $w'$ grams of it. Exchanging $w'$ grams of item $j$ with $w'$ grams of item 1 changes the value by:

$$\Delta Z = w' \cdot (\rho_1 - \rho_j) = w' \cdot (5.0 - \rho_j) > 0$$

The strict positivity holds because $\rho_1 > \rho_j$. This contradicts the optimality of $\mathcal{O}$. Hence any optimal solution **must** greedily prefer item 1.

By induction, this argument generalizes to all items, proving that the greedy algorithm is **provably optimal** for the Fractional Knapsack.

---

## 3.3 Algorithmic Pseudocode (Analysis Perspective)

```
FRACTIONAL-KNAPSACK(W, v[1..n], w[1..n]):
    for i = 1 to n:
        ratio[i] = v[i] / w[i]
    SORT items by ratio in descending order      // O(n log n)
    totalValue = 0
    remaining  = W
    for each item i in sorted order:
        if w[i] <= remaining:
            totalValue = totalValue + v[i]
            remaining  = remaining  - w[i]
        else:
            fraction = remaining / w[i]
            totalValue = totalValue + fraction * v[i]
            remaining  = 0
            BREAK
    return totalValue
```

### Complexity Analysis

$$\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \underbrace{O(n)}_{\text{ratio computation}} + \underbrace{O(n \log n)}_{\text{sorting}} + \underbrace{O(n)}_{\text{packing loop}} \\
&= O(n \log n)
\end{aligned}$$

The **sorting step dominates**, making $O(n \log n)$ the asymptotic time complexity. This is optimal in the comparison-based model because any algorithm that identifies the ordering of ratios requires $\Omega(n \log n)$ time in the worst case.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Greedy Execution Path)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1([Start])
    node2[Input: n items, values v_i, weights w_i, capacity W]
    node3[Compute ratio rho_i = v_i / w_i for all items]
    node4[Sort items in descending order of rho_i]
    node5[Initialize totalValue 0, remaining W]
    node6{Is remaining > 0 and items left?}
    node7{Is w_i less or equal remaining?}
    node8[Take whole item: totalValue = totalValue + v_i, remaining = remaining - w_i]
    node9[Take fraction: x = remaining / w_i, totalValue = totalValue + x times v_i, remaining = 0]
    node10[Output totalValue]
    node11([End])

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 -->|Yes| node7
    node6 -->|No| node10
    node7 -->|Yes| node8
    node7 -->|No| node9
    node8 --> node6
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node11
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[INPUT MODULE: Item list and capacity W]
    blockB[RATIO COMPUTATION UNIT: Calculate rho_i]
    blockC[SORTING ENGINE: Descending order by rho]
    blockD[GREEDY SELECTOR: Iterative capacity fitting]
    blockE[FRACTION HANDLER: Partial take logic]
    blockF[ACCUMULATOR: Running total value Z]
    blockG[OUTPUT MODULE: Optimal value Z star]

    blockA --> blockB
    blockB --> blockC
    blockC --> blockD
    blockD --> blockE
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockD
    blockD -->|Capacity exhausted| blockG
```

---

## 4.3 Greedy vs. Dynamic Programming — Decision Boundary

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Problem: Knapsack Selection])
    nodeB{Are fractions allowed?}
    nodeC{Is item divisibility continuous?}
    nodeD[Use GREEDY ALGORITHM]
    nodeE[Optimal in O of n log n]
    nodeF[Use DYNAMIC PROGRAMMING]
    nodeG[Optimal in O of n times W]
    nodeH[Approximation or heuristic]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB -->|Yes| nodeC
    nodeB -->|No| nodeF
    nodeC -->|Yes| nodeD
    nodeC -->|No| nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Schematic — Capacity Filling Visual

```
Knapsack Capacity W = 10
+----------------------+--------+
| Item 1   (w=2, ρ=5)  | Full   |  Z = 10
+----------------------+--------+
| Item 3   (w=4, ρ=3.75)| Full   |  Z = 25
+----------------------+--------+
| Item 2   (w=3, ρ=1.67)| Full   |  Z = 30
+----------------------+--------+
| Item 4 (w=5, ρ=1.4)  | x=0.2  |  Z = 31.4
+----------------------+--------+
|         Remaining = 0         |
+--------------------------------+
```
