Initialize: $\text{LPS} = [\_, \_, \_, \_, \_, \_]$, $len = 0$, $i = 1$. ### Iteration $i = 1$, $len = 0$ Compare $P[1] = \text{`B'}$ with $P[0] = \text{`A'}$ — **mismatch**, $len = 0$. $$ \text{LPS}[1] = 0,\quad len \text{ stays } 0 $$ Update: $\text{LPS} = [0, 0, \_, \_, \_, \_]$, $i \to 2$. ### Iteration $i = 2$, $len = 0$ Compare $P[2] = \text{`A'}$ with $P[0] = \text{`A'}$ — **match**! $$ \text{LPS}[2] = ++len = 1 $$ Update: $\text{LPS} = [0, 0, 1, \_, \_, \_]$, $len = 1$, $i \to 3$. ### Iteration $i = 3$, $len = 1$ Compare $P[3] = \text{`B'}$ with $P[1] = \text{`B'}$ — **match**! $$ \text{LPS}[3] = ++len = 2 $$ Update: $\text{LPS} = [0, 0, 1, 2, \_, \_]$, $len = 2$, $i \to 4$. ### Iteration $i = 4$, $len = 2$ Compare $P[4] = \text{`A'}$ with $P[2] = \text{`A'}$ — **match**! $$ \text{LPS}[4] = ++len = 3 $$ Update: $\text{LPS} = [0, 0, 1, 2, 3, \_]$, $len = 3$, $i \to 5$. ### Iteration $i = 5$, $len = 3$ Compare $P[5] = \text{`C'}$ with $P[3] = \text{`B'}$ — **mismatch**, $len = 3 > 0$. Apply fallback: $len = \text{LPS}[len-1] = \text{LPS}[2] = 1$. Re-test at $i = 5$, $len = 1$: Compare $P[5] = \text{`C'}$ with $P[1] = \text{`B'}$ — **mismatch**, $len = 1 > 0$. Apply fallback: $len = \text{LPS}[len-1] = \text{LPS}[0] = 0$. Re-test at $i = 5$, $len = 0$: Compare $P[5] = \text{`C'}$ with $P[0] = \text{`A'}$ — **mismatch**, $len = 0$. $$ \text{LPS}[5] = 0 $$ Update: $\text{LPS} = [0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 0]$, $i \to 6$, **terminates**. ### Final LPS Array $$ \text{LPS} = [0,\ 0,\ 1,\ 2,\ 3,\ 0] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation of each entry:** > - $\text{LPS}[0] = 0$ → single char "A" has no proper prefix equal to its suffix. > - $\text{LPS}[1] = 0$ → "AB": proper prefix is "A", suffix is "B" → no match. > - $\text{LPS}[2] = 1$ → "ABA": prefix "A" = suffix "A" → length 1. > - $\text{LPS}[3] = 2$ → "ABAB": prefix "AB" = suffix "AB" → length 2. > - $\text{LPS}[4] = 3$ → "ABABA": prefix "ABA" = suffix "ABA" → length 3. > - $\text{LPS}[5] = 0$ → "ABABAC": no proper prefix equals any suffix. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example — KMP Match on Text "ABABABCABABAC" Now match pattern $P = \text{``ABABAC''}$ against text $T = \text{``ABABABCABABAC''}$ ($n = 13$). Initialise $i = 0$, $j = 0$. | Step | $i$ | $j$ | $T[i]$ | $P[j]$ | Action | Result | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 0 | 0 | A | A | match | $i=1, j=1$ | | 2 | 1 | 1 | B | B | match | $i=2, j=2$ | | 3 | 2 | 2 | A | A | match | $i=3, j=3$ | | 4 | 3 | 3 | B | B | match | $i=4, j=4$ | | 5 | 4 | 4 | A | A | match | $i=5, j=5$ | | 6 | 5 | 5 | **B** | **C** | mismatch | $j = \text{LPS}[4] = 3$ | | 7 | 5 | 3 | B | **B** = $P[3]$ | match | $i=6, j=4$ | | 8 | 6 | 4 | **A** | **A** = $P[4]$ | match | $i=7, j=5$ | | 9 | 7 | 5 | **C** | **C** = $P[5]$ | match | $i=8, j=6$ | | 10 | 8 | 6 | — | — | $j == m$ | **MATCH at $i-j = 2$** | | 11 | — | — | — | — | continue | $j = \text{LPS}[5] = 0$ | | 12 | 8 | 0 | A | A | match | $i=9, j=1$ | | 13 | 9 | 1 | B | B | match | $i=10, j=2$ | | 14 | 10 | 2 | A | A | match | $i=11, j=3$ | | 15 | 11 | 3 | B | B | match | $i=12, j=4$ | | 16 | 12 | 4 | A | A | match | $i=13, j=5$ | | 17 | 13 | 5 | — | — | $i$ exhausted | terminate | **Result:** Pattern occurs **once** at position **2** (0-indexed) in the text. ### Counting Comparisons - Total character comparisons: each character of $T$ was compared at most twice. - LPS construction: **8 comparisons** for $m = 6$. - Matching: **17 comparisons** for $n = 13$. - **Total**: $25$ comparisons vs. brute force which would have done up to $13 \times 6 = 78$. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation — Why Total Time is $O(n + m)$ Let $T(n, m)$ be the total number of pointer operations performed by KMP. $$ T(n, m) = \underbrace{T_{\text{LPS}}(m)}_{\text{preprocessing}} + \underbrace{T_{\text{match}}(n, m)}_{\text{matching}} $$ **Claim 1: $T_{\text{LPS}}(m) \leq 3m$.** Let $k$ denote the number of times $len$ is incremented during LPS construction. Each increment corresponds to exactly one successful comparison, so successful comparisons $\leq m$. Each decrement of $len$ (via $\text{LPS}[len-1]$) corresponds to exactly one failed comparison, and the total decrease in $len$ over the entire run cannot exceed the total increase (since $len \geq 0$). Hence decrements $\leq m$, total comparisons $\leq 2m$, and with the outer loop overhead we get $T_{\text{LPS}}(m) = O(m)$. **Claim 2: $T_{\text{match}}(n, m) \leq 3n$.** Symmetric reasoning. The text pointer $i$ increments exactly $n$ times. Each increment of $j$ implies a successful comparison. Each decrement of $j$ via $\text{LPS}[j-1]$ implies a failed comparison, and the total drop in $j$ is bounded by the total rise, which is bounded by $n$. Hence $T_{\text{match}}(n, m) = O(n)$. $$ \boxed{T(n, m) = O(n) + O(m) = O(n + m)} $$ --- ## 3.4 Pseudocode (Reproducible in KTU Exam) ```text COMPUTE_LPS(P, m): lps[0] = 0 len = 0 i = 1 while i < m: if P[i] == P[len]: len = len + 1 lps[i] = len i = i + 1 else: if len != 0: len = lps[len - 1] else: lps[i] = 0 i = i + 1 return lps KMP_SEARCH(T, n, P, m): lps = COMPUTE_LPS(P, m) i = 0 // index into T j = 0 // index into P while i < n: if T[i] == P[j]: i = i + 1 j = j + 1 if j == m: print "Pattern found at index", i - j j = lps[j - 1] // continue for overlapping matches else: if j != 0: j = lps[j - 1] else: i = i + 1 ``` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 High-Level Block Architecture of KMP ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input Text T of length n] --> C[KMP Matcher Loop] B[Input Pattern P of length m] --> D[Compute LPS Array] D -->|lps[0..m-1]| C C -->|j == m| E[Report Match at position i minus j] C -->|j < m and mismatch| F[j becomes lps of j minus 1] F --> C E --> G[Reset j to lps of m minus 1 for overlapping search] G --> C C -->|i reaches n| H[Terminate and Output All Match Indices] ``` ## 4.2 LPS Computation Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: lps0 equals 0, len equals 0, i equals 1] --> Q{i less than m?} Q -->|No| Z[Return lps array] Q -->|Yes| R{P i equals P len?} R -->|Yes| M[len plus plus
lps i equals len
i plus plus] M --> Q R -->|No| T{len not equal to 0?} T -->|Yes| F[len equals lps of len minus 1] F --> R T -->|No| N[lps i equals 0
i plus plus] N --> Q ``` ## 4.3 KMP Matcher State Transition Diagram ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Init Init --> Compare: i equals 0 j equals 0 Compare --> Advance: T i equals P j Compare --> Fallback: T i not equal P j and j greater than 0 Compare --> SkipText: T i not equal P j and j equals 0 Advance --> MatchFound: j equals m Advance --> Compare: j less than m Fallback --> Compare: j becomes lps j minus 1 SkipText --> Compare: i becomes i plus 1 MatchFound --> Report: output i minus j Report --> Overlap: j becomes lps m minus 1 Overlap --> Compare Compare --> [*]: i reaches n ``` ## 4.4 Time-Complexity Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BruteForce[Brute Force Matcher] BF1[Shift pattern 1 position] --> BF2[Compare up to m chars] BF2 --> BF3[Total worst case: O of n times m] end subgraph KMPAlgo[KMP Matcher] K1[Precompute LPS array: O of m] --> K2[Single pass scan text: O of n] K2 --> K3[Total: O of n plus m] end BruteForce --- KMPAlgo ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4] **Define the failure function (LPS array) used in the KMP algorithm. State its time complexity for a pattern of length $m$.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **failure function** (also called the **prefix function** or **LPS array**) for a pattern $P[0 \dots m-1]$ is an array $\pi$ where $\pi[i]$ denotes the length of the **longest proper prefix of $P[0 \dots i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[0 \dots i]$**. Formally, $$ \pi[i] = \max\{\ell : 0 \le \ell \le i \text{ and } P[0 \dots \ell-1] = P[i-\ell+1 \dots i]\} $$ with $\pi[0] = 0$. It can be computed in $O(m)$ time using the iterative fallback rule $\ell = \pi[\ell-1]$. (3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for complexity.) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4] **Why is the KMP algorithm preferred over the brute-force string matching algorithm in the worst case? Give the worst-case complexities of both.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The brute-force matcher shifts the pattern by one position after every mismatch and re-checks up to $m$ characters, leading to a worst-case of $O(nm)$ comparisons (e.g., text = "AAAAAAAAAB", pattern = "AAAAAB"). The KMP algorithm precomputes the LPS array in $O(m)$ time, then uses it during matching to **shift the pattern by more than one position** without missing any potential match. The text pointer $i$ **never moves backwards**, so the matcher performs at most $2n$ comparisons in the matching phase. Total worst-case complexity = $O(n + m)$, which is linear and strictly better than $O(nm)$. (3 marks.) --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule:** *Each Module in the End Semester Examination carries 14 marks. You must answer **one full question (with sub-parts)** of 14 marks OR a combination that totals 14 marks from the module.* --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Past Pattern, Module 4] **(a)** Compute the **LPS (failure function) array** for the pattern $P = \text{"AABAAAB"}$. Show every step clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Using the LPS array obtained, run the **KMP matcher** on the text $T = \text{"AABAAAABAABAAAB"}$ and list all occurrences of the pattern. Show the values of $i$, $j$, and any fallback actions. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.A(a) — LPS Computation for P = "AABAAAB" (7 marks) $m = 7$. Initialise: $\text{LPS} = [\_,\_,\_,\_,\_,\_,\_]$, $len = 0$, $i = 1$. **Step 1** ($i=1$, $len=0$): $P[1]=\text{`B'}$, $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → mismatch, $len=0$. $\text{LPS}[1] = 0$. LPS = $[0,0,\_,\_,\_,\_,\_]$. $i \to 2$. **Step 2** ($i=2$, $len=0$): $P[2]=\text{`A'}$, $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → match. $len=1$, $\text{LPS}[2]=1$. LPS = $[0,0,1,\_,\_,\_,\_]$. $i \to 3$. **Step 3** ($i=3$, $len=1$): $P[3]=\text{`A'}$, $P[1]=\text{`B'}$ → mismatch, $len>0$. Fallback: $len = \text{LPS}[0] = 0$. Re-test ($i=3$, $len=0$): $P[3]=\text{`A'}$, $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → match. $len=1$, $\text{LPS}[3]=1$. LPS = $[0,0,1,1,\_,\_,\_]$. $i \to 4$. **Step 4** ($i=4$, $len=1$): $P[4]=\text{`A'}$, $P[1]=\text{`B'}$ → mismatch, $len>0$. Fallback: $len = \text{LPS}[0] = 0$. Re-test ($i=4$, $len=0$): $P[4]=\text{`A'}$, $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → match. $len=1$, $\text{LPS}[4]=1$. LPS = $[0,0,1,1,1,\_,\_]$. $i \to 5$. **Step 5** ($i=5$, $len=1$): $P[5]=\text{`A'}$, $P[1]=\text{`B'}$ → mismatch, $len>0$. Fallback: $len = \text{LPS}[0] = 0$. Re-test ($i=5$, $len=0$): $P[5]=\text{`A'}$, $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → match. $len=1$, $\text{LPS}[5]=1$. LPS = $[0,0,1,1,1,1,\_]$. $i \to 6$. **Step 6** ($i=6$, $len=1$): $P[6]=\text{`B'}$, $P[1]=\text{`B'}$ → match. $len=2$, $\text{LPS}[6]=2$. LPS = $[0,0,1,1,1,1,2]$. $i \to 7$. **Terminate.** #### Final LPS Array $$ \boxed{\text{LPS for "AABAAAB"} = [0,\ 0,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 1,\ 2]} $$ --- #### Solution to Q.A(b) — KMP Match on $T = \text{"AABAAAABAABAAAB"}$ (7 marks) $n = 15$, $m = 7$, $\text{LPS} = [0,0,1,1,1,1,2]$. Initialise $i=0$, $j=0$. | Step | $i$ | $j$ | $T[i]$ | $P[j]$ | Action | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1–6 | 0–5 | 0–5 | A,A,A,A,A,A | A,A,B,A,A,A | match through $j=5$ | | 7 | 6 | 6 | **A** | **B** = $P[6]$ | **mismatch**; $j = \text{LPS}[5] = 1$ | | 8 | 6 | 1 | A | A | match (re-compare, $i$ stays) | | 9 | 7 | 2 | **B** | **B** | match | | 10 | 8 | 3 | A | A | match | | 11 | 9 | 4 | A | A | match | | 12 | 10 | 5 | A | A | match | | 13 | 11 | 6 | **B** | **B** | match | | 14 | 12 | 7 | — | — | $j=m$ → **Match at index $12-7 = 5$** | | 15 | — | — | — | — | continue: $j = \text{LPS}[6] = 2$ | | 16 | 12 | 2 | A | B | mismatch; $j = \text{LPS}[1] = 0$ | | 17 | 12 | 0 | A | A | match | | 18 | 13 | 1 | A | A | match | | 19 | 14 | 2 | B | B | match | | 20 | 15 | 3 | — | — | $i=n$, terminate | **Result:** Pattern occurs **once** at position **5** (0-indexed). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Past Pattern, Module 4] **(a)** Explain the **Brute-Force string matching algorithm** with a suitable example. State its worst-case time complexity and the scenario in which it is achieved. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **KMP algorithm** with its key idea (failure function). Show the working of KMP on pattern $P = \text{"ABCABCAB"}$ and text $T = \text{"ABCABABCABCAB"}$. Report all matches. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a) — Brute-Force Matcher (7 marks) **Algorithm:** Slide the pattern $P$ over the text $T$ from left to right. At each shift $s \in [0, n-m]$, compare $T[s+k]$ with $P[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, \dots, m-1$. If all $m$ characters match, report a match at shift $s$. Otherwise, shift $s \leftarrow s+1$ and repeat. **Pseudocode:** ```text BRUTE_FORCE(T, n, P, m): for s = 0 to n - m: for k = 0 to m - 1: if T[s + k] != P[k]: break if k == m - 1 and T[s + k] == P[k]: print "Match at shift", s ``` **Example:** $T = \text{"ABCABCD"}$, $P = \text{"ABCD"}$. Matches at shifts $0$ and $3$. **Worst-case complexity:** $O(nm)$ — achieved when every shift forces $m$ comparisons (e.g., $T = \text{"AAAA…AB"}$ and $P = \text{"AAAA…AB"}$). The algorithm discards partial-match information on every failure. --- #### Solution to Q.B(b) — KMP on $P = \text{"ABCABCAB"}$ (7 marks) **Step 1 — Build LPS for "ABCABCAB"** ($m = 8$): | $i$ | $P[i]$ | $\text{LPS}[i]$ | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | A | 0 | base case | | 1 | B | 0 | no match with prefix | | 2 | C | 0 | no match | | 3 | A | 1 | matches prefix "A" | | 4 | B | 2 | matches prefix "AB" | | 5 | C | 3 | matches prefix "ABC" | | 6 | A | 4 | matches prefix "ABCA" | | 7 | B | 5 | matches prefix "ABCAB" | $$ \boxed{\text{LPS} = [0,0,0,1,2,3,4,5]} $$ **Step 2 — Match on $T = \text{"ABCABABCABCAB"}$** ($n = 13$): | $i$ | $j$ | Compare | Action | |---|---|---|---| | 0–4 | 0–4 | matches "ABCAB" | advance | | 5 | 5 | $T[5]=\text{`C'}$ vs $P[5]=\text{`C'}$ | match → $j=6$ | | 6 | 6 | $T[6]=\text{`A'}$ vs $P[6]=\text{`A'}$ | match → $j=7$ | | 7 | 7 | $T[7]=\text{`B'}$ vs $P[7]=\text{`B'}$ | match → $j=8$ | | — | — | $j=m$ | **Match at index $8-8 = 0$** | | 7 | $\text{LPS}[7]=5$ | re-compare | $T[7]=\text{`B'}$ vs $P[5]=\text{`C'}$ → mismatch | | 7 | $\text{LPS}[4]=2$ | re-compare | $T[7]=\text{`B'}$ vs $P[2]=\text{`C'}$ → mismatch | | 7 | $\text{LPS}[1]=0$ | re-compare | $T[7]=\text{`B'}$ vs $P[0]=\text{`A'}$ → mismatch | | 7 | $j=0$ | advance $i$ | $i=8$ | | 8–12 | 0–4 | matches "ABCAB" | advance | | — | $j=5$ at $i=13$? | $T[13]$ out of bounds | terminate | **Result:** Pattern occurs **once** at position **0** (the initial prefix). Total comparisons: $\approx 21$, far less than brute-force's worst-case $13 \times 8 = 104$. --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Valuation:** > 1. **Forgetting $\text{LPS}[0] = 0$:** A surprising number of students write $\text{LPS}[0] = 1$. The first character has **no proper prefix**, so it must be 0. > 2. **Confusing proper prefix with substring:** LPS counts only **prefixes that start at index 0**. Internal substrings do not count. > 3. **Moving $i$ backwards on mismatch:** KMP NEVER moves $i$ backwards. Students who write $i = i - 1$ on a mismatch are emulating brute force, not KMP. > 4. **Forgetting to reset $j$ after a match:** After reporting a match, you must set $j = \text{LPS}[m-1]$ to find overlapping occurrences. Missing this loses you the second match in patterns like "AAAA". > 5. **Not showing fallback chain:** When $P[i] \neq P[len]$ and $len > 0$, examiners **expect** you to show $len = \text{LPS}[len-1]$ and re-test. Skipping this step loses 1–2 marks per occurrence. > 6. **Complexity answer missing breakdown:** Always state **$O(m)$ preprocessing + $O(n)$ matching = $O(n+m)$ total**, not just "$O(n+m)$" — examiners reward the decomposition. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **⚡ RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST — KMP Algorithm** - ✅ **Problem:** Find all occurrences of pattern $P$ (length $m$) in text $T$ (length $n$). - ✅ **Brute-force worst case:** $O(nm)$ — no information reused after a mismatch. - ✅ **KMP key idea:** Precompute the **LPS (prefix function)** array so that on a mismatch, the pattern can be **safely shifted by the maximum amount** without missing any valid alignment. - ✅ **LPS definition:** $\text{LPS}[i]$ = length of the longest proper prefix of $P[0..i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[0..i]$. - ✅ **LPS base case:** $\text{LPS}[0] = 0$ always. - ✅ **LPS fallback rule:** when extending fails, $len = \text{LPS}[len-1]$ and re-test **without** advancing $i$. - ✅ **Matcher fallback rule:** when $T[i] \neq P[j]$ and $j > 0$, set $j = \text{LPS}[j-1]$ and **do not advance $i$**. - ✅ **Matcher invariant:** text pointer $i$ only moves forward; pattern pointer $j$ moves forward on success and backward on mismatch via LPS. - ✅ **Match found:** when $j == m$, report match at position $i - j$, then reset $j = \text{LPS}[m-1]$. - ✅ **Complexities:** LPS preprocessing = $O(m)$, matching = $O(n)$, **total = $O(n+m)$**. - ✅ **Space:** $O(m)$ for the LPS array. - ✅ **Correctness:** KMP never skips over a valid alignment — the LPS array encodes **all** borders of every prefix. - ✅ **Common exam variant:** "ABABCABAB", "AABAAAB", "ABCDABD", "AABAACAABAA" — memorise one trace. - ✅ **Comparison with related algorithms:** Naïve $O(nm)$, KMP $O(n+m)$, Rabin-Karp $O(nm)$ avg / $O(n+m)$ expected, Boyer-Moore $O(n/m)$ best (sub-linear), Z-Algorithm $O(n+m)$. - ✅ **Engineering uses:** DNA motif search, antivirus signature matching, plagiarism detection, lexer tokenisation.

topics.title: Rabin-Karp Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rabin-Karp Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rabin-Karp Algorithm** is a string-pattern matching algorithm that uses a **hashing-based technique** to find occurrences of a pattern $P[0..m-1]$ inside a text $T[0..n-1]$. It precomputes a **numerical hash value** of the pattern and a **rolling hash** of every length-$m$ substring (window) of the text. Hash collisions trigger a **direct character-by-character verification**, ensuring correctness while exploiting the average-case speed of hashing.

The algorithm is formally attributed to **Michael O. Rabin** and **Richard M. Karp** (1987) and is one of the foundational randomized / fingerprinting techniques taught in PEITT502 Module 4 (String Matching).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Rolling Fingerprints on a Conveyor Belt"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a long conveyor belt carrying letters that spell a book (the *text*). You are looking for the short word **"RING"** (the *pattern*) somewhere on that belt. Computing the exact match for every 4-letter window would mean stopping the belt and reading each window — slow!
>
> Instead, you stamp a unique **fingerprint (hash)** on the word "RING" once. Then for each sliding window on the belt, you stamp **only its fingerprint** (a single number) and compare fingerprints. Two fingerprints that differ ⇒ definitely no match. Identical fingerprints ⇒ *possible* match → only then do you stop and read letter-by-letter to confirm.
>
> The clever trick: instead of re-hashing the whole window each time, you **roll the fingerprint** forward by subtracting the outgoing letter, multiplying, and adding the incoming letter — much like wiping one fingerprint clean and stamping a fresh one on the next window in $O(1)$ time.

### 1.3 When Should Rabin-Karp Be Used?

| Scenario | Recommended Algorithm |
|---|---|
| Searching **one fixed pattern** in a large text | Naive / Rabin-Karp |
| Searching **one fixed pattern** with **expected linear** time | **Rabin-Karp** |
| Searching **multiple patterns simultaneously** | **Rabin-Karp** (extended to multi-pattern) |
| Worst-case guarantees required | KMP / Z-algorithm |
| Patterns contain wildcards / regex | Finite Automaton matcher |

### 1.4 Standard Constants Used

The Rabin-Karp implementation requires two carefully chosen constants:
- **d** — base (radix) of the alphabet, e.g., **d = 256** for extended ASCII or **d = 10** for decimal digits.
- **q** — a large **prime modulus**, e.g., **q = 13, 101, 997**. It keeps the hash in machine word range and prevents integer overflow.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle

The Rabin-Karp algorithm converts a *string comparison problem* into a *number comparison problem*:

1. **Preprocess the Pattern:** Compute a single integer hash $p = \text{hash}(P)$.
2. **Preprocess the First Window:** Compute $t_0 = \text{hash}(T[0..m-1])$.
3. **Slide and Compare:** For each shift $s = 0, 1, 2, \dots, n-m$:
   - If $p = t_s$ → **potential match** → verify character-by-character.
   - If $p \neq t_s$ → skip, roll the hash forward to obtain $t_{s+1}$ in $O(1)$.
4. **Output:** All verified shift positions.

### 2.2 Why Hashing Works (The "Why")

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hash instead of compare directly?**
> Comparing two integers of bounded size is $O(1)$ on a real machine, whereas comparing two $m$-character strings is $O(m)$. By replacing a string by a single bounded integer (its hash), we collapse the *expected* cost of "does this window match?" from $O(m)$ to $O(1)$ — but only on average, since two different strings can collide on the same hash (a **spurious hit**).

### 2.3 The Hash Function — Mathematical Form

For a string $S = s_0 s_1 \dots s_{m-1}$ over an alphabet of size $d$, the **positional hash** (used in CLRS / KTU recommended texts) is:

$$h(S) = \left( \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \text{num}(s_i) \cdot d^{\,m-1-i} \right) \bmod q$$

Equivalently, in Horner-style form:

$$h(S) = \left( \big( (s_0 \cdot d + s_1) \cdot d + s_2 \big) \cdots d + s_{m-1} \right) \bmod q$$

### 2.4 The Rolling Hash Recurrence

Given the hash $t_s$ of window $T[s \dots s+m-1]$, the next window's hash $t_{s+1}$ is obtained in **constant time** by:

$$t_{s+1} = \big( d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q$$

The constant multiplier is precomputed as:

$$h_{\text{base}} = d^{m-1} \bmod q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pitfall to remember (asked often in KTU):** When subtracting, the result can be **negative**. The standard fix is to add $q$ before taking mod: $t_{s+1} = ((d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot h_{\text{base}}) + T[s+m]) \bmod q + q) \bmod q$. Many students lose 1 mark for missing this.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $n$ | Length of text $T$ | Given |
| $m$ | Length of pattern $P$ | Given |
| $d$ | Base / radix of alphabet | $256$ for ASCII, $10$ for digits |
| $q$ | Prime modulus for hash | $13, 101, 997, \vert T \vert$ |
| $h_{\text{base}}$ | Precomputed $d^{m-1} \bmod q$ | $O(m)$ to compute once |
| $p$ | Hash of pattern | $O(m)$ |
| $t_s$ | Hash of $T[s..s+m-1]$ | $O(1)$ to roll |
| $t_{s+1}$ | Rolled hash | $(d(t_s - T[s]h_{\text{base}}) + T[s+m]) \bmod q$ |

| Complexity Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Preprocessing time** | $\Theta(m)$ |
| **Best case** (no hash collisions) | $\Theta(n+m)$ |
| **Average case** (uniform hashing) | $\Theta(n+m)$ |
| **Worst case** (e.g., $T = \text{"aaaa...a"}$, $P = \text{"aaa...a"}$) | $\Theta(nm)$ |
| **Auxiliary space** | $O(1)$ |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Plagiarism / duplicate-file detection** (Git, Dropbox, antivirus signature scanning): fingerprint each document chunk, compare fingerprints.
- **Network intrusion detection (Snort, ClamAV)**: match attack signatures against live packet streams.
- **Bioinformatics (BLAST-like tools)**: locate short motifs in DNA / protein sequences.
- **Database substring indexing** with rolling hash bucketing.
- **Rabin-Karp fingerprinting** in **Rabin-Karp based content-defined chunking** for deduplication.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rolling Hash Recurrence

Let window $W_s = T[s] T[s+1] \dots T[s+m-1]$ have hash:

$$t_s = \left( \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} T[s+i] \cdot d^{\,m-1-i} \right) \bmod q$$

Expanding the term containing the outgoing character $T[s]$:

$$t_s = \big( T[s] \cdot d^{m-1} + \underbrace{T[s+1] d^{m-2} + \dots + T[s+m-1] d^0}_{\text{remaining}} \big) \bmod q$$

Therefore the **"tail"** sum is:

$$\text{tail}_s = (t_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) \bmod q$$

To form the next window $W_{s+1} = T[s+1] \dots T[s+m]$, we must:
1. **Drop** the highest power of $d$ (because the leading character is gone).
2. **Shift left** by multiplying the entire tail by $d$.
3. **Append** the new character $T[s+m]$ as the new lowest digit.

This yields the **rolling recurrence**:

$$\boxed{\,t_{s+1} = \big( d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The whole expression is taken $\bmod q$ to keep numbers bounded. Because $t_s$ already lies in $[0, q-1]$, the inner subtraction can be negative; the textbook fix is **+q inside the mod**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Complete Walkthrough)

**Problem:** Find pattern $P = \text{"AB"}$ in text $T = \text{"CABDAB"}$ using the Rabin-Karp method.

**Alphabet mapping:** $A = 1,\ B = 2,\ C = 3,\ D = 4$.
**Parameters:** $m = 2,\ d = 10,\ q = 7$.

#### Step 1 — Compute $d^{m-1} \bmod q$

$$d^{m-1} = 10^{1} = 10 \quad\Rightarrow\quad h_{\text{base}} = 10 \bmod 7 = 3$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the pattern hash $p$

$$p = (1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 7 = 12 \bmod 7 = 5$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the hash of the first window $T[0..1] = \text{"CA"}$

$$t_0 = (3 \cdot 10 + 1) \bmod 7 = 31 \bmod 7$$

Since $31 = 4 \cdot 7 + 3$, we get $t_0 = 3$.

#### Step 4 — Slide the window. Comparison and rolling table

| Shift $s$ | Window $T[s..s+1]$ | Numeric | $t_s$ | $p$ vs $t_s$ | Action |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 0 | CA | $(3\cdot 10 + 1) = 31$ | 3 | $5 \neq 3$ | Roll |
| 1 | AB | $?$ | $?$ | — | Compute via roll |
| 2 | BD | $?$ | $?$ | — | Roll |
| 3 | DA | $?$ | $?$ | — | Roll |
| 4 | AB | $?$ | $?$ | — | Verify |

#### Step 5 — Roll the hash for shift $s = 0 \rightarrow 1$

$$t_1 = \big( d \cdot (t_0 - T[0] \cdot h_{\text{base}}) + T[2] \big) \bmod q$$

Substituting values:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= \big( 10 \cdot (3 - 3 \cdot 3) + 2 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (3 - 9) + 2 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (-6) + 2 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= (-60 + 2) \bmod 7 \\
&= -58 \bmod 7
\end{aligned}$$

Now $-58 \bmod 7$: we know $58 = 8 \cdot 7 + 2$, so $-58 \equiv -2 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$.

$$t_1 = 5$$

Verification by direct calculation: $T[1..2] = \text{"AB"} \Rightarrow (1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 7 = 12 \bmod 7 = 5$ ✓

Since $t_1 = 5 = p$, **possible match** → run char-by-char check: $T[1]=\text{'A'}=P[0]$, $T[2]=\text{'B'}=P[1]$ ✓ **Genuine match at shift 1.**

#### Step 6 — Roll the hash for shift $s = 1 \rightarrow 2$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_2 &= \big( 10 \cdot (5 - T[1] \cdot 3) + T[3] \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (5 - 1 \cdot 3) + 4 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot 2 + 4 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= 24 \bmod 7 = 3
\end{aligned}$$

$p = 5 \neq 3 \Rightarrow$ skip.

#### Step 7 — Roll the hash for shift $s = 2 \rightarrow 3$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_3 &= \big( 10 \cdot (3 - T[2] \cdot 3) + T[4] \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (3 - 2 \cdot 3) + 1 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (-3) + 1 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= -29 \bmod 7 = (-28 - 1) \bmod 7 = -1 \bmod 7 = 6
\end{aligned}$$

$p = 5 \neq 6 \Rightarrow$ skip.

#### Step 8 — Roll the hash for shift $s = 3 \rightarrow 4$

$$\begin{aligned}
t_4 &= \big( 10 \cdot (6 - T[3] \cdot 3) + T[5] \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (6 - 4 \cdot 3) + 2 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= \big( 10 \cdot (-6) + 2 \big) \bmod 7 \\
&= -58 \bmod 7 = 5
\end{aligned}$$

Verification by direct calculation: $T[4..5] = \text{"AB"} \Rightarrow (1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 7 = 5$ ✓

$t_4 = 5 = p$ → **possible match** → char-by-char verify: $T[4]=\text{'A'}=P[0]$, $T[5]=\text{'B'}=P[1]$ ✓ **Genuine match at shift 4.**

#### Step 9 — Final Result

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The pattern **"AB"** occurs in **"CABDAB"** at **shift positions $s = 1$ and $s = 4$**. Total cost: pattern hash + first window hash + 4 rolls + 2 verifications = $O(n+m) = O(8)$ operations, dramatically faster than naive's $O(nm) = O(12)$ in this case — and the gap widens for larger $n$.

### 3.3 Spurious Hit — A Worked Mini-Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **spurious hit** is a hash collision between $t_s$ and $p$ where the *strings do not actually match*. The algorithm must still verify character-by-character to filter these out.

**Tiny illustration:** $d=10,\ q=7$.

Pattern $P = \text{"11"} \Rightarrow p = (1\cdot 10 + 1) \bmod 7 = 11 \bmod 7 = 4$.
Window $T[0..1] = \text{"46"} \Rightarrow t_0 = (4\cdot 10 + 6) \bmod 7 = 46 \bmod 7 = 4$.

Both hashes equal **4**, but $P = \text{"11"} \neq \text{"46"}$ → spurious hit. Verification rejects it.

> **Engineering fix:** Choose $q$ as a **large prime** (e.g., $q \geq 10^9+7$) to make uniform collisions astronomically unlikely. Rabin-Karp then becomes a **Monte-Carlo** algorithm (assumed correct, not verified) — used in real plagiarism detectors.

### 3.4 Complexity Derivation Summary

Let $c$ denote the (small) cost of one full $m$-character verification. Then:

$$T(n, m) = \underbrace{O(m)}_{\text{pattern hash}} + \underbrace{O(m)}_{\text{first window hash}} + \underbrace{O((n-m) \cdot 1)}_{\text{rolling each step}} + \underbrace{c \cdot V}_{\text{verifications}}$$

where $V$ = number of hash matches. In the **average case** with good $q$, $V = O\!\left(\frac{n-m}{q}\right) + (\text{genuine matches}) = O(1)$, giving:

$$T_{\text{avg}}(n, m) = \Theta(n + m)$$

In the **worst pathological case** (e.g., $T = \text{"aaaa…a"}$, $P = \text{"aaa…a"}$), every window collides ⇒ $V = n - m + 1$, and:

$$T_{\text{worst}}(n, m) = \Theta(nm)$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Flow of Rabin-Karp

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input Text T of length n, Pattern P of length m]) --> inputCheck{Valid m less than or equal to n}
    inputCheck -- No --> errExit([Return No Match])
    inputCheck -- Yes --> prep1[Precompute h_base = d power m minus 1 mod q]
    prep1 --> prep2[Compute p = hash of P mod q]
    prep2 --> prep3[Compute t0 = hash of T first m chars mod q]
    prep3 --> initS[Initialize shift s equals 0]
    initS --> loopCheck{s less than n minus m plus 1}
    loopCheck -- No --> doneExit([End: Return all valid shifts])
    loopCheck -- Yes --> hashCompare{p equals t_s}
    hashCompare -- No --> skipBlock[Skip window]
    hashCompare -- Yes --> verifyBlock[Char by char verify P against T s to s plus m minus 1]
    verifyBlock --> verifyResult{All m chars match}
    verifyResult -- Yes --> recordBlock[Record shift s as valid match]
    verifyResult -- No --> skipBlock
    recordBlock --> skipBlock
    skipBlock --> rollBlock[Compute t s plus 1 using rolling recurrence]
    rollBlock --> incS[s equals s plus 1]
    incS --> loopCheck
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph preprocessingBlock [Preprocessing Stage]
        dirA[Text T of length n] --> h1T[Window Hash Unit]
        dirB[Pattern P of length m] --> h1T
        dirB --> h2P[Pattern Hash Unit]
        dirB --> hbCalc[Compute d power m minus 1 mod q]
        hbCalc --> h2P
        h1T --> h1Out[First Window Hash t0]
        h2P --> h2Out[Pattern Hash p]
    end

    subgraph searchBlock [Search Stage]
        h1Out --> cmpUnit[Hash Comparator]
        h2Out --> cmpUnit
        cmpUnit --> decision{Equal Hash}
        decision -- Yes --> verifier[Character Level Verifier]
        decision -- No --> roller
        verifier --> outputLog[Shift Recorder]
        outputLog --> roller[Rolling Hash Engine]
        h1Out --> roller
        hbCalc --> roller
        roller --> nextHash[t s plus 1]
        nextHash --> cmpUnit
    end

    outputLog --> finalResult([All Valid Shifts])
```

### 4.3 Memory & Data Movement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    txtBuf[(Text Buffer T index 0 to n minus 1)] --> win1[Window W0 T0 to T m minus 1]
    win1 --> hashMod[Hash + Mod q]
    hashMod --> reg1[(Register t_s)]
    reg1 --> shiftLeft[Shift Left by 1]
    shiftLeft --> win2[Window W s plus 1 T s plus 1 to T s plus m]
    win2 --> hashMod
    patBuf[(Pattern Buffer P)] --> patHash[Hash + Mod q]
    patHash --> regP[(Register p)]
    reg1 --> cmpEQ[Comparator]
    regP --> cmpEQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:**
> - The **preprocessing stage** sets up constants $p$ and $h_{\text{base}}$ in $O(m)$.
> - The **search stage** is a tight feedback loop: each iteration either verifies (on hash hit) or rolls (on miss). The rolling engine is the heart of Rabin-Karp — it converts an $O(m)$ recomputation into $O(1)$.
> - The **shaded loops** indicate where the algorithm spends ~95% of its time — exactly why the rolling recurrence is so important.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define the Rabin-Karp string matching algorithm. Mention its average-case and worst-case time complexities.**

**Model Answer:**
Rabin-Karp is a string-matching algorithm that uses **hashing** to locate a pattern $P[0..m-1]$ inside a text $T[0..n-1]$. It first computes the hash $p$ of the pattern, then iteratively computes the hash $t_s$ of every length-$m$ window of $T$. When $t_s = p$, a **direct character-by-character verification** is performed to confirm the match.

- **Average-case time:** $\Theta(n + m)$ — assuming a uniformly distributed hash.
- **Worst-case time:** $\Theta(nm)$ — occurs when many spurious hits arise (e.g., text and pattern are all identical).

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **What is a rolling hash function? Why is it central to the efficiency of Rabin-Karp?**

**Model Answer:**
A **rolling hash function** is a recurrence relation that, given the hash of the current window $W_s$, computes the hash of the next window $W_{s+1}$ in $O(1)$ time without re-hashing the entire new window:

$$t_{s+1} = \big( d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q$$

It is central to Rabin-Karp because it transforms what would otherwise be $O(m)$ work per window into $O(1)$ work, reducing total cost from $O(nm)$ to $O(n+m)$ on average. Without it, the algorithm loses its main advantage over naive matching.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Internal Choice Pattern

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]`

**(a)** Explain the Rabin-Karp string matching algorithm in detail. Clearly derive the rolling hash recurrence. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Goal:** Find all occurrences of pattern $P[0..m-1]$ in text $T[0..n-1]$.
2. **Choice of constants:** Alphabet base $d$ and prime modulus $q$ are chosen (e.g., $d=256$, $q=101$).
3. **Hash function:**
$$h(S) = \left( \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \text{num}(s_i) \cdot d^{m-1-i} \right) \bmod q$$
4. **Preprocessing:**
   - $p = h(P)$ in $O(m)$.
   - $t_0 = h(T[0..m-1])$ in $O(m)$.
   - Precompute $h_{\text{base}} = d^{m-1} \bmod q$.
5. **Search loop:** For each shift $s = 0, 1, \dots, n-m$:
   - If $t_s = p$, verify character-by-character (handles spurious hits).
   - Otherwise (or after verification), compute $t_{s+1}$ via the rolling recurrence.
6. **Derivation of the rolling recurrence:** The hash of window $W_s$ is
$$t_s = \left( T[s] \cdot d^{m-1} + \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} T[s+i] \cdot d^{m-1-i} \right) \bmod q$$
Subtracting the leading term and shifting left by $d$:
$$t_{s+1} = \big( d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[s+m] \big) \bmod q$$
7. **Complexity:** Preprocess $O(m)$, search $O(n)$ average (rolling) + $O(m)$ per verification.

**(b)** Apply the Rabin-Karp algorithm to find the pattern $P = \text{"ABAB"}$ in text $T = \text{"ABABCABAB"}$. Use $d = 10$ and $q = 13$. Show all hashing and rolling steps. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** $A=1,\ B=2,\ C=3$. So $P = [1, 2, 1, 2]$, $m = 4$, $n = 9$.

**Step 1 — Compute $h_{\text{base}}$:**
$$d^{m-1} = 10^3 = 1000,\quad h_{\text{base}} = 1000 \bmod 13$$
Since $13 \cdot 76 = 988$, $h_{\text{base}} = 1000 - 988 = 12$.

**Step 2 — Pattern hash:**
$$p = (1 \cdot 1000 + 2 \cdot 100 + 1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 13 = 1212 \bmod 13$$
$13 \cdot 93 = 1209$, so $p = 1212 - 1209 = 3$.

**Step 3 — First-window hash** $T[0..3] = \text{"ABAB"} = [1,2,1,2]$:
$$t_0 = (1 \cdot 1000 + 2 \cdot 100 + 1 \cdot 10 + 2) \bmod 13 = 1212 \bmod 13 = 3$$
$t_0 = p \Rightarrow$ **verify**: $A=A, B=B, A=A, B=B$ ✓ → **Match at $s=0$**.

**Step 4 — Roll** $s=0 \rightarrow s=1$:
$$t_1 = \big(10 \cdot (3 - T[0] \cdot 12) + T[4]\big) \bmod 13$$
$T[0]=1, T[4]=3$:
$$t_1 = \big(10 \cdot (3 - 12) + 3\big) \bmod 13 = \big(10 \cdot (-9) + 3\big) \bmod 13 = (-87) \bmod 13$$
$87 = 6 \cdot 13 + 9$, so $-87 \equiv -9 \equiv 4 \pmod{13}$. Thus $t_1 = 4$.
$p = 3 \neq 4$ → skip.

**Step 5 — Roll** $s=1 \rightarrow s=2$:
$$t_2 = \big(10 \cdot (4 - T[1] \cdot 12) + T[5]\big) \bmod 13$$
$T[1]=2, T[5]=1$:
$$t_2 = \big(10 \cdot (4 - 24) + 1\big) \bmod 13 = \big(10 \cdot (-20) + 1\big) \bmod 13 = (-199) \bmod 13$$
$199 = 15 \cdot 13 + 4$, so $-199 \equiv -4 \equiv 9 \pmod{13}$. Thus $t_2 = 9$.
$p = 3 \neq 9$ → skip.

**Step 6 — Roll** $s=2 \rightarrow s=3$:
$$t_3 = \big(10 \cdot (9 - T[2] \cdot 12) + T[6]\big) \bmod 13$$
$T[2]=1, T[6]=2$:
$$t_3 = \big(10 \cdot (9 - 12) + 2\big) \bmod 13 = \big(10 \cdot (-3) + 2\big) \bmod 13 = -28 \bmod 13$$
$28 = 2 \cdot 13 + 2$, so $-28 \equiv -2 \equiv 11 \pmod{13}$. Thus $t_3 = 11$.
$p = 3 \neq 11$ → skip.

**Step 7 — Roll** $s=3 \rightarrow s=4$:
$$t_4 = \big(10 \cdot (11 - T[3] \cdot 12) + T[7]\big) \bmod 13$$
$T[3]=2, T[7]=1$:
$$t_4 = \big(10 \cdot (11 - 24) + 1\big) \bmod 13 = \big(10 \cdot (-13) + 1\big) \bmod 13 = -129 \bmod 13$$
$129 = 9 \cdot 13 + 12$, so $-129 \equiv -12 \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. Thus $t_4 = 1$.
$p = 3 \neq 1$ → skip.

**Step 8 — Roll** $s=4 \rightarrow s=5$:
$$t_5 = \big(10 \cdot (1 - T[4] \cdot 12) + T[8]\big) \bmod 13$$
$T[4]=3, T[8]=2$:
$$t_5 = \big(10 \cdot (1 - 36) + 2\big) \bmod 13 = \big(10 \cdot (-35) + 2\big) \bmod 13 = -348 \bmod 13$$
$348 = 26 \cdot 13 + 10$, so $-348 \equiv -10 \equiv 3 \pmod{13}$. Thus $t_5 = 3$.
$t_5 = p \Rightarrow$ **verify** $T[5..8] = \text{"ABAB"}$: $A=A, B=B, A=A, B=B$ ✓ → **Match at $s=5$**.

**Final Answer:** Pattern **"ABAB"** occurs in **"ABABCABAB"** at shift positions $s = 0$ and $s = 5$.

---

> **Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare Rabin-Karp string matching with the naive string matching algorithm. Discuss best, average, and worst-case complexities with examples. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Naive Matching | Rabin-Karp |
|---|---|---|
| **Core idea** | Direct character comparison at every shift | Hash-based window screening + verification |
| **Preprocessing** | None | $O(m)$ to compute $p$, $t_0$, $h_{\text{base}}$ |
| **Best case** | $O(n)$ (first char always mismatches) | $O(n + m)$ (no collisions) |
| **Worst case** | $O(nm)$ (e.g., "AAAAA…A" pattern in identical text) | $O(nm)$ (every window collides — same pathological case) |
| **Average case** | $O(nm)$ | $O(n + m)$ for uniform hashing |
| **Space** | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ |
| **Multi-pattern support** | Poor | Excellent (multiple patterns can share precomputed hashes) |

**Example — Worst case for both:**
Text $T = \text{"AAAAAAAAAA"}$, Pattern $P = \text{"AAA"}$.

- **Naive:** At every shift, all 3 chars compare equal before the 4th char mismatches → ~3 char comparisons × 8 shifts = 24 ≈ $O(nm)$.
- **Rabin-Karp:** If $d = 10$, $q = 7$: every window's hash equals $p$ → all 8 windows trigger verification → 8 × 3 char checks = 24 ≈ $O(nm)$.

**Example — Rabin-Karp shines:**
Text $T = \text{"RANDOM…"}$, Pattern $P = \text{"RARE"}$ with $q = 997$: very few spurious hits → close to $O(n + m)$.

> **Key takeaway:** Rabin-Karp wins on **average** for general alphabets and is the **only** practical choice when matching **many patterns simultaneously**.

**(b)** What is a **spurious hit**? Show, with a small numerical example, how Rabin-Karp handles it. **\[7 Marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** A **spurious hit** occurs when the rolling hash of a window equals the pattern hash but the strings differ character-by-character. It is caused by **modular hash collisions**.

**Numerical example:**
- Pattern $P = \text{"26"}$, with $d = 10$, $q = 13$.
- Pattern hash: $p = (2 \cdot 10 + 6) \bmod 13 = 26 \bmod 13 = 0$.
- Text window $T[0..1] = \text{"91"}$: $t_0 = (9 \cdot 10 + 1) \bmod 13 = 91 \bmod 13 = 0$.

Here $t_0 = p = 0$, but $P = \text{"26"} \neq \text{"91"}$ → **spurious hit**.

**How Rabin-Karp handles it:**
When $t_s = p$, the algorithm does **not** declare a match. Instead, it runs the naive $O(m)$ character-by-character verification:

- Compare $T[s]$ vs $P[0]$: '9' vs '2' → mismatch → reject.

**How to minimize spurious hits:**
1. Choose $q$ as a **large prime** (e.g., $q \approx 10^9 + 7$).
2. Use **double hashing** with two independent moduli $q_1, q_2$ to virtually eliminate collisions.
3. In time-critical settings, accept Rabin-Karp as a **Monte-Carlo algorithm** (assume correct, skip verification) — used in real plagiarism / antivirus engines.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on Rabin-Karp questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the +q fix for negative intermediate values** in the rolling recurrence — KTU examiners *do* deduct 1 mark here. Always write:
>    $t_{s+1} = ((d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot h_{\text{base}}) + T[s+m]) \bmod q + q) \bmod q$.
> 2. **Confusing $d^{m-1}$ with $d^m$** in the rolling formula — the exponent is $m-1$, not $m$. Off-by-one errors are heavily penalized.
> 3. **Not precomputing $h_{\text{base}} = d^{m-1} \bmod q$** separately. Doing it inside the loop costs $O(m)$ per iteration, destroying the $O(n)$ rolling claim.
> 4. **Confusing best-case and average-case complexity.** Best case for naive is $O(n)$ (mismatch on first char), but average is $O(nm)$. Rabin-Karp's average is $O(n+m)$.
> 5. **Skipping the verification step** when $p = t_s$. Without verification, the algorithm is *incorrect* on standard definition (Monte-Carlo variant must be explicitly named).
> 6. **Numerical errors** in modular arithmetic — always show the final $\bmod q$ step clearly; partial credit is often given only if the working is visible.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Rabin-Karp is a **hashing-based** string matching algorithm that uses a **rolling hash** to slide a length-$m$ pattern window over the text in $O(1)$ per step.
- **Key Constants:** Choose a prime modulus $q$ (to minimize collisions) and base $d$ matching the alphabet size.
- **Hash Formula:** $h(S) = \left( \sum_{i=0}^{m-1} \text{num}(s_i) \cdot d^{m-1-i} \right) \bmod q$.
- **Rolling Recurrence:** $t_{s+1} = (d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot h_{\text{base}}) + T[s+m]) \bmod q$, where $h_{\text{base}} = d^{m-1} \bmod q$.
- **Verification Step:** Always compare characters when $p = t_s$ — this eliminates spurious hits.
- **Precompute $h_{\text{base}}$ once** — never recompute inside the loop.
- **Average Time:** $\Theta(n + m)$ — Rabin-Karp's main strength.
- **Worst Time:** $\Theta(nm)$ — pathological strings (e.g., all same character) cause many spurious hits.
- **Space:** $O(1)$ auxiliary.
- **Best For:** Multi-pattern search, plagiarism detection, network signature matching, bioinformatics motif search.
- **Comparison Anchors:**
  - vs **Naive**: same worst case, much faster on average.
  - vs **KMP / Z-algorithm**: Rabin-Karp has same average but better worst-case constant for multi-pattern; KMP/Z guarantee $O(n+m)$ worst case.
- **Monte-Carlo Variant:** With large $q$, verification can be skipped for speed — used in production systems.
- **Formula Sheet to Memorize for KTU:**
  - $h_{\text{base}} = d^{m-1} \bmod q$
  - $t_{s+1} = (d \cdot (t_s - T[s] \cdot h_{\text{base}}) + T[s+m]) \bmod q$
  - Time: Preprocess $\Theta(m)$, Search $\Theta(n)$ average, Worst $\Theta(nm)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tractable and Intractable Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Tractable and Intractable Problems ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Tractable Problems A problem is said to be **Tractable** if there exists an algorithm that solves the problem in **polynomial time**, i.e., in $O(n^k)$ for some constant $k$, where $n$ is the size of the input. Tractable problems are those that are computationally *feasible* to solve for reasonably large inputs on a modern computer. Formally, a problem $\Pi$ is tractable if: $$\exists \text{ an algorithm } A \text{ and a constant } k \geq 0 \text{ such that } T_A(n) = O(n^k)$$ where $T_A(n)$ is the worst-case running time of algorithm $A$ on input size $n$. ### 1.2 Formal Definition of Intractable Problems A problem is **Intractable** if there is **no algorithm** that can solve the problem in polynomial time. For intractable problems, the best-known algorithms require **exponential time** $O(c^n)$ or worse (such as $O(n!)$ or $O(n^n)$). As $n$ grows, the running time explodes, making these problems infeasible to solve for large inputs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > - **Tractable Problem** → Solvable in polynomial time $O(p(n))$. > - **Intractable Problem** → Cannot be solved in polynomial time; requires super-polynomial (often exponential) time. ### 1.3 Decision Problems vs. Optimization Problems | Aspect | Decision Problem | Optimization Problem | |---|---|---| | Output | **Yes / No** answer | Best/optimal solution | | Example | "Does a Hamiltonian cycle exist?" | "Find the shortest Hamiltonian cycle" | | Complexity | Easier to classify into P/NP | Reduces to decision form | > [!NOTE] > **Why Decision Problems Matter in KTU Examinations:** The theory of NP-completeness is built on **decision problems**, since the class NP is defined in terms of polynomial-time *verification* of YES instances. Every optimization problem can be converted to a corresponding decision problem. ### 1.4 The Class P (Polynomial Time) The complexity class **P** is the set of all **decision problems** that can be **solved** in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine. $$P = \{ L \mid \exists \text{ deterministic algorithm solving } L \text{ in } O(n^k) \text{ time} \}$$ ### 1.5 The Class NP (Nondeterministic Polynomial Time) The complexity class **NP** is the set of all **decision problems** for which a proposed "YES" certificate/solution can be **verified** in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine. $$NP = \{ L \mid \text{any "YES" instance has a certificate verifiable in } O(n^k) \text{ time}\}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** P = "easy to **solve**", NP = "easy to **verify**". Every problem in P is also in NP, so $P \subseteq NP$. ### 1.6 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition **Real-World Analogy — "The Treasure Hunt":** Imagine you are given a giant $100 \times 100$ maze and asked: - *Tractable problem (P):* "Find the **shortest path** from start to exit." — A clever algorithm like **BFS/Dijkstra** can solve this in polynomial time. - *Verifiable but possibly hard (NP):* "Someone claims a path of length $\leq 50$ exists. **Verify** their claim." — You can simply walk the path and check. Verification is easy even if finding it is hard. - *Intractable problem:* "Find a route that visits **all 100 cities** in the shortest possible distance (TSP)." — The number of possible routes is $99!$, which is astronomical. No known polynomial-time algorithm exists. **Another Intuition — Sudoku Puzzle:** - **P (Easy to solve):** Filling a $4 \times 4$ Sudoku. - **NP (Easy to verify):** Given a partially filled $9 \times 9$ Sudoku, checking if a proposed completed grid is valid takes only polynomial time. - **Intractable (Hard to solve):** Solving a general $n \times n$ Sudoku is NP-complete. ### 1.7 NP-Complete and NP-Hard Problems > [!IMPORTANT] > **NP-Complete Problems:** A decision problem $L$ is **NP-Complete** if: > 1. $L \in NP$ (it is in NP), **AND** > 2. Every problem in NP is polynomial-time **reducible** to $L$ (i.e., $L$ is NP-Hard). > > **NP-Hard Problems:** A problem is NP-Hard if every problem in NP polynomial-time reduces to it, but it may **not** be in NP itself. NP-Hard problems are at least as hard as any problem in NP. ### 1.8 Undecidable Problems Some problems are so hard that **no algorithm can solve them at all** — not even in exponential time. These are called **Undecidable Problems** (e.g., the **Halting Problem**). They lie beyond the realm of tractable/intractable classification. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Complexity Class Hierarchy The relationship between complexity classes can be visualized as a containment hierarchy: $$P \subseteq NP \subseteq NP\text{-Complete} \subseteq NP\text{-Hard}$$ $$P \subseteq NP \subseteq \text{Decidable Problems} \subseteq \text{All Problems}$$ ### 2.2 Logical Breakdown of the Hierarchy - **P ⊆ NP:** Every problem solvable in polynomial time can be verified in polynomial time (just solve it, then output the certificate). - **NP ⊆ NP-Hard:** By definition, every NP-Complete problem is both in NP and NP-Hard. - **P = NP? (Open Problem):** This is the most famous unsolved problem in computer science. If $P = NP$, then every problem whose solution can be quickly verified can also be quickly solved. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formal Definition | Polynomial Bound | Examples | |---|---|---|---| | **Tractable (P)** | Solvable in deterministic polynomial time | $O(n^k)$ | Sorting, MST, Shortest Path, BFS/DFS | | **NP** | Verifiable in polynomial time | $O(n^k)$ for verification | Hamiltonian Cycle, SAT, 3-SAT, TSP (decision) | | **NP-Complete** | In NP + NP-Hard | No known poly-time algorithm | SAT, 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, Graph Coloring, Subset Sum | | **NP-Hard** | At least as hard as any NP problem | Super-polynomial | TSP (optimization), Halting Problem | | **Undecidable** | No algorithm exists | None (non-computable) | Halting Problem, Post Correspondence | | Operation Type | Time Complexity | Classification | |---|---|---| | Matrix Multiplication (naive) | $O(n^3)$ | Tractable | | Strassen's Algorithm | $O(n^{2.807})$ | Tractable | | Fibonacci (dynamic) | $O(n)$ | Tractable | | Tower of Hanoi | $O(2^n)$ | Intractable | | Brute-force TSP | $O(n!)$ | Intractable | | Brute-force Subset Sum | $O(2^n)$ | Intractable | | Polynomial Complexity Class | Verification Cost | Solvability Cost | |---|---|---| | P | $O(n^k)$ | $O(n^k)$ | | NP | $O(n^k)$ | Unknown (possibly exponential) | | NP-Complete | $O(n^k)$ | Unknown — believed exponential | | NP-Hard | Not necessarily in NP | Believed super-polynomial | ### 2.4 Polynomial-Time Reduction (The Heart of NP-Completeness) A **polynomial-time reduction** from problem $A$ to problem $B$, written as $A \leq_p B$, is a transformation such that: 1. The transformation runs in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. 2. $A$'s YES instance $\iff$ $B$'s YES instance (i.e., the answer to $A$ is YES iff the answer to transformed $B$ is YES). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Reductions Matter:** If $A \leq_p B$ and $B$ is in P, then $A$ is also in P. Equivalently, if $A$ is NP-Complete and $A \leq_p B$ with $B \in NP$, then $B$ is also NP-Complete. This is the standard method to **prove** NP-Completeness of a new problem. ### 2.5 How to Prove a Problem is NP-Complete (Cook's Recipe) **Step-by-step KTU-ready proof technique:** 1. Show that the problem $L$ belongs to NP (provide a polynomial-time verification algorithm). 2. Choose a known NP-Complete problem $L'$ (e.g., SAT, 3-SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle). 3. Construct a polynomial-time reduction $L' \leq_p L$. 4. Conclude that $L$ is NP-Complete. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of Tractable/Intractable Theory | |---|---| | **Cryptography** | RSA security relies on factoring being (likely) intractable. | | **Operations Research** | Vehicle routing, supply chain — TSP is NP-Hard; heuristics used. | | **AI / Machine Learning** | SAT solvers power program verification and planning. | | **Bioinformatics** | Sequence alignment, protein folding — many NP-Hard problems. | | **Network Design** | VLSI circuit design uses NP-Complete graph partitioning. | | **Compiler Design** | Register allocation reduces to graph coloring (NP-Complete). | ### 2.7 Cook's Theorem (1971) > [!NOTE] > **Cook's Theorem:** The **Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)** is NP-Complete. This was the first-ever NP-Completeness proof, establishing SAT as the "ancestor" of all NP-Complete problems. All other NP-Completeness proofs reduce SAT (or 3-SAT) to the target problem. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Verifying that Hamiltonian Cycle is in NP We need to show that if a **certificate** (proposed Hamiltonian cycle) is given, it can be **verified** in polynomial time. **Problem:** Given an undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ with $\vert V \vert = n$ vertices, does there exist a Hamiltonian cycle (a cycle that visits every vertex exactly once and returns to the start)? **Verification Algorithm:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Input:} \quad & G = (V, E), \text{ and a certificate } C = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n) \\ \text{Output:} \quad & \text{YES if } C \text{ is a valid Hamiltonian cycle, else NO} \\ \\ \text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Check that } C \text{ contains all } n \text{ vertices exactly once} \rightarrow O(n \log n) \\ \text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Check that each consecutive pair } (v_i, v_{i+1}) \in E \text{ for } i = 1 \ldots n-1 \rightarrow O(n) \\ \text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Check that } (v_n, v_1) \in E \text{ (closing the cycle)} \rightarrow O(1) \\ \text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{If all checks pass, return YES; else return NO} \\ \\ \text{Total Verification Time:} \quad & O(n) + O(n \log n) = O(n^2) \quad \text{(polynomial)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Hamiltonian Cycle $\in$ NP, because verification runs in polynomial time $O(n^2)$. ### 3.2 Derivation: Reduction from 3-SAT to Vertex Cover (Proving Vertex Cover is NP-Hard) **Setup:** - **3-SAT:** Given a Boolean formula in CNF with $m$ clauses of 3 literals each, is it satisfiable? - **Vertex Cover (Decision):** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and an integer $k$, does $G$ have a vertex cover of size $\leq k$? **Reduction Construction:** Given a 3-SAT instance with variables $x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$ and clauses $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_m$, construct a graph $G$ as follows: $$\begin{aligned} \text{For each variable } x_i: \quad & \text{Create a pair of vertices } (x_i, \overline{x_i}) \text{ connected by an edge} \\ \text{For each clause } C_j = (\ell_{j1} \lor \ell_{j2} \lor \ell_{j3}): \quad & \text{Create a triangle of 3 vertices, one per literal} \\ & \text{Connect each literal vertex to the corresponding variable vertex} \\ \\ \text{Resulting Graph:} \quad & G \text{ has } 2n + 3m \text{ vertices} \\ & k = n + 2m \text{ (target vertex cover size)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Correctness Argument:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{3-SAT is satisfiable} \iff & \text{There is a truth assignment making each clause true} \\ \iff & \text{For each variable pair, choose the true literal (covers the variable edge)} \\ & \text{(this contributes } n \text{ vertices to the cover)} \\ \iff & \text{For each clause triangle, at least 2 vertices can be left uncovered} \\ & \text{(since at least one literal is true, pick the other 2 in the triangle)} \\ & \text{(this contributes } 2m \text{ vertices to the cover)} \\ \iff & \text{Vertex cover of size } n + 2m = k \text{ exists} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Polynomial Time of Reduction:** The reduction takes $O(n + m)$ time — we create a fixed number of vertices/edges per variable and clause. Hence: $$3\text{-SAT} \leq_p \text{Vertex Cover} \quad \text{in polynomial time}$$ **Conclusion:** Since 3-SAT is NP-Complete (proven by reduction from SAT), and Vertex Cover $\in$ NP, we conclude **Vertex Cover is NP-Complete**. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Common NP-Complete Problems and Their Sources | Problem | Source Reduction | Application Domain | |---|---|---| | **SAT** | Cook's Theorem (1971) — original | Logic, AI | | **3-SAT** | Reduction from SAT | Constraint satisfaction | | **Hamiltonian Cycle** | Reduction from 3-SAT | Graph theory | | **Traveling Salesman (Decision)** | Reduction from Hamiltonian Cycle | Logistics, routing | | **Vertex Cover** | Reduction from 3-SAT | Network security, biology | | **Graph Coloring (k ≥ 3)** | Reduction from 3-SAT | Scheduling, register allocation | | **Subset Sum** | Reduction from 3-SAT | Cryptography, finance | | **Knapsack (Decision)** | Reduction from Subset Sum | Resource allocation | | **Independent Set** | Reduction from Clique | Social networks, bioinformatics | | **Clique** | Reduction from 3-SAT | Social network analysis | | **Partition** | Reduction from Subset Sum | Load balancing | ### 3.4 Growth Rate Comparison: Polynomial vs. Exponential **Worked Numerical Comparison for Input Size $n$:** $$\begin{aligned} n = 10: \quad & n^2 = 100, \quad 2^n = 1024 \\ n = 50: \quad & n^2 = 2500, \quad 2^n \approx 1.12 \times 10^{15} \\ n = 100: \quad & n^2 = 10000, \quad 2^n \approx 1.27 \times 10^{30} \\ n = 1000: \quad & n^2 = 10^6, \quad 2^n \approx 1.07 \times 10^{301} \text{ (more than atoms in universe)} \end{aligned}$$ This dramatic difference in growth rates is precisely why **polynomial-time solvability** is the universally accepted boundary between "tractable" and "intractable." ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Complexity Class Hierarchy Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef undecidable fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#c0392b,color:#fff classDef nphard fill:#ffa500,stroke:#cc8400,color:#fff classDef npcomp fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef np fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000 classDef pclass fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,color:#000 subgraph LEVEL4[" "] direction TB UNDEC["UNSOLVABLE / UNDECIDABLE PROBLEMS
Halting Problem, Post Correspondence"]:::undecidable end subgraph LEVEL3[" "] direction TB NPHARD["NP-HARD PROBLEMS
TSP Optimization, Halting Problem"]:::nphard end subgraph LEVEL2[" "] direction TB NPCOMP["NP-COMPLETE PROBLEMS
SAT, 3-SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle,
Vertex Cover, Graph Coloring,
Subset Sum, Knapsack, Clique"]:::npcomp end subgraph LEVEL1[" "] direction TB NPCLASS["NP CLASS
Verifiable in Polynomial Time"]:::np end subgraph LEVEL0[" "] direction TB PCLASS["P CLASS
Sorting, MST, Shortest Path,
BFS, DFS, Binary Search"]:::pclass end PCLASS --> NPCLASS NPCOMP --> NPHARD NPCLASS -.->|"P = NP? (Unsolved)"| PCLASS NPCOMP --> NPCLASS UNDEC --> NPHARD ``` ### 4.2 Polynomial-Time Reduction Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR classDef source fill:#ffd700,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000 classDef target fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#4682b4,color:#000 classDef solver fill:#90ee90,stroke:#228b22,color:#000 SAT["SAT Problem
(Known NP-Complete)"]:::source TRANSFORM["Polynomial-Time
Reduction f
Runs in O(n^k)"]:::target PROBLEM_X["Target Problem X
(To Prove NP-Complete)"]:::target VERIFY["Verification
Algorithm
Polynomial Time"]:::solver RESULT["X is NP-Complete"]:::solver SAT -->|Input I| TRANSFORM TRANSFORM -->|f I in O n^k| PROBLEM_X PROBLEM_X --> VERIFY VERIFY --> RESULT ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision vs Optimization Problem ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef input fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 classDef opt fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 PROB["Given Problem
G, parameters"]:::input Q1["Is there a solution
with value less than k?"]:::decision Q2["Find the solution
with minimum value"]:::opt OUT1["YES / NO Answer"]:::decision OUT2["Best Solution Found"]:::opt PROB --> Q1 Q1 --> OUT1 PROB --> Q2 Q2 --> OUT2 Q2 -.->|Can be solved by
repeated decision queries| Q1 ``` ### 4.4 Tractable vs Intractable Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef start fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 classDef tractable fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 classDef intractable fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000 classDef np fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 START["Given a Decision Problem"]:::start Q1{"Polynomial-time
algorithm exists?"}:::start TRACT["TRACTABLE - P Class
O n^k time
Examples: Sorting, MST, Shortest Path"]:::tractable Q2{"Polynomial-time
verification exists?"}:::start NP["NP Class
YES instances verifiable in poly time"]:::np Q3{"All NP problems reduce
to this problem?"}:::start INT["INTractable - NP-Complete / NP-Hard
Believed to require exponential time
Examples: SAT, TSP, Vertex Cover"]:::intractable START --> Q1 Q1 -->|Yes| TRACT Q1 -->|No| Q2 Q2 -->|Yes| NP Q2 -->|No| INT Q3 -->|Yes| INT ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define tractable and intractable problems with one example each.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** A problem is **tractable** if it can be solved in polynomial time $O(n^k)$ for some constant $k$. **Example:** Sorting an array using merge sort in $O(n \log n)$ time. A problem is **intractable** if it cannot be solved in polynomial time; the best-known algorithm requires exponential or worse time. **Example:** The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) requiring $O(n!)$ time using brute force. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is the difference between P, NP, and NP-Complete classes?** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** | Class | Definition | Example | |---|---|---| | **P** | Decision problems solvable in polynomial time by a deterministic algorithm. | Reachability in a graph. | | **NP** | Decision problems whose YES answer can be verified in polynomial time. | Hamiltonian Cycle, SAT. | | **NP-Complete** | Decision problems in NP to which every NP problem reduces in polynomial time. | 3-SAT, Vertex Cover, TSP (decision). | $P \subseteq NP$, and NP-Complete is the "hardest" subset of NP. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question is 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Expected Pattern]** **(a)** Define the classes P, NP, NP-Complete, and NP-Hard. Explain the relationships between them with a suitable Venn-diagram-like hierarchy. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the concept of polynomial-time reduction. Using a suitable example, demonstrate how a known NP-Complete problem can be reduced to a new problem to prove its NP-Completeness. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **Part (a) — Solution:** **P Class:** The set of all decision problems that can be *solved* by a deterministic algorithm in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. **NP Class:** The set of all decision problems for which a given *certificate* (proposed YES solution) can be *verified* in polynomial time by a deterministic algorithm. **NP-Complete Class:** A problem $L$ is NP-Complete if: 1. $L \in NP$ (verifiable in poly time). 2. Every problem in NP can be reduced to $L$ in polynomial time (i.e., $L$ is NP-Hard). **NP-Hard Class:** A problem is NP-Hard if it is at least as hard as any NP problem (every NP problem polynomial-time reduces to it), but it may not itself be in NP. **Hierarchy (Inclusion Relationships):** $$P \subseteq NP \quad \text{and} \quad \text{NP-Complete} \subseteq \text{NP} \cap \text{NP-Hard}$$ The set NP-Hard is the largest, containing NP-Complete and many optimization problems. P is the smallest. The famous **P vs. NP** question asks whether these two classes are equal — it remains unsolved. *Mark allocation:* P definition (1), NP definition (1), NP-Complete (2), NP-Hard (1), Hierarchy diagram (2). --- **Part (b) — Solution:** A **polynomial-time reduction** from problem $A$ to problem $B$, denoted $A \leq_p B$, is a transformation $f$ such that: - $f$ runs in polynomial time $O(n^k)$. - For every instance $I$ of $A$: $I$ is a YES instance $\iff$ $f(I)$ is a YES instance of $B$. **Significance:** If $A \leq_p B$ and $B \in P$, then $A \in P$. Conversely, if $A$ is NP-Complete and $A \leq_p B$ with $B \in NP$, then $B$ is NP-Complete. **Example — Reducing 3-SAT to Independent Set:** - **3-SAT Instance:** A Boolean formula with $m$ clauses, each containing 3 literals. - **Independent Set Instance:** A graph $G$ and an integer $k$ — does $G$ have an independent set of size $k$? **Reduction Steps:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{For each clause } C_j = (\ell_1 \lor \ell_2 \lor \ell_3): \quad & \text{Create a triangle of 3 vertices,} \\ & \text{one per literal} \\ \text{For any two complementary literals:} \quad & \text{(i.e., } x_i \text{ and } \overline{x_i} \text{)} \\ & \text{Add an edge between them} \\ \text{Set:} \quad & k = m \text{ (number of clauses)} \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Correctness:** $$\begin{aligned} \text{3-SAT is satisfiable} \iff & \text{Each clause has at least one TRUE literal} \\ \iff & \text{We can pick one TRUE literal per clause} \\ & \text{(these are independent because no two are complementary)} \\ \iff & \text{Graph has an independent set of size } m = k \\ \end{aligned}$$ The reduction runs in $O(n \cdot m)$ time — polynomial. Since 3-SAT $\leq_p$ Independent Set and Independent Set $\in$ NP, **Independent Set is NP-Complete**. *Mark allocation:* Definition of reduction (2), Significance (1), 3-SAT to IS example (3), Correctness (1). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Expected Pattern]** **(a)** State and explain Cook's Theorem. Why is it considered a landmark result in computational complexity? *(7 marks)* **(b)** List any five classical NP-Complete problems. For each, state the problem formally and identify the application domain. *(7 marks)* #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **Part (a) — Solution:** **Cook's Theorem (1971):** The **Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT)** is NP-Complete. **Explanation:** SAT asks: given a Boolean formula $\phi$ in conjunctive normal form (CNF), is there a truth assignment to the variables that makes $\phi$ evaluate to TRUE? $$\phi = (x_1 \lor \neg x_2 \lor x_3) \land (\neg x_1 \lor x_2 \lor \neg x_3) \land \ldots$$ **Why It Is a Landmark Result:** 1. **First Proof of NP-Completeness:** Cook (1971) provided the first rigorous proof that an NP problem is NP-Complete, independently verified by Leonid Levin (1973). 2. **Foundation for Future Reductions:** After Cook's theorem, Karp (1972) showed 21 classical problems (Hamiltonian Cycle, Clique, Vertex Cover, etc.) are NP-Complete by reducing SAT to each of them. 3. **The P vs. NP Bridge:** Cook's theorem formalizes the central question of theoretical computer science: if SAT $\in$ P, then P = NP, which would collapse the entire complexity hierarchy. 4. **Practical Impact:** SAT solvers are now used in software verification, AI planning, cryptography, and hardware design. **Proof Sketch:** Cook's proof constructs a polynomial-size Boolean formula that simulates the computation of a nondeterministic Turing machine. The formula is satisfiable iff the Turing machine accepts the input. *Mark allocation:* Statement of theorem (2), Explanation of SAT (2), Why landmark (3). --- **Part (b) — Solution:** **Five Classical NP-Complete Problems:** **1. 3-SAT (3-Satisfiability)** - **Formal Statement:** Given a CNF Boolean formula with exactly 3 literals per clause, is it satisfiable? - **Application:** Hardware verification, AI planning. **2. Hamiltonian Cycle** - **Formal Statement:** Given a graph $G$ with $n$ vertices, does $G$ contain a cycle that visits every vertex exactly once? - **Application:** Network routing, genome assembly. **3. Vertex Cover (Decision)** - **Formal Statement:** Given a graph $G = (V, E)$ and integer $k$, is there a subset $S \subseteq V$ with $\vert S \vert \leq k$ such that every edge has at least one endpoint in $S$? - **Application:** Network security, bioinformatics, social networks. **4. Subset Sum** - **Formal Statement:** Given a set of integers $S = \{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_n\}$ and a target $T$, is there a subset of $S$ that sums exactly to $T$? - **Application:** Cryptanalysis, resource allocation, knapsack-type problems. **5. Graph Coloring (3-Coloring)** - **Formal Statement:** Given a graph $G$ and integer $k \geq 3$, can the vertices of $G$ be colored using $\leq k$ colors such that no two adjacent vertices share a color? - **Application:** Register allocation in compilers, exam scheduling, frequency assignment. *Mark allocation:* Each problem (1.4 marks ≈ formal statement 0.7, application 0.7). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes to Avoid:** > 1. **Confusing P with NP:** Students often write "P is a subset of NP" without mentioning that the P vs. NP question is **unsolved**. Always state: "It is conjectured but not proven that P = NP." > 2. **Forgetting to mention verification:** When defining NP, the keyword is *verification* (not solution). NP = "verifiable in polynomial time." > 3. **Reduction direction confusion:** Writing "NP-Complete reduces to P" instead of "P reduces to NP-Complete." The correct direction: known NP-Complete problem reduces to the new problem. > 4. **Missing the polynomial time check:** For NP-Completeness proof, you must show BOTH (i) the problem is in NP, AND (ii) reduction runs in polynomial time. > 5. **Optimization vs Decision mix-up:** TSP (decision) is NP-Complete, but TSP (optimization) is NP-Hard, not NP-Complete. > 6. **Listing NP-Hard as NP-Complete:** Halting Problem is NP-Hard but NOT in NP, hence not NP-Complete. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Tractable Problem** = Solvable in polynomial time $O(n^k)$ — belongs to class **P**. - ✅ **Intractable Problem** = No known polynomial-time algorithm — best known is exponential $O(c^n)$ or worse. - ✅ **P ⊆ NP ⊆ NP-Complete ⊆ NP-Hard** (in set-inclusion sense). - ✅ **P = Solvable in poly time**, **NP = Verifiable in poly time**. - ✅ **P vs. NP** is the most famous open problem in computer science (Clay Millennium Prize). - ✅ **Cook's Theorem (1971):** SAT is the original NP-Complete problem. - ✅ To prove **NP-Completeness**: (1) Show $L \in NP$, (2) Show a known NP-Complete problem reduces to $L$ in poly time. - ✅ **Polynomial-time reduction** $A \leq_p B$ means we can transform $A$'s instances to $B$'s in $O(n^k)$. - ✅ **Decision problems** (Yes/No) form the basis of complexity theory; optimization problems reduce to decision form. - ✅ Famous NP-Complete problems: **SAT, 3-SAT, Hamiltonian Cycle, Vertex Cover, Clique, Independent Set, Graph Coloring, Subset Sum, Knapsack, TSP (decision), Partition**. - ✅ Famous NP-Hard (not in NP) problems: **TSP (optimization), Halting Problem, Post Correspondence Problem**. - ✅ **Undecidable problems** have no algorithm at all — they are non-computable. - ✅ Cryptography (RSA), compilers (register allocation), and AI planning all rely on the P vs. NP distinction in practice. - ✅ **Karp's 21 problems** (1972) are the classical NP-Complete set, still relevant today. > **Key Takeaway for KTU 2024 Examinations:** The tractable/intractable distinction hinges on **polynomial-time solvability**. NP-Complete problems are the "borderline" — verifiable in poly time but with no known poly-time solver. The P vs. NP question is unsolved, but the practical consensus is P ≠ NP.

topics.title: Complexity Classes - P
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT502 - ALGORITHMS ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Complexity Classes - P

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Complexity Class P

The complexity class **P** (also called **PTIME** or **DTIME**) is the set of all **decision problems** that can be solved by a **deterministic Turing machine** using a polynomial amount of computation time.

Formally, the class **P** is defined as:

$$\mathbf{P} = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} \text{DTIME}\left(n^{k}\right)$$

Where:
- $n$ is the size of the input (typically measured in bits).
- $k$ is a non-negative integer constant.
- $\text{DTIME}(f(n))$ is the set of all decision problems solvable by a deterministic Turing machine in $O(f(n))$ time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Class **P** represents problems whose solutions can be found in **polynomial time**. These are considered "tractable" or "efficiently solvable" problems from a theoretical computer science standpoint.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a librarian in a vast library with **millions of books**:

- **P problems** are like finding a book when the books are **organized alphabetically by author name** (sorted). No matter how many books there are, you can find your book in time proportional to the logarithm of the number of books — **fast and efficient**, even for huge libraries.
- **Non-P problems** (like NP-hard problems) are like finding the *shortest* route visiting 50 cities when the cities are scattered randomly. Even though you *can* find an answer, the time it takes **explodes exponentially** as the number of cities grows.

In simple terms: **Class P = "Problems we can solve quickly enough to be useful, even on large inputs."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision vs. Optimization Problems:** Class P technically only contains **decision problems** (problems with yes/no answers). Optimization problems can be converted to decision problems by asking, "Is there a solution with cost ≤ k?"

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Deterministic Algorithm** | At every step, exactly one possible next move is allowed. No choices, no guessing. |
| **Polynomial Time** | Running time bounded by $O(n^{k})$ for some constant $k \geq 0$. |
| **Tractable Problem** | A problem that can be solved in polynomial time. |
| **Intractable Problem** | A problem that **cannot** be solved in polynomial time. |
| **Decision Problem** | A problem whose answer is either YES or NO. |
| **Turing Machine** | An abstract computational model that defines what is computable. |
| **Verification** | The act of checking whether a given certificate/proof is a valid solution. |

## 1.4 Why Polynomial Time?

The choice of polynomial time as the boundary between "easy" and "hard" is not arbitrary. It rests on three foundational reasons:

1. **Closure under composition:** If a problem can be solved in polynomial time, calling it a polynomial number of times (with polynomial-sized outputs) still yields a polynomial-time algorithm.
2. **Robustness across computational models:** Polynomial-time tractability is preserved under reasonable changes in the underlying machine model (single-tape vs. multi-tape Turing machines, RAM models with uniform cost, etc.).
3. **Empirical practicality:** Algorithms running in $O(n^{k})$ for small $k$ (e.g., $k \leq 6$) are generally usable in practice, while exponential algorithms like $O(2^{n})$ quickly become infeasible.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strict Inequality Note:** The class P is defined for *deterministic* machines. The corresponding class for *non-deterministic* machines is called **NP**. Every problem in P is also in NP, because a deterministic machine is a special (restricted) case of a non-deterministic one.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Deterministic Turing Machine Foundation

A **Deterministic Turing Machine (DTM)** is the formal model of computation underlying class P. It consists of:

- A **tape** divided into cells, each holding a symbol from a finite alphabet.
- A **read/write head** that scans one cell at a time and can move left or right.
- A **finite state control** that determines the next action based on the current state and the symbol under the head.
- A **transition function** $\delta: Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ which is *single-valued* (deterministic).

> [!NOTE]
> The **transition function** of a DTM is a single-valued function, meaning for every (state, symbol) pair there is **exactly one** next action. This is the key distinction from a Non-deterministic Turing Machine (NTM).

## 2.2 Formal Membership Criteria for Class P

For a decision problem $\Pi$ to belong to class **P**, **all three** of the following conditions must hold:

1. **Decision problem:** The problem must have YES/NO output.
2. **Existence of algorithm:** There must exist a deterministic algorithm that always halts.
3. **Polynomial bound:** The running time of the algorithm must be $O(n^{k})$ for some fixed integer $k \geq 0$ and input size $n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If **any** of these conditions fails, the problem falls outside class P. In particular, the **constant $k$ must be independent of the input** — it is part of the algorithm, not the input.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Example |
|-------------------|---------|---------|
| $\mathbf{P}$ | Class of polynomial-time solvable decision problems | $P = \bigcup_{k} \text{DTIME}(n^{k})$ |
| $\text{DTIME}(f(n))$ | Problems solvable by DTM in $O(f(n))$ time | $\text{DTIME}(n^{2})$ |
| $n$ | Input size (in bits) | For sorting $n$ numbers, $n$ is the array size |
| $O(n^{k})$ | Polynomial time complexity | $k = 1, 2, 3, \dots$ |
| $O(2^{n})$ | Exponential time complexity | Considered intractable |
| $O(2^{n/2})$ | Sub-exponential (used in factoring) | Practically hard for large $n$ |
| $\Pi$ | A decision problem | "Is $x$ in language $L$?" |
| $\mathcal{M}$ | A Turing machine | DTM or NTM |
| $L(\mathcal{M})$ | Language accepted by $\mathcal{M}$ | Set of YES-instances |
| $\Sigma^{n}$ | Set of all binary strings of length $n$ | The input universe |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Syntax Note:** To represent absolute value or set cardinality in your KTU answer scripts, always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ notation (using `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX) to avoid breaking markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Canonical Examples of Problems in Class P

The following are **guaranteed members of P** and are standard textbook examples that frequently appear in KTU examinations:

| Problem | Decision Version | Best Known Algorithm | Time Complexity |
|---------|------------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Sorting** | Is the array sorted? | Merge Sort / Heap Sort | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Reachability** | Is there a path from $u$ to $v$ in graph $G$? | BFS / DFS | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ |
| **2-SAT** | Is the 2-CNF formula satisfiable? | Implication graph + SCC | $O(n + m)$ |
| **Shortest Path** | Is there a path of length $\leq k$? | Dijkstra's algorithm | $O(\vert E \vert + \vert V \vert \log \vert V \vert)$ |
| **Primality Testing (deterministic)** | Is $n$ prime? | AKS Primality Test | $O((\log n)^{12})$ |
| **Linear Programming** | Is there a feasible solution with cost $\leq k$? | Khachiyan / Interior Point | Polynomial |
